A **group** is a named bucket of users. You put alice and bob into a group called `photos`, give the photo files to that group, and now both members can use them. Add a third person to the group later and they get access automatically — no need to touch each file.

Just like users have a UID, every group has a number called a **GID** (group ID). And just like usernames, the group name is the friendly label while the GID is what the system tracks underneath.

A user can belong to several groups at once. There are two kinds of membership, but the idea is simple: you have one **primary group** (your main group, used by default for new files you create) and you can be an extra member of many other **supplementary groups** (the additional buckets you've been added to). To see your groups:

```bash
groups        # lists the groups you belong to
id            # also shows your groups, with their GID numbers
```

Groups are also how Linux hands out special privileges. For example, members of a group often named `sudo` (or `wheel`) are allowed to borrow root powers. Being added to that group is how a normal user gets permission to run admin tasks.

One important gotcha: when someone adds you to a new group, your **current** login session does not see the change yet. Your group membership is locked in at the moment you log in. You must **log out and back in** (start a fresh session) for the new group to take effect. This surprises everyone the first time.

**Recap:** A group is a named bucket of users (with a numeric GID) used to share access; you have one primary group plus any number of supplementary ones; some groups grant privileges like sudo; and a new group membership only applies after you start a new login session.

items.prompt: What is a group in Linux?
choices: A named collection of users that can share access
A backup copy of all your files
A folder that cannot be deleted
items.explanation: A group is a named bucket of users, used so several people can share access to the same files or privileges. It is not a backup, a password, or a special folder.

items.prompt: alice and bob both need to edit the same photo files. What is the cleanest way to arrange that?
choices: Give them both the root password
Put them in a shared group and give the files to that group
Email the files back and forth
items.explanation: A shared group is exactly the tidy tool for this: both members get access, and adding a third person later is automatic. Sharing the root password or setting UID 0 hands out unlimited power, which is dangerous and overkill.

items.prompt: What is a GID?
choices: The numeric ID of a group
items.explanation: A GID (group ID) is the number that identifies a group, just as a UID identifies a user. It is not a filename, a graphics setting, or a password.

items.prompt: Can one user belong to more than one group at the same time?
choices: No, a user can only ever be in one group
Yes — one primary group plus any number of supplementary groups
Only root can be in more than one group
Only if the user has no password
items.explanation: A user has one primary group (used by default for new files) and can be a supplementary member of many other groups. This is normal for every user, not just root, and has nothing to do with passwords.

items.prompt: Which command lists the groups you currently belong to?
items.explanation: The 'groups' command lists your group memberships (and 'id' shows them with GID numbers). 'delete', 'format', and 'shutdown' do not report your groups.

items.prompt: An administrator just added carol to the 'sudo' group, but in her already-open terminal she still cannot run admin tasks. Why?
choices: The change needs a reboot of the whole building's power
Group changes only apply after she starts a new login session
Groups never affect what a user can do
items.explanation: Group membership is locked in when a session starts. Carol must log out and back in (a fresh session) for the new group to take effect. A reboot is not required — just a new login — and groups absolutely do affect what a user can do.

items.prompt: What is the difference between a primary group and a supplementary group?
choices: There is no difference; the words mean the same thing
The primary group is your main/default group; supplementary groups are extra ones you also belong to
Supplementary groups are only for root
The primary group is your password and the supplementary group is your username
items.explanation: You have exactly one primary (default) group plus any number of supplementary (extra) groups. They are different roles, both available to ordinary users, and neither is a password or username.

items.prompt: Why are groups often used to hand out privileges like the ability to use sudo?
choices: Because adding a user to the right group is a simple, central way to grant the same permission to many people
Because groups make the computer run faster
Because every user must be in the sudo group
items.explanation: Putting a user in a group like 'sudo' is a clean, central way to grant a permission, and it scales to many people. Groups do not speed up the machine, are not mandatory for every user, and do not delete files.

lessons.title: Creating Accounts and the Files Behind Them
lessons.content:
Now that you know what users and groups are, how do you make and manage them? And where does Linux actually keep this information?

Linux stores accounts in a couple of plain text files. You normally don't edit them by hand — commands do it for you — but it helps to know they exist.

**`/etc/passwd`** lists the accounts. Despite the name, it holds **no passwords**. Each line describes one user, with fields separated by colons:

```
alice:x:1000:1000:Alice Smith:/home/alice:/bin/bash
```

That reads as: user name `alice`; an `x` standing in for the password; UID `1000`; primary group GID `1000`; a comment (full name); the home folder; and the **login shell** (the program you get when you log in). This file is readable by everyone, which is why it must not contain the real password.

**`/etc/shadow`** is where the real secret lives — but not the password itself. It stores a **password hash**: a scrambled, one-way version of your password. You can turn a password into its hash, but you cannot turn the hash back into the password. When you log in, Linux hashes what you typed and compares it to the stored hash. `/etc/shadow` is readable by **root only**, so other users cannot copy your hash and try to crack it. (Common misconception: the password is *not* kept in `/etc/passwd`.)

**`/etc/group`** lists the groups and who belongs to them.

Useful commands (admin actions need `sudo`, which you'll meet next):

```bash
sudo useradd -m alice     # create user alice, -m makes her home folder
sudo passwd alice         # set or change alice's password
sudo usermod -aG photos alice   # add alice to the 'photos' group (-aG = add to group)
groups alice              # show which groups alice is in
id alice                  # show alice's UID, primary GID, and groups
who                       # show who is logged in right now
```

A tip you saw before: a group added with `usermod` only takes effect after a fresh login.

**Recap:** `/etc/passwd` lists accounts (no passwords) in colon fields; `/etc/shadow` holds the one-way password hash and is root-only; `/etc/group` lists groups; and `useradd`, `passwd`, `usermod`, `id`, `groups`, and `who` create and inspect accounts.

items.prompt: Despite its name, what does the file /etc/passwd actually contain?
choices: Everyone's actual passwords in plain text
A list of accounts and their details — but no passwords
items.explanation: /etc/passwd lists accounts (name, UID, primary group, home, login shell) but holds no passwords — just an 'x' placeholder. It is readable by everyone, which is exactly why real passwords are kept out of it.

items.prompt: Where are password hashes stored, and why not in /etc/passwd?
choices: In /etc/shadow, which only root can read, so others cannot copy the hashes
In the user's home folder, in a file called password.txt
Nowhere — Linux does not store anything about passwords
items.explanation: Hashes live in /etc/shadow, which is readable by root only — so ordinary users cannot grab everyone's hashes and try to crack them. /etc/passwd is world-readable, so putting secrets there would be unsafe. Linux definitely does store a hash; it just keeps it protected.

items.prompt: What is a password 'hash'?
choices: A scrambled, one-way version of the password that cannot be turned back
The number of characters in the password
items.explanation: A hash is a one-way scramble: you can turn a password into its hash but not the hash back into the password. At login, Linux hashes what you typed and compares. It is not the password reversed, a nickname, or a length count.

items.prompt: In the line 'alice:x:1000:1000:Alice Smith:/home/alice:/bin/bash', what does the last field '/bin/bash' represent?
choices: Alice's login shell — the program she gets when she logs in
The amount of disk space alice may use
items.explanation: The final field is the login shell, the program started when the user logs in (here, the bash shell). It is not a password, email, or disk quota.

items.prompt: Which command creates a new user named alice along with her home folder?
items.explanation: useradd creates the account and the -m flag makes her home folder. 'rm' deletes, 'passwd -d' removes a password, and 'reboot' restarts the machine — none create a user.

items.prompt: Which command sets or changes alice's password?
items.explanation: passwd sets or changes a password (writing the hash into /etc/shadow). useradd creates an account, 'groups' lists group membership, and 'who' shows who is logged in.

items.prompt: What does 'sudo usermod -aG photos alice' do?
items.explanation: usermod -aG adds the user to a group (the -aG means 'add to this group'). It does not delete the group, change a password, or rename the user. Remember alice must start a new login session for it to take effect.

items.prompt: Which file lists the groups on the system and who belongs to them?
items.explanation: /etc/group lists groups and their members. /etc/shadow holds password hashes, /etc/hostname holds the computer's name, and /etc/photos is not a standard file.

items.prompt: Why do you normally use commands like useradd instead of opening /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow in a text editor?
choices: The files are encrypted and cannot be opened
The commands fill in the fields correctly and keep the files consistent, avoiding mistakes
The files do not really exist
items.explanation: The commands fill in fields in the right format and keep related files in sync, which avoids easy hand-editing mistakes that could lock you out. The files are plain text (not encrypted), they do exist, and editing them is allowed — just risky.

lessons.title: Borrowing Root Power: sudo vs su
lessons.content:
You know root can do anything, and that you should stay a normal user. So how do you do the occasional task that *needs* root power — like installing software? You **borrow** root's power briefly. There are two classic tools: `sudo` and `su`.

**`sudo`** means "do this one command as the superuser." You type `sudo` in front of a command, enter **your own** password, and just that command runs with root power:

```bash
sudo apt update        # update the software list, as root, just this once
```

When the command finishes, you are an ordinary user again. Two nice things: you use **your** password (no need to know root's), and the system can **record** who ran what, so there's a trail.

You earn the right to use `sudo` by being in a group such as `sudo` or `wheel`, or by a rule in a settings file called `/etc/sudoers` (you'll meet it later). The key idea now: `sudo` borrows power for **one command at a time**.

**`su`** means "switch user." By itself, `su` tries to *become* root, asking for **root's** password, and then leaves you sitting in a root shell where everything you type runs as root until you exit. `su alice` switches to alice instead.

Why prefer `sudo`?
- `sudo` needs *your* password; `su` (to root) needs the *root* password, which ideally no human knows or shares.
- `sudo` runs **one** command then drops back; `su` leaves you in an all-powerful shell where it's easy to forget you're root and make a costly mistake.
- `sudo` actions are logged per command.

This is also why logging in directly as root is discouraged: it's like staying in "administrator mode" all day. Borrowing power for a single command is far safer.

**Recap:** `sudo` runs one command as root using your own password and is logged; `su` switches into another user's shell (root by default) using *that* user's password and keeps that power until you exit; prefer `sudo` because it's per-command, safer, and uses your own credentials.

items.prompt: What does 'sudo apt update' do?
choices: Logs you in permanently as root
Runs just that one command (apt update) with root power, then returns you to normal
items.explanation: sudo runs a single command with root power and then you are an ordinary user again. It does not log you in as root permanently, delete root, or change your password.

items.prompt: When you run a command with sudo, whose password do you normally type?
items.explanation: sudo asks for YOUR own password and checks that you are allowed to use sudo. That means no one needs to know or share root's password. (su, by contrast, asks for the target account's password.)

items.prompt: Why is using sudo generally preferred over logging in as root and working there all day?
choices: sudo borrows root power for just one command and records who ran it, limiting risk
Logging in as root is impossible
sudo gives you more disk space
items.explanation: sudo grants root power per command and keeps a log, so a mistake affects only that one command and there's a trail of who did what. Staying logged in as root is like leaving administrator mode on all day — one slip can be disastrous. Speed and disk space are not the reason.

items.prompt: What does the 'su' command (with no name after it) try to do?
choices: Switch you into a root shell, asking for root's password
items.explanation: 'su' means 'switch user', and by itself it switches to root, prompting for ROOT's password, leaving you in a root shell. It does not shut down, show uptime, or update software.

items.prompt: A key difference: after 'su' to root succeeds, what is your situation compared with running a single 'sudo' command?
choices: You are dropped back to a normal user immediately, same as sudo
You stay in a root shell, where everything you type runs as root until you exit
Nothing changes; su has no effect
items.explanation: su leaves you in an ongoing root shell — every command runs as root until you type 'exit'. sudo, by contrast, runs just one command then returns you to normal. su does have an effect and does not delete files.

items.prompt: How does a normal user typically earn the right to use sudo?
choices: By being added to a group such as 'sudo' or 'wheel', or by a rule in /etc/sudoers
items.explanation: Permission to use sudo comes from membership in a group like 'sudo'/'wheel' or a rule in /etc/sudoers. It does not require the root password, is not granted to everyone by default, and has nothing to do with folder names.

items.prompt: One safety advantage of sudo is that the system can keep a record of sudo commands. Why does that matter?
choices: It makes the disk fill up faster
It provides a trail of who ran which admin command, useful for accountability
items.explanation: Logging sudo commands creates an audit trail showing who did what — helpful for security and troubleshooting. It is about accountability, not disk usage or speed, and the point is to reveal admin actions, not hide them.

items.prompt: Which statement about the root password is the safest practice on a well-run system?
choices: Everyone should memorize the root password
No human needs to know root's password; people use sudo with their own passwords instead
The root password should be the same as every user's password
Print the root password and tape it to the monitor
items.explanation: Best practice is that no one needs the root password day to day — authorized users borrow root power via sudo with their own passwords. Sharing, reusing, or posting the root password are all serious security risks.

lessons.title: Behind the Scenes: Service Accounts, sudoers, Keys and Login
lessons.content:
This lesson zooms out to a few ideas you'll bump into later. The goal is recognition, not mastery — a one-line mental model for each.

**Service accounts and nologin shells.** Remember that many users are background programs, not people. A program like a web server gets its own **service account** so it runs with only the access it needs (this is called *least privilege* — give each account the minimum power required). Such accounts usually get a special login shell called **nologin**: instead of giving a usable shell, it just prints a short message and exits. So even if someone got hold of that account, they couldn't open a working session with it.

**`/etc/sudoers` and `visudo`.** Earlier you used `sudo`. The rules for *who* may run *which* commands as root live in a file called `/etc/sudoers`. A rule can be broad ("alice may run anything") or precise ("the deploy account may run *only* the restart-web command"). It can also say **NOPASSWD** — meaning that specific command runs via sudo without asking for a password, handy for automated scripts. Because a typo here could lock everyone out of sudo, you edit it with a special tool, `visudo`, which checks the file for mistakes before saving.

**SSH key-based login.** When connecting to another computer over the network, you often skip typing a password entirely. Instead you use a **key pair**: a private key you keep secret and a matching public key placed on the server. The key proves who you are — more convenient and stronger than a password. (You'll cover SSH in depth in the networking topic.)

**PAM and nsswitch (one line each).** **PAM** (Pluggable Authentication Modules) is the framework programs use to check logins, so each program doesn't reinvent password checking. **nsswitch** (the Name Service Switch, configured in `/etc/nsswitch.conf`) tells the system *where* to look up accounts — local files, or a central company directory. In big organizations, accounts live in such a directory so one login works on every machine, but the overall login journey is the same.

The diagram shows that journey: a login program collects your username and secret, the system verifies it, looks up your account, sets your UID/GID and groups, and finally starts your shell.

**Recap:** Service accounts run programs with least privilege and often use a nologin shell; `/etc/sudoers` (edited safely with `visudo`) defines who may run which commands as root, optionally NOPASSWD; SSH key login uses a key pair instead of a password; and PAM checks logins while nsswitch decides where accounts are looked up.

items.prompt: What is a 'service account'?
choices: An account a background program runs under, with only the access it needs
The account used by the customer-service department
Another name for the root account
items.explanation: A service account belongs to a background program (like a web server) and is given only the access it needs — least privilege. It is not a subscription, a department's login, or root.

items.prompt: What does giving a service account a 'nologin' shell accomplish?
choices: It makes the account log in faster
It means the account cannot open a usable interactive session — the shell just prints a message and exits
It gives the account root power
items.explanation: A nologin shell prints a short message and exits instead of opening a working shell, so even a stolen service credential can't be used for an interactive session. It does not speed up login, grant root, or delete the account.

items.prompt: The idea of giving each account only the power it actually needs is called:
items.explanation: Least privilege means granting only the minimum access required, which limits damage if an account is misused. The other options describe handing out too much power, the opposite of the goal.

items.prompt: What does the file /etc/sudoers control?
choices: Who may run which commands as root via sudo, and whether a password is required
How much memory each program uses
items.explanation: /etc/sudoers defines the sudo rules: which users may run which commands as root, and options like NOPASSWD. It has nothing to do with wallpaper, web filtering, or memory limits.

items.prompt: Why edit /etc/sudoers with the special tool 'visudo' rather than any text editor?
choices: visudo checks the file for mistakes before saving, so a typo can't lock everyone out of sudo
Only visudo can open the file at all
items.explanation: visudo validates the syntax before saving; a broken sudoers file could otherwise lock everyone (including you) out of sudo. It is about safety, not looks, and it does not grant everyone root.

items.prompt: A sudoers rule says the 'deploy' account may run ONLY the command that restarts the web service, with NOPASSWD. What can 'deploy' do?
choices: Run any command as root without a password
Restart the web service as root without being asked for a password — and nothing else via sudo
Edit any file on the system
items.explanation: The rule grants exactly one command, as root, with no password prompt (handy for automation). It does not grant other commands, root login, or general file editing. Scoping NOPASSWD to one specific command keeps it relatively safe.

items.prompt: In SSH key-based login, what proves your identity instead of a typed password?
choices: A key pair — a secret private key you hold plus a matching public key on the server
items.explanation: SSH key login uses a key pair: you keep the private key secret, the server holds the matching public key, and the key proves who you are — more convenient and stronger than a password. It does not rely on a phone or serial number, and the whole point is to avoid typing a password.

items.prompt: In one line, what is PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules)?
choices: A program that draws the login screen
The framework programs use to check logins, so each program doesn't reinvent authentication
items.explanation: PAM is the shared authentication framework that applications call to verify logins, so they don't each implement password checking. It is not the login screen's graphics, RAM, or a backup tool.

items.prompt: In one line, what does nsswitch (the Name Service Switch, /etc/nsswitch.conf) decide?
choices: WHERE the system looks up accounts and groups — e.g. local files or a central company directory
The speed of the login screen
items.explanation: nsswitch tells the system where to find account and group information — local files and/or a central directory. It does not pick cables, set screen speed, or define password hashing.

items.prompt: Put the login journey in the right order.
choices: Start the shell first, then ask for the username last
Collect username and secret, verify the secret, look up the account, set UID/GID and groups, then start the login shell
Delete the account, then log in
Set the wallpaper, then verify the password
items.explanation: The login program collects your username and secret, the system verifies it, looks up your account, sets your UID/GID and group memberships, and finally starts your login shell. The other orderings are nonsensical — you can't run the shell before knowing who you are.

subtopics.title: The Boot Chain: Firmware to Init

lessons.title: Firmware: BIOS/MBR vs UEFI/GPT
lessons.content:
When you press the power button, no kernel exists in RAM yet — firmware baked into the motherboard runs first. Its job is to initialize hardware (POST: power-on self-test), then find and run the first stage of software that will eventually load Linux.

**Legacy BIOS** is the old way. After POST, BIOS reads the very first 512-byte sector of the boot disk — the **Master Boot Record (MBR)**. The MBR holds a tiny boot-strap code stub plus a 4-entry partition table. Because 512 bytes is almost nothing, that stub just chain-loads a real bootloader. MBR also caps disks at ~2 TiB and 4 primary partitions.

**UEFI** is the modern replacement (treat it as the default today). Instead of executing raw sectors, UEFI understands a filesystem. It reads a **GPT** (GUID Partition Table) disk, finds the **EFI System Partition (ESP)** — a small FAT32 partition mounted at `/boot/efi` — and executes a `.efi` application directly (e.g. `grubx64.efi`, `systemd-bootx64.efi`). UEFI keeps a list of boot entries in NVRAM, which you inspect and edit with `efibootmgr`.

**Secure Boot** is a UEFI feature: firmware verifies each `.efi` binary's cryptographic signature against keys in NVRAM before running it, blocking unsigned/tampered bootloaders.

```bash
# Is this machine booted via UEFI? If this dir exists, yes.
ls /sys/firmware/efi

# List UEFI boot entries and their order
efibootmgr -v
```

Know the pairing: BIOS goes with MBR; UEFI goes with GPT + ESP + Secure Boot.

items.front: What does firmware (BIOS/UEFI) do before any bootloader runs?
items.back: Runs POST (power-on self-test) to initialize and check hardware, then locates and executes the first-stage boot software — either MBR boot code (BIOS) or a .efi application on the ESP (UEFI).

items.front: How big is the MBR and what does it contain?
items.back: 512 bytes: the first disk sector. It holds a tiny bootstrap code stub plus a 4-entry primary partition table. Too small to hold a real bootloader, so it chain-loads one.

items.front: What is the EFI System Partition (ESP) and how is it formatted?
items.back: A small FAT32 partition (commonly mounted at /boot/efi) on a GPT disk where UEFI firmware finds and executes .efi bootloader applications like grubx64.efi or systemd-bootx64.efi.

items.front: What does `efibootmgr` do?
items.back: Reads and modifies the UEFI boot entries stored in firmware NVRAM — listing entries, their .efi targets, and the boot order; you can add, delete, and reorder entries.

items.front: What does Secure Boot enforce?
items.back: Before executing each .efi binary, UEFI verifies its cryptographic signature against keys held in NVRAM, refusing to run unsigned or tampered bootloaders/kernels.

items.prompt: Which set of technologies forms the modern UEFI pairing (as opposed to legacy BIOS)?
choices: GPT + EFI System Partition + Secure Boot
items.explanation: UEFI reads a GPT disk, finds the FAT32 EFI System Partition, executes a .efi application, and can verify it with Secure Boot. MBR + 512-byte sector is the legacy BIOS pairing.

items.prompt: On a running Linux box, how can you quickly tell it was booted in UEFI mode rather than legacy BIOS?
items.explanation: The kernel only populates /sys/firmware/efi when it was booted via UEFI. /proc/cmdline always has content, and there is no /boot/mbr file.

items.prompt: Why does the MBR boot code almost always 'chain-load' a separate bootloader instead of booting the kernel directly?
choices: Because the kernel refuses to be loaded by firmware
Because 512 bytes is far too small to hold logic that can read a filesystem and load a kernel
Because Secure Boot requires a second stage
Because GPT disks cannot store a kernel
items.explanation: The MBR is a single 512-byte sector shared with the partition table, leaving only a tiny stub — nowhere near enough to parse filesystems and load a multi-megabyte kernel, so it hands off to a real bootloader.

items.front: What practical limits does the MBR partition scheme impose that GPT removes?
items.back: MBR caps usable disk size at ~2 TiB and allows only 4 primary partitions. GPT supports far larger disks and many partitions (typically 128).

lessons.title: Bootloader to Kernel: GRUB2, initramfs, and the cmdline
lessons.content:
Firmware hands off to a **bootloader**, whose job is to load the kernel image and an initial RAM filesystem into memory, pass the kernel its command line, and jump into it.

**GRUB2** is the dominant desktop/server bootloader. Its menu and behavior come from `grub.cfg`, which you don't hand-edit — you set values in `/etc/default/grub` and `/etc/grub.d/`, then regenerate with `grub-mkconfig -o /boot/grub/grub.cfg` (Debian: `update-grub`). Embedded systems use **U-Boot** (the ARM SoC workhorse, scriptable via its `bootcmd` environment); minimal UEFI setups often use **systemd-boot**, which is just a thin menu over `.efi` stubs on the ESP.

The bootloader loads two things from `/boot`:

- **`vmlinuz`** — the compressed kernel image (a `bzImage` on x86).
- **`initramfs`** (a.k.a. initrd) — a compressed cpio archive containing a minimal root filesystem with `/init` and just enough kernel modules.

**Why initramfs exists:** the real root filesystem might live on LVM, LUKS-encrypted volumes, RAID, or an NVMe/USB device whose driver isn't built into the kernel. The kernel can't mount a disk it can't talk to. So the bootloader loads a small RAM-based root that contains those drivers; that early userspace probes hardware, loads modules, assembles/unlocks the real root, then pivots to it.

```bash
# The exact command line the running kernel was given
cat /proc/cmdline
# e.g. BOOT_IMAGE=/vmlinuz root=UUID=... ro quiet console=ttyS0

# Peek inside the initramfs to see its bundled tools/modules
lsinitramfs /boot/initrd.img-$(uname -r) | head
```

Key **kernel command-line** tokens: `root=` (which device/UUID is the real root), `ro` (mount it read-only first, fsck-safe), `console=` (where early kernel output goes — e.g. `ttyS0` serial for embedded), and `init=` (override which program becomes PID 1).

items.front: What two files does the bootloader load from /boot, and what is each?
items.back: vmlinuz — the compressed kernel image (bzImage on x86). initramfs/initrd — a compressed cpio archive holding a minimal root FS with /init and early kernel modules.

items.front: Why does an initramfs exist at all? (the key 'aha')
items.back: The real root may sit on LVM/LUKS/RAID/NVMe/USB whose drivers aren't built into the kernel. The kernel can't mount a device it can't drive, so initramfs supplies those modules in a RAM root, sets the real root up, then pivots to it.
items.hint: Drivers needed to mount the real root.

items.front: How do you correctly change GRUB2's configuration?
items.back: Don't hand-edit grub.cfg. Edit /etc/default/grub and scripts in /etc/grub.d/, then regenerate: grub-mkconfig -o /boot/grub/grub.cfg (or update-grub on Debian).

items.front: Name three bootloaders and where each is typically used.
items.back: GRUB2 — mainstream desktop/server. U-Boot — embedded ARM SoCs (scriptable bootcmd). systemd-boot — minimal UEFI-only setups, a thin menu over .efi stubs on the ESP.

items.front: What is the format of an initramfs archive and how do you inspect it?
items.back: It's a compressed cpio archive of a minimal root filesystem. Inspect with lsinitramfs (or unmkinitramfs to extract) on Debian-family systems.

items.prompt: What does the `root=` kernel command-line parameter specify?
choices: The user account that becomes PID 1
The device or UUID holding the real root filesystem to pivot to
The path to the GRUB configuration file
Which TTY shows the login prompt
items.explanation: root= tells the kernel/initramfs which block device (often by UUID) contains the real root filesystem that will replace the initramfs.

items.prompt: Why is `ro` commonly passed on the kernel command line for the initial root mount?
choices: It disables Secure Boot for faster booting
It mounts root read-only first so fsck can safely check the filesystem before it is remounted read-write
It forces the kernel to use the initramfs permanently
It tells GRUB to skip the menu
items.explanation: Mounting read-only first lets a filesystem check run safely; init later remounts the root read-write once it's clean.

items.prompt: On an embedded board with no display, which cmdline token routes early kernel messages to the serial port?
items.explanation: console=ttyS0 (often with a baud rate, e.g. ttyS0,115200) directs kernel console output to the first serial UART — standard practice on headless embedded targets.

items.prompt: Where does the kernel record the exact command line it was booted with?
items.explanation: /proc/cmdline is the read-only view of the actual command line the running kernel received — useful for confirming what root=, ro, console=, etc. were really passed.

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Review the diagram above.

lessons.title: Kernel Handoff to PID 1: init, targets, and diagnostics
lessons.content:
Once the kernel has decompressed, probed hardware, and — via the initramfs — assembled and mounted the **real root filesystem**, it performs the userspace handoff: it `execve()`s the program at `/sbin/init` (overridable with `init=`), which becomes **PID 1**. This is the boundary where kernel-space booting ends and userspace begins. PID 1 is special: it never exits, it adopts and reaps orphaned processes, and it owns service startup.

The pivot itself is precise: early userspace in the initramfs runs `switch_root` (modern) to swap the RAM root for the real root and re-exec the real `init`, freeing the initramfs memory.

**Historically**, PID 1 was **SysV init**, which drove **runlevels** (0=halt, 1=single-user, 3=multi-user text, 5=graphical, 6=reboot) via numbered `/etc/rc?.d/` scripts run serially. **Modern systems** use **systemd**, which replaces runlevels with **targets** — dependency-graphed unit groups started in parallel. The rough mapping: `multi-user.target` ≈ runlevel 3, `graphical.target` ≈ runlevel 5, plus `rescue.target` and `emergency.target` for recovery. systemd brings up the target's dependencies, ending at a login prompt.

```bash
# What target is the system in / set the default
systemctl get-default

# Boot diagnostics: kernel ring buffer, this-boot's journal, timing
dmesg | less                 # kernel messages since power-on
journalctl -b                # all logs from the current boot
journalctl -k -b             # kernel messages only, current boot
systemd-analyze              # total firmware/loader/kernel/userspace time
systemd-analyze blame        # slowest units
systemd-analyze critical-chain  # the dependency path that gated boot
```

**Full narration, power button to login:** firmware POSTs and finds the ESP/MBR → bootloader (GRUB2/U-Boot) loads `vmlinuz` + `initramfs` and passes the cmdline → kernel decompresses, probes hardware, mounts the initramfs → initramfs loads drivers, assembles/unlocks the real root, `switch_root`s into it → kernel `execve`s `/sbin/init` as PID 1 → systemd reaches the default target → getty/display-manager shows the login prompt.

items.front: What exact action marks the kernel-to-userspace handoff at the end of boot?
items.back: The kernel execve()s /sbin/init (overridable via init=), and that program becomes PID 1. Everything before is kernel-space booting; everything after is userspace.

items.front: What makes PID 1 special compared to ordinary processes?
items.back: It is started directly by the kernel, must never exit while the system runs, adopts and reaps orphaned/zombie processes, and is responsible for bringing up all other services.

items.front: How does early userspace swap the initramfs for the real root before running the real init?
items.back: It calls switch_root (the modern replacement for pivot_root + chroot), which makes the mounted real root the new / , re-execs the real /sbin/init, and frees the initramfs RAM.

items.front: Map the common SysV runlevels to systemd targets.
items.back: runlevel 3 (multi-user text) ≈ multi-user.target; runlevel 5 (graphical) ≈ graphical.target; runlevel 1 (single-user) ≈ rescue.target; plus emergency.target for the most minimal recovery shell.

items.front: What is the core architectural difference between SysV runlevels and systemd targets?
items.back: SysV ran numbered rc scripts serially per runlevel. systemd targets are dependency-graphed groups of units started in parallel, tracking ordering (After/Requires) explicitly — faster and more predictable.
items.hint: Serial scripts vs parallel dependency graph.

items.prompt: Which command shows only kernel messages from the current boot via the journal?
items.explanation: journalctl -k limits output to kernel (ring-buffer) messages, and -b restricts it to the current boot. dmesg shows the same kernel buffer but isn't a journal query.

items.prompt: You want to find which single chain of dependencies most delayed boot. Which command is designed for that?
items.explanation: blame lists units by individual init time, but critical-chain shows the time-ordered dependency path that actually gated reaching the target — the real critical path.

items.prompt: A system boots but you only get a minimal recovery shell with most services down. Which target was most likely reached?
items.explanation: emergency.target gives the most minimal environment (root shell, root FS often read-only, almost nothing else started) — typically reached when boot fails badly, e.g. a bad /etc/fstab entry.

items.prompt: In the full power-button-to-login narration, which ordering is correct?
choices: Bootloader → firmware POST → kernel → initramfs → PID 1
Firmware POST → bootloader loads vmlinuz+initramfs → kernel mounts initramfs → switch_root to real root → execve PID 1 → target → login
Firmware POST → kernel → bootloader → real root → PID 1 → initramfs
items.explanation: Firmware runs first (POST, find boot device), the bootloader loads the kernel + initramfs and the cmdline, the kernel mounts the initramfs which sets up and switch_roots into the real root, the kernel execs PID 1 (systemd), which reaches the default target ending at the login prompt.

items.prompt: What does `init=/bin/sh` on the kernel command line accomplish?
choices: Tells the kernel to execve /bin/sh as PID 1 instead of the normal init — useful for emergency recovery
items.explanation: init= overrides which program the kernel execs as PID 1. Pointing it at /bin/sh drops you to a raw shell as PID 1 (no service manager) — a classic password-reset/recovery trick.

lessons.title: Why systemd, and the unit model
lessons.content:
## From SysV scripts to a service manager

For decades, init was **PID 1** running serial shell scripts in `/etc/rc.d/`. Each `init` script started the next, in a fixed order, with no real knowledge of *what* it was starting. Boot was slow (strictly sequential), dependencies were encoded by hand as numeric prefixes (`S20`, `K80`), and once a daemon was launched init forgot about it — no supervision, no restart, no idea which processes belonged to which service.

**systemd** replaced this. It is PID 1 and the system/service manager on essentially every modern non-embedded Linux. Its wins:

- **Parallelism** — independent units start concurrently, not one-at-a-time.
- **Declarative dependencies** — you state relationships in unit files; systemd computes the order.
- **Socket/D-Bus activation** — start a service on first connection, not at boot.
- **cgroup-based supervision** — every process a service spawns lives in a cgroup, so systemd tracks and kills the *whole* tree reliably (no orphaned children).
- **Unified logging** — `journald` captures everything a service writes to stdout/stderr.

Everything systemd manages is a **unit**, identified by `name.type`:

```ini
sshd.service      # a daemon
sshd.socket       # a listening socket (activation)
multi-user.target # a grouping / sync point (like a runlevel)
backup.timer      # a scheduled trigger (cron replacement)
data.mount        # a filesystem mount point
system.slice      # a cgroup resource group
```

The unit *type* is the suffix; the most common is `.service`. Targets are not things that "do work" — they are named groups used as boot milestones and dependency anchors.

items.front: What process is systemd, and what two roles does it play?
items.back: systemd runs as PID 1 (the first userspace process the kernel starts) and acts as both the init system and the system/service manager for the whole machine.

items.front: Name four concrete capabilities systemd has that SysV-init scripts lacked.
items.back: Parallel startup, declarative dependency resolution, on-demand socket/D-Bus activation, cgroup-based process supervision (track & kill the whole tree), and unified logging via journald.

items.front: How does systemd reliably kill every process a service spawned, including double-forked children?
items.back: Each service's processes are placed in their own cgroup. systemd tracks membership by cgroup, not by parsing PIDs, so it can stop or kill the entire process tree even when children re-parent to PID 1.
items.hint: It is not done by PID tracking.

items.front: How is a systemd unit named, and what does the suffix mean?
items.back: A unit is named `name.type`, e.g. `sshd.service`. The suffix after the final dot is the unit *type* and tells systemd what kind of object it is (service, socket, target, timer, mount, slice, etc.).

items.front: What is a `.target` unit, conceptually?
items.back: A grouping/synchronization unit that pulls in a set of other units and acts as a named milestone. It does no work itself; it is the systemd analogue of a SysV runlevel (e.g. multi-user.target, graphical.target).

items.front: Match the use case to the unit type: schedule a nightly job; listen on a port and start a daemon lazily; group CPU/memory limits for a set of services.
items.back: Nightly job -> `.timer`. Lazy port-triggered daemon -> `.socket` (socket activation). Resource grouping -> `.slice` (a cgroup hierarchy node).

items.prompt: A junior engineer says "systemd boots faster than SysV mainly because it skips running scripts." What is the more accurate primary reason?
choices: It starts independent units in parallel and computes order from declared dependencies, instead of running everything strictly sequentially
It is written in C instead of shell, so the same sequential steps run faster
It caches the previous boot and replays it
It disables logging during boot to save time
items.explanation: The big win is parallelism plus dependency resolution: only truly ordered units wait on each other, the rest start concurrently. Language and caching are not the mechanism, and logging stays on.

items.prompt: Which statement about `.target` units is correct?
choices: A target is a daemon process that supervises other daemons
A target is a grouping/milestone unit that pulls in other units and runs no work of its own
A target replaces cgroups for resource control
A target is the only unit type that can have dependencies
items.explanation: Targets are synchronization points / groupings (e.g. multi-user.target), the modern replacement for runlevels. They start no processes themselves; many unit types can carry dependencies.

items.prompt: You see units named `home.mount`, `tmp.mount`, and `dev-sda2.swap`. What generated these?
choices: Someone hand-wrote them; they must be deleted
systemd auto-generates mount/swap units from /etc/fstab and the running mount table
The kernel writes them directly to /etc
items.explanation: systemd generators translate /etc/fstab entries (and active mounts) into transient `.mount`/`.swap` units so the filesystem layer participates in the dependency graph. They are normal, not hand-written.

lessons.title: Unit file anatomy: [Unit], [Service], [Install]
lessons.content:
## Anatomy of a `.service` unit

A unit file is an INI-style file with sections. A typical service:

```ini
[Unit]
Description=My App API
Documentation=https://example.com/docs
After=network-online.target postgresql.service
Wants=network-online.target
Requires=postgresql.service

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/usr/bin/myapp --config /etc/myapp.conf
ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
User=myapp
Group=myapp
WorkingDirectory=/var/lib/myapp
Environment=LOG_LEVEL=info

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

**`[Unit]`** holds metadata and dependencies. The two that confuse people:

- `After=` / `Before=` set **ordering only** — *when* relative to another unit.
- `Wants=` / `Requires=` set **requirement** — *whether the dependency is pulled in*. `Wants=` is soft (start it too, but ignore its failure); `Requires=` is hard (if it fails to start, we fail too). **These are orthogonal to ordering** — `Requires=foo` without `After=foo` will start both at once, which is almost never what you want.

**`[Service]`** defines the runtime: `ExecStart=` (the command), `Restart=` policy, and identity (`User=`/`Group=`). `Type=` is critical and covered next.

**`[Install]`** is read only by `systemctl enable`. `WantedBy=multi-user.target` means "when I'm enabled, create a symlink so multi-user.target wants me at boot." A unit with no `[Install]` section can be started manually but **cannot be enabled** — there is nothing telling systemd where to hook it in.

items.front: What is the difference between `After=` and `Requires=`?
items.back: `After=` controls ORDERING only (start this after that unit). `Requires=` controls REQUIREMENT (pull that unit in, and fail if it fails). They are orthogonal: you usually need BOTH together to get "start B after A, and only if A is up."

items.front: Wants= vs Requires= — what happens to your service if the dependency fails to start?
items.back: With `Wants=` (soft): your service still starts; the dependency failure is ignored. With `Requires=` (hard): if the dependency fails or is stopped, your service is also stopped/fails. Prefer `Wants=` unless the dependency is truly mandatory.
items.hint: Soft = start anyway; hard = die with it.

items.front: What does the `[Install]` section do, and who reads it?
items.back: `[Install]` (e.g. `WantedBy=multi-user.target`) is read only by `systemctl enable`/`disable`. enable creates a symlink in the target's `.wants/` dir so the unit auto-starts at boot. It has no effect on a one-shot `systemctl start`.

items.front: A unit file has no `[Install]` section. What can and cannot you do with it?
items.back: You can `systemctl start`/`stop`/`status` it manually, and other units can depend on it. You CANNOT `systemctl enable` it — there is no WantedBy/RequiredBy telling systemd where to wire it into the boot graph.

items.front: What does `WantedBy=multi-user.target` accomplish on enable?
items.back: It tells `systemctl enable` to create a symlink in `multi-user.target.wants/` pointing at your unit, so that reaching multi-user.target at boot pulls your service in (a soft Wants relationship).

items.front: What is `Restart=on-failure` plus `RestartSec=5`, and why use `User=`?
items.back: `Restart=on-failure` restarts the service only on a nonzero exit/signal (not on clean exit); `RestartSec=5` waits 5s between attempts. `User=myapp` runs the daemon as an unprivileged account instead of root, limiting blast radius if it is compromised.

items.prompt: Your service writes `Requires=postgresql.service` but omits `After=`. Postgres and your app are slow to start. What is the likely bug?
choices: Both start in parallel, so your app may connect before Postgres is ready and crash
Postgres will never start because Requires blocks it
systemd will refuse to load the unit
items.explanation: Requires pulls Postgres in but does NOT order startup. Without `After=postgresql.service`, both launch simultaneously and your app can race ahead of a not-yet-listening database. Requirement and ordering are independent.

items.prompt: You run `systemctl enable myapp` and get "The unit files have no installation config (WantedBy=, RequiredBy=, Also=, Alias= settings ...)." Why?
choices: The unit has no [Install] section, so enable has nowhere to wire it into the boot graph
Only root-owned units can be enabled
items.explanation: enable needs an [Install] section (typically WantedBy=) to know which target should pull the unit in at boot. Add `[Install]` with `WantedBy=multi-user.target` and re-enable.

items.prompt: Which directive belongs in the [Service] section, not [Unit]?
items.explanation: ExecStart= (the command to run) is runtime behavior and lives in [Service]. After=, Requires=, and Description= are metadata/dependencies and belong in [Unit].

items.prompt: What is the safest default restart policy for a long-running daemon you do NOT want restarted after a clean shutdown?
items.explanation: `Restart=on-failure` restarts only on abnormal exit (nonzero code/signal/timeout) but respects a clean `systemctl stop` or exit 0. `Restart=always` would fight an intentional stop.

lessons.title: Type=, file precedence, drop-ins & systemctl verbs
lessons.content:
## Type=, file precedence, and managing units

**`Type=`** tells systemd *how to decide the service is "up"* so it can start dependents at the right moment. Getting it wrong is a classic failure.

- `Type=simple` (default): the process started by `ExecStart` **is** the daemon and stays in the foreground. systemd considers it active immediately.
- `Type=exec`: like simple, but "active" waits until the binary is actually `execve`'d.
- `Type=forking`: `ExecStart` **forks and the parent exits**; the child is the daemon. Pair with `PIDFile=`. Use only for old daemons that daemonize themselves. If you mark a foreground program `forking`, systemd waits for a parent exit that never comes and times out.
- `Type=oneshot`: runs to completion then exits; use `RemainAfterExit=yes` to stay "active." Good for setup tasks.
- `Type=notify`: the daemon calls `sd_notify(READY=1)` when truly ready — the most accurate signal.

### Where units live (precedence)

```text
/etc/systemd/system/        # admin overrides  -- HIGHEST precedence
/run/systemd/system/        # runtime, transient
/usr/lib/systemd/system/    # packaged/vendor defaults -- LOWEST
```

**Never edit vendor files in `/usr/lib`** — a package update overwrites them. Instead use **drop-ins**:

```bash
sudo systemctl edit myapp.service     # creates .../myapp.service.d/override.conf
```

A drop-in only sets the keys you change; it merges over the vendor file and survives upgrades. After editing any unit file by hand you must run:

```bash
sudo systemctl daemon-reload          # re-read unit files from disk
sudo systemctl restart myapp.service  # apply to the running process
```

`daemon-reload` reloads systemd's view of the *files*; `restart` applies them to the *running* service. `enable` wires boot autostart; `start` runs it now — different verbs, different effects.

items.front: What does `Type=` actually control?
items.back: How systemd determines that the service has finished starting up (reached "active"), so it knows when to launch units that ordered themselves After= this one. It does not change how the program runs, only how readiness is detected.

items.front: When should you use `Type=forking`, and what's the danger of misusing it?
items.back: Use `Type=forking` only for legacy daemons that double-fork and let the parent exit (pair with PIDFile=). If you set forking on a foreground program, systemd waits for a parent exit that never happens and the start times out / is marked failed.

items.front: What does `Type=notify` require of the program, and why is it the most accurate?
items.back: The program must call `sd_notify("READY=1")` (libsystemd) when it is genuinely ready to serve. systemd holds dependents until that signal, so there is no race window like with Type=simple, which is "ready" the instant the process exists.

items.front: What is `Type=oneshot` for, and what does `RemainAfterExit=yes` add?
items.back: `Type=oneshot` runs a command to completion then exits (good for setup/migration steps); systemd waits for it to finish before continuing. `RemainAfterExit=yes` keeps the unit reported as "active" after the process exits, so it counts as satisfied.

items.front: Rank the unit search paths by precedence and say which you should edit.
items.back: Highest to lowest: /etc/systemd/system > /run/systemd/system > /usr/lib/systemd/system. Edit /etc (via drop-ins) — never /usr/lib, since package updates overwrite vendor files there.

items.front: What does `systemctl edit myapp.service` create, and why prefer it over editing the vendor file?
items.back: It creates a drop-in `/etc/systemd/system/myapp.service.d/override.conf` that overrides only the keys you set and merges over the vendor unit. It survives package upgrades, whereas edits to /usr/lib/systemd/system get clobbered.

items.front: Distinguish `daemon-reload`, `restart`, `enable`, and `start`.
items.back: daemon-reload: re-read unit FILES from disk into systemd's memory. restart: apply files to the RUNNING process. enable: create boot-time autostart symlink ([Install]). start: run the service NOW. enable does not start; start does not enable.
items.hint: files vs process; boot vs now.

items.prompt: You enable a freshly written service and reboot — it runs. But `systemctl start` it now without rebooting did nothing earlier. Most likely cause?
choices: enable and start are the same; you imagined it
You edited the unit file but never ran `systemctl daemon-reload`, so systemd used a stale/empty view
Services can only start at boot
items.explanation: After creating or editing a unit file, systemd must `daemon-reload` to pick it up. enable created the boot symlink (effective after reboot), but the live manager hadn't re-read the file, so an immediate start saw nothing to do.

items.prompt: A daemon runs fine in the foreground but as a service it logs "start operation timed out" and is killed. The unit says `Type=forking`. Best fix?
choices: Change to `Type=simple` (or `Type=notify` if it supports sd_notify)
items.explanation: `Type=forking` makes systemd wait for the parent to exit. A foreground daemon never forks/exits, so the start times out. Use `Type=simple` for a foreground process, or `Type=notify` if it can signal readiness.

items.prompt: You want to change one environment variable on a vendor-provided unit so it survives package updates. Best approach?
choices: Copy the file to /etc and edit the copy fully
Run `systemctl edit foo.service` and set only Environment= in the drop-in, then daemon-reload
items.explanation: `systemctl edit` writes a minimal drop-in in /etc/.../foo.service.d/ that overrides just that key and merges over the vendor file, surviving upgrades. Editing /usr/lib is clobbered on update; a full /etc copy can drift from upstream changes.

items.prompt: Which command makes systemd re-read unit files after you hand-edited one in /etc?
items.explanation: `systemctl daemon-reload` reloads the manager's view of unit files from disk. (reload-or-restart acts on the service process; reset-failed clears failed state; revert removes overrides.)

lessons.title: Timers, socket activation, hardening & journald
lessons.content:
## Timers, socket activation, hardening, and journald

### Timers (the cron replacement)
A `.timer` triggers a same-named `.service`. Unlike cron, timers integrate with the dependency graph, log to journald, and can catch up missed runs.

```ini
# backup.timer
[Unit]
Description=Nightly backup
[Timer]
OnCalendar=*-*-* 02:30:00
Persistent=true
[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
```

`OnCalendar=` uses systemd's calendar syntax (test with `systemd-analyze calendar`). `Persistent=true` runs the job after boot if the machine was off at the scheduled time. `OnBootSec=`/`OnUnitActiveSec=` give monotonic schedules. List active timers with `systemctl list-timers`.

### Socket activation
A `.socket` unit owns the listening socket; systemd starts the matching service on first connection, enabling lazy start and zero-downtime restarts.

```ini
# myapp.socket
[Socket]
ListenStream=8080
[Install]
WantedBy=sockets.target
```

### Hardening (cheap, high-value)
```ini
[Service]
ProtectSystem=strict      # / mounted read-only to the service
PrivateTmp=true           # private /tmp, /var/tmp
NoNewPrivileges=true      # cannot gain privs via setuid/exec
ProtectHome=true
```
Check a unit's exposure with `systemd-analyze security myapp.service`.

### journald / journalctl
journald stores **structured, indexed binary** logs (set `Storage=persistent` in `journald.conf` to keep them across reboots). Query them:

```bash
journalctl -u myapp.service     # this unit only
journalctl -u myapp -f          # follow (like tail -f)
journalctl -u myapp --since "1 hour ago"
journalctl -b                   # current boot;  -b -1 = previous boot
journalctl -p err               # priority err and worse
journalctl -k                   # kernel ring buffer (dmesg)
```

items.front: Name three advantages a systemd `.timer` has over a crontab entry.
items.back: Timers integrate with the dependency graph (can wait on other units), log to journald with full service output, support Persistent=true to catch up missed runs after downtime, and reuse all service features (User=, hardening, Type=). Cron has none of these.

items.front: In a timer, what do `OnCalendar=` and `Persistent=true` do?
items.back: `OnCalendar=*-*-* 02:30:00` sets a wall-clock schedule (here daily at 02:30). `Persistent=true` makes systemd run the job immediately after boot if the scheduled time was missed while the machine was off.

items.front: How does a .timer know which service to run, and how do you list timers?
items.back: By default a timer triggers the service with the same base name (backup.timer -> backup.service); override with Unit= in [Timer]. List all active timers and their next/last run with `systemctl list-timers`.

items.front: What problem does socket activation solve?
items.back: A `.socket` unit holds the listening socket itself; systemd starts the service only on first connection (lazy start, faster boot) and can hand the same socket to a restarted service, enabling near-zero-downtime restarts since connections queue in the kernel.

items.front: What do ProtectSystem=strict, PrivateTmp=true, and NoNewPrivileges=true each do?
items.back: ProtectSystem=strict: mounts the whole filesystem read-only to the service (writable only where you allow). PrivateTmp=true: gives the service its own /tmp and /var/tmp. NoNewPrivileges=true: forbids gaining new privileges via setuid binaries/exec, blocking privilege escalation.
items.hint: RO fs; private tmp; no setuid gain.

items.front: How do you make journald logs persist across reboots, and how is data stored?
items.back: journald stores logs as structured, indexed binary records. Set `Storage=persistent` in /etc/systemd/journald.conf (and create /var/log/journal) so logs survive reboots; otherwise they default to volatile in /run and are lost on reboot.

items.front: Give the journalctl flags to: show only one unit, follow live, show the previous boot, filter to errors, show kernel messages, and limit by time.
items.back: `-u NAME` (one unit), `-f` (follow), `-b -1` (previous boot; `-b` = current), `-p err` (priority error and worse), `-k` (kernel/dmesg), `--since "1 hour ago"` / `--until` (time window).

items.prompt: A nightly cleanup must still run even if the server was powered off at the scheduled time and only booted at 09:00. Which timer setting handles this?
items.explanation: `Persistent=true` records the last run and, if a scheduled OnCalendar time was missed while off, runs the job shortly after the next boot. The others affect boot-relative timing, accuracy, or jitter — not missed-run catch-up.

items.prompt: You run `journalctl -u myapp -f -p warning`. What do you see?
choices: All historical logs for every unit
A live-following stream of myapp's messages at priority warning and more severe
Logs from the previous boot only
items.explanation: `-u myapp` scopes to that unit, `-f` follows new entries live (tail -f style), and `-p warning` filters to warning and worse. Kernel-only is `-k`; previous boot is `-b -1`.

items.prompt: Which command quickly estimates how exposed a service is, scoring its sandboxing directives?
items.explanation: `systemd-analyze security UNIT` produces an exposure score and lists which hardening directives (ProtectSystem, PrivateTmp, NoNewPrivileges, etc.) are set or missing. is-active/journalctl/cat report status, logs, and the merged file, not a security score.

items.prompt: With socket activation, why can a service restart cause (almost) no dropped client connections?
choices: systemd buffers all application data in RAM
The .socket unit keeps the listening socket open, so new connections queue in the kernel while the service process restarts
items.explanation: Because systemd owns the listening socket, it stays open across a service restart. Incoming connections queue in the kernel backlog and are handed to the freshly started process, so clients see a brief delay rather than a connection refused.

lessons.title: The Ring Model and the Syscall Gateway

items.front: On x86-64, which ring is kernel mode and which is user mode?
items.back: Ring 0 is kernel mode (full privilege: hardware, MMU, privileged instructions). Ring 3 is user mode where ordinary applications run with no direct hardware access.

items.front: What instruction does user code execute to enter the kernel, and why can't it just call kernel functions directly?
items.back: A trap instruction (`syscall` on x86-64, `svc` on ARM). A direct call would bypass the privilege boundary; the trap switches to ring 0 at one fixed, controlled entry point chosen by the kernel.
items.hint: One controlled door, not arbitrary jumps.

items.front: How does the kernel know which operation a syscall is requesting?
items.back: By the syscall number placed in a designated register (rax on x86-64). The kernel uses it to index the syscall table and dispatch to the right handler (e.g. number 1 = write).
items.hint: A number in a register, then a table lookup.

items.front: Why is the user/kernel boundary called a 'narrow' or 'controlled' gateway?
items.back: There is a single fixed entry point, a validated syscall number, and arguments the kernel treats as untrusted. Code cannot jump into arbitrary kernel addresses, which is what preserves isolation.

items.prompt: A normal C program in ring 3 tries to execute a privileged instruction that reprograms the MMU. What happens?
choices: The CPU silently allows it because the process owns its own memory
The CPU raises a fault/exception; the kernel typically kills the process
It is automatically converted into the equivalent system call
It succeeds only if the program was compiled with -O2
items.explanation: Privileged instructions executed in ring 3 trigger a CPU fault. The kernel handles the exception, usually delivering SIGILL/SIGSEGV and terminating the offender. That hardware enforcement is what makes user mode a sandbox.

items.prompt: On x86-64, where does the kernel expect the syscall NUMBER to be when the `syscall` instruction executes?
choices: On the top of the user stack
In the first argument register, rdi
items.explanation: The syscall number goes in rax; arguments go in rdi, rsi, rdx, r10, r8, r9. rdi holds the first argument, not the number. This is the syscall convention, which differs from the C function calling convention.

items.prompt: Which statement best describes what 'trapping into the kernel' does to CPU privilege level?
choices: It permanently elevates the process to ring 0
It switches from ring 3 to ring 0 for the duration of the syscall, then returns to ring 3
It has no effect on privilege; only the scheduler changes rings
It drops the process from ring 3 to ring 4 for safety
items.explanation: A trap raises privilege to ring 0 at the kernel entry point, the kernel does the work, then execution returns to ring 3 where the application resumes. The elevation is temporary and scoped to the call.

items.prompt: Why must the kernel treat syscall arguments (like a pointer passed to write()) as untrusted?
choices: Because user pointers are always encrypted
Because a malicious or buggy ring-3 program could pass garbage, kernel, or out-of-range addresses
Because the C standard requires it
Because pointers change value when crossing the trap
items.explanation: Anything from ring 3 could be hostile or buggy: a bad pointer, an address that overlaps kernel memory, or an invalid length. The kernel must validate and copy carefully (copy_from_user), never blindly dereference, or it would expose a privilege-escalation hole.

lessons.title: What the Kernel Provides, libc, and Syscall Families

items.front: Name the five core services the Linux kernel provides behind the syscall gateway.
items.back: Process scheduling, memory management (virtual memory / paging / MMU), the VFS (unified file interface), the network stack (TCP/IP, sockets), and device drivers.

items.front: What role does glibc/musl play relative to system calls?
items.back: It is a thin wrapper library: it marshals arguments into registers, executes the trap instruction, and converts the kernel's return into a C return value plus errno. It also adds conveniences like stdio buffering.
items.hint: The shim between your C call and the raw trap.

items.front: API vs ABI — what is the difference, and which does Linux keep stable across kernel versions?
items.back: API = source-level contract (function/type names you compile against). ABI = binary contract (argument registers, struct layouts, syscall numbers). Linux keeps the syscall ABI stable so old binaries keep running.

items.front: What does the MMU do, and how does it tie into kernel memory management?
items.back: The MMU translates a process's virtual addresses into physical frame addresses using page tables the kernel sets up, faulting into the kernel on a miss (demand paging). It gives each process its own isolated address space.

items.front: Which syscall underlies both creating threads and creating container namespaces?
items.back: clone(). With different flags it shares more or less of the parent (address space, file descriptors, namespaces). fork() is essentially clone() with a conservative flag set; threads share the address space; CLONE_NEW* flags create new namespaces.
items.hint: One flexible primitive, many flag combinations.

items.prompt: Why might a single printf() in your program NOT correspond to a single write() syscall seen under strace?
choices: strace cannot see write() at all
stdio in libc buffers output, so multiple printf calls may coalesce into one write, or none until flush
printf is a kernel function, not a libc function
write() is deprecated and never actually runs
items.explanation: libc's stdio layer buffers. Several printf calls can accumulate in a userspace buffer and be flushed with one write() (or at exit). This is exactly why strace output sometimes 'lags' your printfs — the syscall happens at flush time.

items.prompt: Reading one file with the classic open/read/close pattern issues roughly how many system calls (ignoring buffering details)?
choices: Zero — file access does not use syscalls
At least three: open, read, close (more read()s for larger files)
items.explanation: open(), read(), and close() are three distinct syscalls; large files need multiple read() calls. fopen/fclose are libc wrappers that themselves call open/close. Each is a separate trap into the kernel.

items.prompt: Which group correctly lists FILE-related system calls?
items.explanation: open/read/write/lseek/mmap are file-oriented. The first option is the process family, the third is the signal family, and the fourth is IPC/synchronization. Recognizing families lets you skim strace output fast.

items.prompt: Linux maintains a stable syscall ABI. What practical guarantee does this give?
choices: Source code never needs recompiling between distros
A binary compiled years ago can still issue the same syscalls and run on a newer kernel
All kernel internal functions keep the same names
items.explanation: The stable syscall ABI means syscall numbers and their argument conventions do not change incompatibly, so an old userspace binary keeps working on a new kernel. Internal kernel symbols and driver APIs, by contrast, are explicitly NOT stable.

lessons.title: Tracing, errno, and What Physically Happens on read()
lessons.content:
When a program 'just fails,' reach for **strace** before guessing. It intercepts every syscall (via ptrace), printing the name, decoded arguments, return value, and on failure the symbolic errno. `ltrace` does the same for library calls. `/proc/<pid>/syscall` shows the syscall a process is blocked in right now.

```bash
strace -f -e trace=openat,read,write cat /etc/hostname
strace -c ls            # histogram: counts + time per syscall
cat /proc/$(pgrep -n sshd)/syscall   # which syscall is it stuck in?
```

A failing line looks like `openat(AT_FDCWD, "/nope", O_RDONLY) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)`. At the ABI level the kernel returned a small negative number; libc saw it, set the thread-local `errno` to `ENOENT`, and returned `-1`. Always check the return value, then read `errno` — strace just shows you both at once.

Distinguish a **syscall** from an **interrupt**. A syscall is *synchronous and voluntary*: your code executes the trap and expects to resume. An **interrupt** is *asynchronous*: a hardware device (NIC, timer, the radar ADC you once serviced) or a software event yanks the CPU into a handler regardless of what was running. Both transfer control to the kernel, but one is requested and one is imposed. Each transfer is a **context switch** of privilege; switching to a *different process* is a heavier context switch (save/restore registers, swap page tables).

What physically happens on `read(fd, buf, 4096)`? (1) libc traps in with `__NR_read` and the three args. (2) Ring 0; VFS routes by fd to the backing filesystem/driver. (3) The kernel checks its **page cache**; on a miss it issues block I/O and the calling process is put to **sleep (S/D state)** while DMA fills a kernel buffer. (4) An **interrupt** from the disk signals completion. (5) The kernel uses **`copy_to_user`** to move bytes from kernel space into your `buf`, validating the address first (never a raw memcpy — a bad pointer must fail safely, not crash the kernel). (6) It returns the byte count; you wake back in ring 3. The mirror, `copy_from_user`, guards writes inbound — the trust boundary enforced in code.

items.front: What does strace show you for each system call, and what underlying mechanism does it use?
items.back: It shows the syscall name, decoded arguments, the return value, and on failure the symbolic errno. It works via the ptrace() mechanism, which lets one process intercept another's syscalls.
items.hint: Name, args, return, errno — powered by ptrace.

items.front: When a syscall fails, what does the kernel return at the ABI level, and what does libc do with it?
items.back: The kernel returns a small negative value (e.g. -ENOENT). libc detects this, stores the positive code in the thread-local errno, and returns -1 (or NULL) to your program.
items.hint: Negative from kernel; -1 plus errno from libc.

items.front: Syscall vs interrupt: what is the key difference?
items.back: A syscall is synchronous and voluntary — user code executes a trap and expects to resume. An interrupt is asynchronous and imposed — a hardware device or event diverts the CPU regardless of what was running. Both enter the kernel.
items.hint: Requested by you vs forced by hardware.

items.front: What do copy_to_user() and copy_from_user() do, and why not a plain memcpy?
items.back: They move data across the user/kernel boundary while validating the userspace address can be safely accessed. A bad user pointer makes them fail cleanly (returning an error) instead of faulting in ring 0 and crashing or corrupting the kernel.
items.hint: Safe, validated copies across the trust boundary.

items.front: Which file reveals the syscall a running process is currently blocked in, and how is that useful?
items.back: /proc/<pid>/syscall. It tells you what a hung or slow process is waiting on (e.g. a read on a network fd) without attaching strace, which is handy for a quick, low-impact diagnosis.

items.prompt: During a read() that misses the page cache and goes to disk, what is the calling process typically doing?
choices: Busy-spinning in ring 3 polling the disk register directly
Sleeping (S or D state) while the kernel issues block I/O and DMA fills a buffer
Running in ring 0 the entire time at 100% CPU
Killed and restarted by the scheduler
items.explanation: On a cache miss the process is put to sleep (interruptible S, or uninterruptible D during the actual transfer) so the CPU can run other work. A completion interrupt from the device later wakes it. User code never polls the disk register directly — that is ring-0 driver territory.

items.prompt: strace prints: openat(AT_FDCWD, "/etc/secret", O_RDONLY) = -1 EACCES (Permission denied). What do you conclude?
choices: The syscall succeeded but returned no data
The open failed due to permissions; errno was set to EACCES and the call returned -1
items.explanation: The return value -1 with EACCES means the openat syscall failed specifically on permissions (not a missing file, which would be ENOENT). This is the canonical strace workflow: find the first failing syscall and read its errno to pinpoint the real problem.

items.prompt: Which command gives you a histogram of how many times each syscall was made and the time spent in each?
items.explanation: strace -c produces a summary table: calls, errors, and time per syscall. -f follows child processes (forks) but still shows the per-call trace; ltrace traces library calls; /proc/self/syscall shows only the current single syscall.

items.prompt: Why does the kernel use copy_to_user() to deliver read() data into your buffer instead of memcpy()?
choices: copy_to_user encrypts the data in transit
The user pointer is untrusted; copy_to_user validates it and fails safely instead of faulting in ring 0
memcpy cannot copy more than 4096 bytes
items.explanation: The destination pointer came from ring 3 and could be invalid or hostile. copy_to_user checks the address and handles a fault gracefully (returning bytes-not-copied) rather than letting a bad dereference crash or compromise the kernel. It is the trust boundary expressed in code.

lessons.title: procfs: Reading the Kernel as Files
lessons.content:
`/proc` is a *virtual* filesystem (procfs). Nothing in it lives on disk; every `read()` is a controlled trip into kernel data structures rendered as text. This is the everything-is-a-file philosophy applied to live kernel state, and it means you can answer most system questions with `cat`, no special tooling required.

Two halves live under `/proc`. First, **global kernel info**: `/proc/cpuinfo` (one block per logical CPU), `/proc/meminfo` (the canonical memory accounting that `free` parses), `/proc/loadavg` (1/5/15-minute run-queue averages plus running/total tasks), `/proc/mounts` (the kernel's live mount table), `/proc/version`, `/proc/cmdline` (the kernel command line from boot), and `/proc/interrupts` (per-CPU IRQ counts). Second, a numbered directory **per process**, named by PID.

Unlike a regular file, a procfs file has size 0 until you read it; the content is generated on demand. That is why `ls -l /proc/meminfo` shows 0 bytes but `cat` returns data.

```bash
# Who am I, how loaded is the box, what booted it?
cat /proc/loadavg            # 0.12 0.18 0.21 2/431 18922
cat /proc/cmdline            # root=UUID=... ro quiet
nproc                        # count of [proc/cpuinfo] processor blocks
grep -c ^processor /proc/cpuinfo

# Is a mount really there? (more truthful than /etc/mtab)
grep ' /home ' /proc/mounts
```

The key mental shift from your radar-tech days: these are not log files being written somewhere, they are a *window* the kernel opens when you look. Reading is cheap and side-effect-free, so explore freely.

items.front: What kind of filesystem is /proc, and where does its content physically live?
items.back: It is a virtual/pseudo filesystem (procfs). The content lives nowhere on disk — it is generated on demand from in-kernel data structures each time you read a file.
items.hint: Think about why ls shows size 0.

items.front: Which /proc file holds the canonical memory accounting, and what userspace tool parses it?
items.back: /proc/meminfo. Tools like `free` (and `vmstat`, `top`) read and reformat /proc/meminfo rather than measuring memory themselves.

items.front: What does /proc/loadavg report?
items.back: The 1-, 5-, and 15-minute load averages (run-queue length, including uninterruptible/D-state tasks), followed by running/total task counts and the last-created PID.
items.hint: Three decimals plus a runnable/total fraction.

items.front: Why does `ls -l /proc/cpuinfo` show a size of 0 bytes even though `cat` returns text?
items.back: procfs files are generated on demand; they have no stored length. The kernel produces the bytes only when a process actually reads the file, so the stat size is reported as 0.

items.front: Which /proc file gives the kernel command line the system booted with?
items.back: /proc/cmdline — e.g. `root=UUID=... ro quiet console=ttyS0`. It reflects what the bootloader passed to the kernel.
items.hint: Ties back to the boot-chain module.

items.prompt: You want to count the number of logical CPUs without running any tool other than reading a file. Which command works?
items.explanation: /proc/cpuinfo emits one block per logical CPU, each beginning with a `processor` line, so counting those lines yields the logical-CPU count (the same figure `nproc` reports).

items.prompt: Which statement about /proc/mounts is most accurate?
choices: It is a static file edited by the administrator at install time
It is the kernel's live view of currently mounted filesystems
It only lists disk partitions, never virtual mounts like tmpfs
items.explanation: /proc/mounts is the kernel's authoritative live mount table (including tmpfs, proc, sysfs, etc.). /etc/fstab is the desired-state config; the two often differ.

items.prompt: Reading files under /proc is best characterized as:
choices: Expensive disk I/O that should be cached
A side-effect-free, cheap inspection of kernel state
Something that requires root for every file
Equivalent to issuing a reboot-class syscall
items.explanation: Reads hit in-memory kernel structures, are cheap, and have no side effects, so /proc is safe to explore freely. (A few per-PID files are root- or owner-restricted, but reading is not destructive.)

items.prompt: Which /proc file would you read to see per-CPU interrupt counts — directly relevant to your radar/hardware instincts?
items.explanation: /proc/interrupts shows one row per IRQ with counts per CPU and the handling device/driver — ideal for spotting which hardware is generating the most interrupts.

lessons.title: Per-Process Introspection: memory, open fds, and the device tree
lessons.content:
Every running process gets `/proc/<pid>/`, the answer key for "what is this process doing." The high-value entries:

- **cmdline** — the exact argv, NUL-separated (`tr '\0' ' '` to read it).
- **status** — human-readable: state, PPID, UIDs, and the memory fields `VmRSS` (resident set, real RAM) and `VmSize` (virtual address space). For "how much memory is this process *actually* using," `VmRSS` is your answer.
- **fd/** — a symlink per open file descriptor. Listing it answers "what files/sockets does this process have open."
- **maps** — every memory region: code, heap, stack, and each mapped library with its permissions.
- **limits**, **cwd**, **root**, **environ**, **stat** round it out.

```bash
pid=$(pgrep -n sshd)               # newest sshd
```
