```bash
cat /proc/$pid/cmdline | tr '\0' ' '; echo
grep -E 'VmRSS|VmSize|^State' /proc/$pid/status
ls -l /proc/$pid/fd                # each fd -> file/socket/pipe it points to
awk '{print $6}' /proc/$pid/maps | sort -u | head   # mapped objects
```

Now **sysfs** (`/sys`): where `/proc` is process-centric, `/sys` is the kernel **device model** laid out as directories. The real tree is `/sys/devices`; `/sys/class/<type>` (e.g. `net`, `block`) and `/sys/block` are curated *symlink views* into it, and `/sys/module/<name>` exposes loaded modules' parameters and reference counts. Map a network interface to its driver and link state without `ip`:

```bash
ls /sys/class/net                  # lo eth0 wlan0 ...
cat /sys/class/net/eth0/operstate  # up / down
cat /sys/class/net/eth0/address    # MAC, same as `ip link`
readlink -f /sys/class/net/eth0    # -> the real /sys/devices/... path
```

items.front: To answer 'how much real RAM is this process using?', which /proc/<pid>/status field do you read — and why not VmSize?
items.back: Read VmRSS (Resident Set Size = pages actually in physical RAM). VmSize is the total virtual address space, which includes unmapped/never-touched and swapped regions, so it overstates real usage.

items.front: How do you list every file, socket, and pipe a process has open using only /proc?
items.back: List /proc/<pid>/fd/ — each numbered entry is a symlink to the open file, socket (socket:[inode]), or pipe. `ls -l /proc/<pid>/fd` resolves them. This is what `lsof -p` reads under the hood.
items.hint: A directory of symlinks per descriptor.

items.front: What does /proc/<pid>/maps show, and name two regions you'd expect to see.
items.back: The process's virtual memory map: one line per region with address range, permissions (r/w/x/p), offset, and backing object. Expect to see the executable's text, the heap, the stack, and each mapped shared library (.so).

items.front: In sysfs, what is the relationship between /sys/class/net/eth0 and /sys/devices/...?
items.back: /sys/class/net/eth0 is a symlink into the real device tree under /sys/devices/. /sys/class and /sys/block are convenience *views* organized by function; /sys/devices is the canonical hierarchy.

items.front: Why is /proc/<pid>/cmdline awkward to cat directly, and how do you fix it?
items.back: Its arguments are separated by NUL bytes, not spaces, so they run together on a terminal. Pipe through `tr '\0' ' '` (then echo a newline) to read it.

items.prompt: A monitoring script must report a daemon's real RAM footprint using only /proc. What should it read?
items.explanation: VmRSS is the resident set — pages actually backed by physical RAM — which is the closest single field to 'real memory used.' VmSize is virtual reservation; MemTotal is system-wide.

items.prompt: You need to know which network interface a driver created and its current up/down state, but `ip` is unavailable. Best source?
items.explanation: /sys/class/net/<iface>/ exposes operstate (up/down), address (MAC), speed, etc. — the same data `ip link` formats. /etc/network/interfaces is config, not live state.

items.prompt: What does `readlink -f /sys/class/net/eth0` return?
choices: The IP address bound to eth0
An error, because /sys is not browsable
The canonical /sys/devices/... path the class entry points to
items.explanation: /sys/class/net entries are symlinks into the real device hierarchy under /sys/devices/...; readlink -f resolves the link to that canonical path.

items.prompt: Which sysfs subtree would you inspect to see a loaded kernel module's parameters and reference count?
items.explanation: /sys/module/<name>/ exposes the module's parameters (parameters/), refcnt, and related attributes for each loaded module.

lessons.title: sysctl Tuning, Persistence, and the Observability Layer
lessons.content:
`/proc/sys/` is special: unlike the read-only views, many of its files are **writable**, and writing one changes a live kernel parameter. The `sysctl` command is just a friendly front-end — the dotted name maps directly to a path: `net.ipv4.ip_forward` ↔ `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward`.

```bash
# Turn this box into a router (live, lost on reboot):
sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward        # read current value
sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1   # set it
echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward   # identical effect

# Reduce swap aggressiveness:
sysctl -w vm.swappiness=10
```

Those writes vanish on reboot. For **persistence**, drop a file in `/etc/sysctl.d/` (the modern, package-friendly location) rather than editing the monolithic `/etc/sysctl.conf`:

```bash
cat >/etc/sysctl.d/99-router.conf <<'EOF'
net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1
vm.swappiness = 10
EOF
sysctl --system     # reload all *.conf from the sysctl.d dirs now
```

The classic **observability tools are all procfs readers**: `vmstat` (memory/swap/IO/CPU from `/proc/stat` + `/proc/meminfo`), `iostat`/`mpstat` (sysstat package), `free` (`/proc/meminfo`), `uptime` (`/proc/loadavg`). They sample these files and compute deltas — nothing magic.

The **MODERN successors** are eBPF-based: `bpftrace`, the bcc tools (`execsnoop`, `opensnoop`, `biolatency`), and `bpftop`. Instead of polling text files, they attach safe, verified programs to kernel hooks (tracepoints, kprobes) for low-overhead, event-driven tracing — the natural evolution of `/proc` sampling, covered fully in the eBPF module. Rule of thumb: reach for `/proc`+`free`/`vmstat` for a quick snapshot; reach for bpftrace/bcc when you need *why* and *which*, per-event, in production.

items.front: How does the sysctl name net.ipv4.ip_forward map to the filesystem?
items.back: Dots become slashes under /proc/sys: net.ipv4.ip_forward = /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward. `sysctl -w name=val` and `echo val > /proc/sys/.../name` are equivalent.

items.front: You ran `sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1`. Why is the box no longer forwarding after a reboot, and how do you fix it?
items.back: Writes to /proc/sys are runtime-only and reset at boot. Persist by adding `net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1` to a file in /etc/sysctl.d/ (e.g. 99-router.conf); it is applied at boot and via `sysctl --system`.

items.front: What does vm.swappiness control, and what does a lower value do?
items.back: It biases the kernel between reclaiming page cache and swapping out anonymous pages. Lower values (e.g. 10) make the kernel avoid swapping process memory, preferring to drop cache; higher values swap more readily.

items.front: What do free, uptime, and vmstat have in common with respect to /proc?
items.back: They are all procfs readers: free and vmstat parse /proc/meminfo and /proc/stat, uptime reads /proc/loadavg. They sample these files (and compute deltas) rather than measuring the kernel directly.

items.front: What is the core advantage of eBPF tools (bpftrace, bcc) over /proc-polling tools like vmstat?
items.back: They attach safe, verified programs to kernel hooks (tracepoints/kprobes) and report per-event data with low overhead — answering 'why' and 'which process/file/syscall' — instead of periodically sampling aggregate counters from text files.

items.prompt: Which command set turns on IP forwarding immediately AND survives a reboot?
choices: sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1  plus a line in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf
items.explanation: The -w write applies it live; persistence requires an entry in /etc/sysctl.d/ (or sysctl.conf) so it is reapplied at boot. fstab is unrelated to kernel parameters.

items.prompt: Why are most files under /proc/sys/ different from the rest of /proc?
choices: They are stored on disk rather than generated
Many are writable, and writing them changes a live kernel parameter
They require a kernel module to appear
They are owned by the user, not root
items.explanation: /proc/sys is the sysctl tree: many entries are writable tunables. Writing one (via echo or sysctl -w) immediately changes kernel behavior at runtime.

items.prompt: What is the recommended modern way to make a sysctl setting persistent on a package-managed system?
choices: Edit the kernel source and recompile
Drop a *.conf file into /etc/sysctl.d/ and run `sysctl --system`
Add it to the GRUB command line every boot
items.explanation: /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf is the drop-in, package-friendly location; `sysctl --system` reloads all such files. It is preferred over editing the monolithic /etc/sysctl.conf.

items.prompt: A daemon is mysteriously opening many files under load and you need to see exactly which paths, per event, in production. Best tool?
items.explanation: opensnoop attaches to the open-family syscalls via eBPF and prints each open with the process and path — event-level 'which file' detail that aggregate /proc samplers like free/vmstat cannot provide.

lessons.title: Device Files: /dev, char vs block, major/minor & mknod
lessons.content:
In Unix "everything is a file," and device files in `/dev` are the handles user space uses to talk to hardware. Two flavors matter:

- **Character devices** (`c`): unbuffered, byte-stream, no random seek granularity guaranteed -- think a UART, `/dev/ttyUSB0`, `/dev/random`. This is the model closest to your radar serial/register world.
- **Block devices** (`b`): fixed-size blocks with a buffer cache and seeking -- disks like `/dev/sda`, `/dev/nvme0n1`.

Run `ls -l /dev` and the first column shows `c` or `b`, and where size would be you instead see two numbers: the **major** and **minor**. The major identifies the *driver*; the minor identifies *which device* that driver handles (or a sub-function). The kernel routes a syscall on `/dev/X` to the driver registered for that major number.

```bash
ls -l /dev/null /dev/sda /dev/ttyS0
# crw-rw-rw- 1 root root  1, 3 ... /dev/null   (char, major 1 minor 3)
# brw-rw---- 1 root disk  8, 0 ... /dev/sda    (block, major 8 minor 0)
```

Historically you created nodes by hand with **`mknod`** (a node is just an inode carrying type + major/minor; it holds no data):

```bash
sudo mknod /dev/mychardev c 240 0
```

The stock pseudo-devices are worth memorizing: `/dev/null` (discard), `/dev/zero` (infinite zeros), `/dev/random` & `/dev/urandom` (entropy), `/dev/full` (always ENOSPC, great for testing error paths), `/dev/tty` (your controlling terminal), and `/dev/loop*` (mount a file as a block device). On modern systems you rarely run `mknod` -- nodes appear automatically, which the next lesson explains.

items.front: In `ls -l /dev`, what does the leading `c` vs `b` tell you, and what replaces the size field?
items.back: `c` = character device (byte stream, e.g. tty, random), `b` = block device (buffered, seekable, e.g. disk). In place of a size you see two numbers: the major and minor device numbers.

items.front: What do the major and minor numbers identify, respectively?
items.back: The major number identifies the driver responsible for the device (the kernel uses it to route operations). The minor number identifies which specific device/instance that driver should handle.

items.front: What does `mknod /dev/foo c 240 0` create, and does it allocate storage?
items.back: It creates a character device node with major 240, minor 0. A device node is just an inode recording type + major/minor; it stores no data and does not by itself connect to a working driver.
items.hint: Think inode metadata, not a file with contents.

items.front: What is `/dev/full` and why is it useful?
items.back: A pseudo-device that always returns ENOSPC ("No space left on device") on write while reading as zeros. It's used to test how programs handle disk-full / write-error conditions.

items.front: Difference between `/dev/random` and `/dev/urandom` on modern Linux?
items.back: Both draw from the same CSPRNG. Classically `/dev/random` could block waiting for entropy; on modern kernels (post-getrandom) it only blocks until the pool is initialized once, then behaves like `/dev/urandom`. For nearly all uses `urandom` is correct.

items.prompt: A syscall is issued on `/dev/sda` (major 8). How does the kernel decide which code handles it?
choices: It matches the filename string `sda` against registered driver names
It uses the major number to look up the driver registered for that major
It uses the minor number to find the correct driver
It reads the file's contents to find a driver header
items.explanation: The major number selects the driver; the minor then tells that driver which device/instance. The filename is just a convenient label on the inode.

items.prompt: Which statement about character vs block devices is correct?
choices: Block devices are unbuffered byte streams; character devices use the page/buffer cache
Character devices are typically byte-stream oriented; block devices use fixed-size blocks and a buffer cache
Only block devices can have major/minor numbers
Character devices cannot be created with mknod
items.explanation: Character devices (`c`) are byte-stream oriented (ttys, random); block devices (`b`) deal in fixed-size, randomly-addressable blocks through a buffer cache (disks).

items.prompt: Why do you rarely need `mknod` on a modern desktop Linux system?
choices: Because /dev no longer uses device nodes at all
Because devtmpfs + udev create and remove nodes automatically as devices appear
Because all devices are now block devices managed by systemd
Because the kernel forbids manual node creation for security
items.explanation: The kernel's devtmpfs populates /dev automatically when drivers register devices, and udev manages permissions, ownership and symlinks on top -- so manual mknod is mostly historical.

lessons.title: The Dynamic /dev: devtmpfs, udev rules & persistent naming
lessons.content:
Static `/dev` (thousands of pre-made nodes) is gone. Today the flow is **kernel -> devtmpfs -> udev**:

1. A driver registers a device. The kernel mounts **`devtmpfs`** on `/dev` and creates a bare node immediately (so early boot has working nodes).
2. The kernel broadcasts a **uevent** over a netlink socket.
3. **`systemd-udevd`** receives it, applies rules, and sets ownership/permissions, adds symlinks, runs helpers, and tags the device.

Inspect any device and the attributes you can match on:

```bash
udevadm info --query=all --name=/dev/ttyUSB0
udevadm info --attribute-walk --name=/dev/ttyUSB0   # walk up the parent chain
udevadm monitor --udev --property                   # watch events live (hotplug a device)
```

Rules live in **`/etc/udev/rules.d/`** (your overrides) and `/lib/udev/rules.d/` (distro defaults); they're processed in lexical order, so prefix with a number like `99-`. A rule is comma-separated *match* keys (`==`) and *assignment* keys (`=`, `+=`). Match on `SUBSYSTEM`, `KERNEL`, the device's own `ATTR{...}`, or a parent's `ATTRS{...}` / `ENV{...}`. A persistent, **reboot-surviving** symlink for a specific FTDI USB-serial adapter:

```bash
# /etc/udev/rules.d/99-radar.rules
SUBSYSTEM=="tty", ATTRS{idVendor}=="0403", ATTRS{idProduct}=="6001", ATTRS{serial}=="AB12CD", SYMLINK+="radar0", MODE="0660", GROUP="dialout"
```

Reload and re-trigger without rebooting:

```bash
sudo udevadm control --reload
sudo udevadm trigger --subsystem-match=tty
```

Now `/dev/radar0` always points at *that* adapter regardless of enumeration order. This is the same mechanism behind predictable NIC names (`enp3s0`) and persistent disk paths in `/dev/disk/by-id/`.

items.front: What is devtmpfs and why does it exist alongside udev?
items.back: devtmpfs is a kernel-maintained tmpfs mounted on /dev that auto-creates a basic node the instant a driver registers a device, so /dev works in early boot. udev runs in user space on top of it to set permissions/ownership, add symlinks, and run rules.

items.front: How does udev learn that a device appeared or went away?
items.back: The kernel broadcasts uevents over a netlink socket; systemd-udevd listens on it, then matches the event against rules in /lib/udev/rules.d and /etc/udev/rules.d.

items.front: In a udev rule, what is the difference between `ATTR{...}` and `ATTRS{...}`?
items.back: `ATTR{...}` matches a sysfs attribute on the device itself; `ATTRS{...}` matches an attribute on any parent device in the chain (e.g. the USB hub/controller). Use `udevadm info --attribute-walk` to find both.
items.hint: The extra S means search up the parent chain.

items.front: Why prefix a custom udev rules file with a number like `99-`?
items.back: Rules files in rules.d are processed in lexical (alphanumeric) order. A high number like 99- makes your rule run after distro defaults so it can override or augment them; SYMLINK+= still accumulates additional links.

items.front: After editing a udev rule, which commands apply it without a reboot?
items.back: `sudo udevadm control --reload` to reload the rule set, then `sudo udevadm trigger` (optionally scoped with --subsystem-match) to replay uevents so the rule fires against already-present devices.

items.prompt: You want `/dev/radar0` to always point at one specific USB-serial adapter, surviving reboots and port changes. The most robust match key is:
choices: The minor number of the device
The mount point of the filesystem on it
items.explanation: Kernel names like ttyUSB0 depend on enumeration order. Matching vendor+product+serial uniquely identifies that physical adapter, so the SYMLINK is stable across reboots and re-plugs.

items.prompt: Which `udevadm` subcommand shows live uevents as you hotplug a device?
items.explanation: `udevadm monitor` prints kernel and udev events in real time; `info` queries a device's current properties/attributes, `trigger` replays events, and `settle` waits for the event queue to drain.

items.prompt: Predictable network interface names like `enp3s0` and persistent paths in /dev/disk/by-id/ are produced by:
choices: The kernel hardcoding them at compile time
udev rules that build stable names from bus topology / device attributes
DHCP for NICs and the filesystem for disks
items.explanation: udev derives these names/symlinks from stable attributes (PCI bus path, disk WWN/serial), giving deterministic naming instead of probe-order-dependent eth0/sda.

items.front: Kernel uevent to udev to /dev node + rule
items.back: Review the diagram above.

lessons.title: Kernel Modules & a Hello-World Char Driver

items.front: What is a `.ko` file, and how does `insmod` differ from `modprobe`?
items.back: A .ko is a compiled loadable kernel module. `insmod` loads exactly the file you name with no dependency handling. `modprobe` looks the module up in /lib/modules, resolves and loads dependencies via modules.dep, and honors /etc/modprobe.d/ options and blacklists.

items.front: What does `depmod` do and when must you run it?
items.back: depmod scans installed modules and (re)builds modules.dep (and the alias map) that modprobe uses to resolve dependencies. Run it after installing/removing modules in /lib/modules (kernel package installs run it for you).

items.front: What is the kernel taint flag and what commonly sets it?
items.back: Taint marks the kernel as being in a state maintainers won't fully support. Loading an out-of-tree or proprietary/non-GPL module, force-loading, or a prior oops sets it. Check `dmesg` or /proc/sys/kernel/tainted; bug reports from tainted kernels are usually dismissed.

items.front: Where do you set persistent module parameters and blacklists?
items.back: In files under /etc/modprobe.d/. e.g. `options snd_hda_intel power_save=1` sets a param; `blacklist nouveau` prevents autoloading. modprobe (and the initramfs) read these.

items.front: Why must a module's use count (refcount) be zero before `rmmod` succeeds?
items.back: The count tracks open handles / dependent modules. Unloading while in use would free code still being executed and crash the kernel, so rmmod refuses (you see it in the lsmod 'Used by' column).

items.front: In a char driver, what is the role of `struct file_operations`?
items.back: It's a table of function pointers (.open, .read, .write, .release, .unlocked_ioctl, ...) that the kernel calls when user space performs the corresponding syscall on the device node. It maps VFS operations to your driver code.

items.front: How does a platform driver get bound to a hardware block, and how does it then reach the registers?
items.back: It declares an of_match_table; the kernel matches the device tree node's `compatible` string to the driver and calls its probe(). The driver then ioremap()s the MMIO register region and uses request_irq() to claim the interrupt line.

items.prompt: You wrote hello.c. Which build command compiles it against the running kernel's headers?
items.explanation: Kernel modules must be built with the kbuild system against matching kernel headers; `make -C .../build M=$PWD` (with `obj-m += hello.o`) does that. Plain gcc can't produce a valid .ko, and insmod/modprobe load, not build.

items.prompt: Which is the MODERN, recommended way to register a character device (vs the legacy register_chrdev)?
choices: mknod() called from inside the module
items.explanation: The cdev interface -- reserve a dev_t range with alloc_chrdev_region, then cdev_init/cdev_add to bind your file_operations -- is the current model. register_chrdev is the older all-in-one shortcut. request_irq and platform_device_register serve different purposes.

items.prompt: For an EE bringing up a custom memory-mapped peripheral on an ARM SoC, the path from device tree to register access is:
choices: compatible-string match -> probe() -> ioremap() MMIO -> request_irq()
items.explanation: The device tree's compatible string binds the platform driver, whose probe() runs; the driver ioremap()s the MMIO register block to get a virtual address and request_irq()s to handle interrupts -- the kernel-side analogue of poking radar registers.

lessons.title: Debian-Family: dpkg, APT, and the .deb
lessons.content:
On Debian and Ubuntu there are **two layers**. `dpkg` is the low-level tool that unpacks and registers a single `.deb` file; it knows nothing about where packages come from and will not fetch dependencies. **APT** (`apt`, `apt-get`, `apt-cache`) is the high-level layer that talks to repositories, resolves dependencies, and then calls `dpkg` to do the actual install.

A `.deb` is just an `ar` archive containing `control.tar` (metadata: name, version, `Depends:`, maintainer scripts) and `data.tar` (the files to install). You can crack one open without installing it:

```bash
ar t mytool.deb              # list members
dpkg-deb -I mytool.deb       # show control info
dpkg-deb -c mytool.deb       # list files it would install
```

Day-to-day commands:

```bash
sudo apt update              # refresh the package index
sudo apt install nginx       # resolve deps + install
apt-cache policy nginx       # candidate version + repo source
sudo dpkg -i mytool.deb      # install a local .deb (no deps!)
sudo apt install -f          # then fix missing deps
```

Repositories are configured in `/etc/apt/sources.list` and `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/*.list` (or the newer deb822 `.sources` format). Each line names a mirror URL, a suite (e.g. `bookworm`), and components (`main`, `contrib`). `apt update` downloads the **index** from each; `apt install` then knows what is available. The mental model that matters most: `apt` is online and dependency-aware, `dpkg` is local and dumb. Reach for `dpkg` only when you already have a `.deb` in hand or need to inspect the package database directly.

items.front: What is the division of labor between `dpkg` and `apt`?
items.back: `dpkg` is the low-level engine: it unpacks/configures a single local `.deb` and records it in the package database, but does not fetch dependencies. `apt` is the high-level front end: it talks to repositories, resolves dependencies, downloads packages, then invokes `dpkg` to install them.
items.hint: One is online and dependency-aware; one is local and dumb.

items.front: A `.deb` file is fundamentally what kind of archive, and what are its two main payload members?
items.back: It is an `ar` archive. The two payloads are `control.tar.*` (metadata: package name, version, `Depends:`, maintainer scripts) and `data.tar.*` (the actual files to be installed onto the filesystem). A `debian-binary` member holds the format version.
items.hint: Same `ar` format used for static libraries; metadata vs. files.

items.front: After `sudo dpkg -i tool.deb` fails complaining about missing dependencies, what single command repairs it?
items.back: `sudo apt install -f` (or `apt --fix-broken install`). `dpkg` installed the package but cannot fetch deps; `apt -f` reads the broken state, downloads the missing dependencies from the repos, and completes the configuration.
items.hint: Let the online layer clean up after the dumb one.

items.front: Where are APT repositories configured, and what three things does a sources entry specify?
items.back: In `/etc/apt/sources.list` and `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/*.list` (or deb822 `.sources` files). Each entry specifies the mirror URL, the suite/distribution (e.g. `bookworm`), and the components (e.g. `main contrib non-free`).

items.prompt: You want to see every file a local `tool.deb` would install WITHOUT installing it. Which command?
items.explanation: `dpkg-deb -c` lists the contents of a `.deb` file on disk. `dpkg -L` lists files of an already-INSTALLED package (and takes a package name, not a file). `apt-cache show` reads index metadata, not a local file's file list.

items.prompt: What does `sudo apt update` actually do?
choices: Upgrades all installed packages to their newest versions
Refreshes the local copy of the repositories' package indexes
items.explanation: `apt update` only re-downloads and verifies the package INDEX from each configured repo, so APT knows what versions exist. Upgrading installed packages is `apt upgrade` / `apt full-upgrade` — a separate step.

items.prompt: Which tool will fetch and install a package's dependencies automatically?
items.explanation: Only `apt install` resolves and downloads dependencies from repositories. `dpkg -i` installs one local file without fetching deps; `dpkg-deb -x` and `ar x` merely extract the archive.

items.prompt: `apt-cache policy nginx` is most useful for answering which question?
choices: What candidate version is available and from which repo?
items.explanation: `apt-cache policy` shows the installed version, the candidate (what would be installed), and the priority-ranked list of repositories/mirrors offering it. It is the go-to tool when you are unsure where a version is coming from.

lessons.title: RPM/dnf, pacman, Signing, and Owning Files
lessons.content:
The same two-layer idea reappears across families. **Red Hat/Fedora**: `rpm` is the low-level tool, `dnf` (formerly `yum`) is the dependency-resolving front end; repos live in `/etc/yum.repos.d/*.repo`. **Arch**: `pacman` is a single tool that does both, with repos in `/etc/pacman.conf` and the AUR for community builds. Translating between families is mostly vocabulary:

```bash
# Debian            # RHEL/Fedora          # Arch
apt install pkg     dnf install pkg        pacman -S pkg
apt remove pkg      dnf remove pkg         pacman -R pkg
apt update          dnf check-update       pacman -Sy
dpkg -L pkg         rpm -ql pkg            pacman -Ql pkg
dpkg -S /path/file  rpm -qf /path/file     pacman -Qo /path/file
```

Two cross-cutting skills matter most in practice. **Which package owns a file?** When a stray binary or config appears, ask the database: `dpkg -S /usr/bin/curl`, `rpm -qf /usr/bin/curl`, or `pacman -Qo`. The inverse — list everything a package installed — is `dpkg -L`, `rpm -ql`, `pacman -Ql`.

**Package signing and trust.** This is the security backbone. Every repo publishes a signed index; your machine holds the repo's public **GPG key**. APT verifies the signature on the `InRelease`/`Release` file, which in turn lists the cryptographic hashes of every package — so a tampered or man-in-the-middle package fails verification. RPM signs each package directly (`rpm --checksig`). The crucial mental model: **you trust a key, not a server.** A compromised mirror cannot forge a validly signed package. Modern Debian keeps third-party keys in `/etc/apt/keyrings/` and references them per-source with `signed-by=`, rather than the deprecated global `apt-key`, so one vendor's key cannot vouch for another's packages.

items.front: Across Debian, RHEL, and Arch, what command answers "which installed package owns this file?"
items.back: Debian: `dpkg -S /path/to/file`. RHEL/Fedora: `rpm -qf /path/to/file`. Arch: `pacman -Qo /path/to/file`. All query the local package database to map a file back to its owning package.

items.front: What command lists ALL files installed by an already-installed package on each family?
items.back: Debian: `dpkg -L pkg`. RHEL/Fedora: `rpm -ql pkg`. Arch: `pacman -Ql pkg`. This is the inverse of the "who owns this file?" query.

items.front: In the package-signing security model, what exactly do you trust, and why does that defeat a malicious mirror?
items.back: You trust a maintainer's/repo's GPG public key, not the server hosting the files. The signed index commits to the hash of every package, so any tampered package (even from a compromised mirror or MITM) fails signature/hash verification. A mirror cannot produce a validly signed index without the private key.
items.hint: Trust the key, not the host.

items.front: Why is modern APT's `signed-by=` keyring approach safer than the old global `apt-key`?
items.back: With global `apt-key`, any key in the global trust set could vouch for ANY repository. With per-source `signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/vendor.gpg`, a given key only authorizes its own repo, so one third party's key cannot silently authenticate packages from another source. It scopes trust.
items.hint: Scoping trust to one repo instead of all.

items.prompt: A mysterious binary `/usr/bin/widget` appears on a Fedora box. Which command tells you which package put it there?
items.explanation: `rpm -qf FILE` (query-file) maps a file path to its owning package. `rpm -ql` is the inverse (list a package's files) and expects a package name. `rpm -V` verifies a package against its recorded metadata.

items.prompt: Why can a compromised package mirror NOT slip a backdoored package onto a correctly configured Debian system?
choices: Because APT downloads everything over HTTPS only
Because the signed index commits to each package's hash, and the mirror lacks the private signing key
Because dpkg refuses to install anything from a mirror
items.explanation: Trust rests on the GPG signature of the repo index, which lists each package's hash. Without the private key the mirror cannot forge a valid signature, and an altered package won't match the signed hash. HTTPS alone does not provide this (mirrors are often plain HTTP); trust is in the key.

items.prompt: Which mapping correctly translates `apt install pkg` to Arch?
items.explanation: `pacman -S pkg` syncs/installs a package (with dependencies) from the configured repos — the analogue of `apt install`. `-R` removes, `-Qo` queries the owner of a file, and `-Sy` alone just refreshes the database.

items.prompt: On RHEL/Fedora, what is the relationship between `rpm` and `dnf`?
choices: They are unrelated; dnf is for containers only
`rpm` resolves dependencies and `dnf` installs single files
`dnf` resolves dependencies from repos and drives `rpm` for the low-level install
`dnf` replaced `rpm` entirely and rpm no longer exists
items.explanation: Just like dpkg/apt on Debian: `rpm` is the low-level package tool, while `dnf` (successor to yum) is the high-level resolver that pulls from repos and ultimately invokes the rpm machinery. Both still exist and have distinct roles.

lessons.title: Source Builds, Flatpak/Snap/Nix, and Security Updates
lessons.content:
Sometimes no package exists, so you build from source. The classic Autotools dance:

```bash
./configure --prefix=/usr/local   # probe system, generate Makefile
make -j$(nproc)                    # compile
sudo make install                  # copy artifacts into --prefix
```

The trap: `make install` scatters files with no database tracking them, so there is no clean uninstall and no dependency bookkeeping. That pain is *why packaging exists*. Prefer `--prefix=/usr/local` (or tools like `checkinstall`/`stow`) to keep hand-built software out of `/usr` where the package manager rules.

**The system-vs-language tension.** Language managers — `pip`/`venv`, `npm`, `cargo` — install into their own ecosystems and ignore the OS database. Mixing `sudo pip install` with apt-managed Python is a classic way to break a system, because both fight over the same `/usr/lib/python*`. The discipline: keep language deps in **virtual environments** / project-local dirs (`venv`, `node_modules`, `~/.cargo`), and let the system manager own the system. This unresolved tension is a major motivation for **containers** (Module 14).

**Modern universal formats** sidestep the conflict by bundling dependencies and sandboxing:
- **Flatpak** — desktop apps, runtime + sandbox (Flathub), portals for access.
- **Snap** — Canonical's confined packages with auto-update; uses squashfs.
- **AppImage** — one self-contained executable file, no install step.
- **Nix** — purely functional, content-addressed store (`/nix/store`) giving reproducible, atomically rollback-able environments.

**Staying patched.** Apply security updates promptly: `apt upgrade` / `dnf upgrade`, and on Debian/Ubuntu enable `unattended-upgrades` for automatic security patches. Use **version pinning/holds** (`apt-mark hold pkg`, dnf `versionlock`, APT pin priorities) to freeze a known-good version, but audit held packages — a forgotten hold is a lingering vulnerability.

items.front: What is the classic three-step Autotools build-from-source sequence, and what does each step do?
items.back: `./configure` probes the system and generates a Makefile (set install location with `--prefix`); `make` compiles the sources; `sudo make install` copies the built artifacts into the prefix. The downside: nothing records the installed files, so there is no clean uninstall or dependency tracking.

items.front: Why is `sudo pip install <pkg>` system-wide considered risky on a modern distro?
items.back: The system Python is managed by apt/dnf. A system-wide pip install writes into the same `/usr/lib/python*` paths, can overwrite or shadow distro-managed files, and creates a state the OS package manager doesn't know about — leading to breakage on upgrades. Use a `venv` / project-local install instead.
items.hint: Two managers fighting over the same files.

items.front: Contrast how Flatpak/Snap/AppImage handle dependencies versus a traditional .deb/.rpm.
items.back: Traditional packages share libraries with the rest of the system and rely on the package manager to resolve dependencies. Flatpak/Snap/AppImage instead BUNDLE their dependencies (and typically run sandboxed/confined), trading larger size and duplicated libs for isolation, portability across distros, and freedom from the host's dependency state.

items.front: What makes Nix's approach to package management reproducible and rollback-friendly?
items.back: Nix installs every build into a content-addressed, immutable store (`/nix/store/<hash>-name`). Because each package and its exact dependency closure has a unique path, multiple versions coexist without conflict, builds are reproducible, and switching/rolling back an environment is just repointing symlinks atomically.
items.hint: Immutable hashed store + atomic profile switches.

items.front: How do you freeze a Debian package at its current version, and what is the maintenance hazard?
items.back: `sudo apt-mark hold <pkg>` (RHEL: dnf `versionlock`; APT also supports pin priorities in `/etc/apt/preferences.d/`). The hazard: a held package is excluded from upgrades including SECURITY updates, so a forgotten hold becomes a silent, lingering vulnerability. Audit holds periodically (`apt-mark showhold`).
items.hint: hold/versionlock — but it also blocks security patches.

items.prompt: What is the main reason `make install` is inferior to using a package for the same software?
choices: It compiles slower than the package build
It leaves untracked files with no clean uninstall or dependency management
items.explanation: `make install` just copies files into the prefix; no database records them, so removal is manual and error-prone and dependencies aren't tracked. Solving exactly this bookkeeping problem is why package managers exist.

items.prompt: You need a Python data tool for one project without disturbing the apt-managed system Python. Best practice?
choices: sudo pip install into the system site-packages
Create a venv and pip install into it
apt remove python3 then reinstall via pip
items.explanation: A virtual environment (`python -m venv .venv`) isolates the project's packages from the system Python entirely, avoiding the system-vs-language conflict. The other options corrupt or destabilize the OS-managed interpreter.

items.prompt: Which universal package format is a single self-contained executable file that requires no installation step?
items.explanation: An AppImage bundles the app and its libraries into one executable file you can `chmod +x` and run anywhere. Flatpak and Snap install through their own daemons/runtimes; Nix uses a store under /nix.

items.prompt: On Debian/Ubuntu, which mechanism automatically applies security updates without manual intervention?
items.explanation: `unattended-upgrades` is the package/service that periodically installs security (and optionally other) updates automatically. `apt-mark hold` does the opposite (freezes a package); the others inspect or reconfigure state.

lessons.title: Regular Expressions: BRE, ERE, and PCRE
lessons.content:
A regex is a tiny pattern language for matching text. Linux ships **three flavors**, and knowing which one a tool speaks saves hours of confusion.

- **BRE** (Basic RE) is the default for `grep` and `sed`. Here `+`, `?`, `{`, `(`, `|` are *literal* unless backslash-escaped: you write `\(group\)`, `a\{2,3\}`, `foo\|bar`.
- **ERE** (Extended RE) is `grep -E` (aka `egrep`) and `sed -E`. Metacharacters work *unescaped*: `(group)`, `a{2,3}`, `foo|bar`. Almost always what you want.
- **PCRE** (Perl-compatible) is `grep -P` and most languages. Adds lazy quantifiers, lookaround, `\d`, `\b`, named groups.

The building blocks:

- **Anchors:** `^` start of line, `$` end of line.
- **Character classes:** `[abc]`, ranges `[a-z]`, negation `[^0-9]`, and POSIX classes `[[:digit:]]`, `[[:alpha:]]`, `[[:space:]]`.
- **The dot** `.` matches any single character (except newline).
- **Quantifiers:** `*` (0+), `+` (1+), `?` (0 or 1), `{n}`, `{n,}`, `{n,m}`.

```bash
# ERE: match a US-style phone number
grep -E '\(?[0-9]{3}\)?[-. ]?[0-9]{3}[-. ]?[0-9]{4}' contacts.txt

# BRE equivalent quantifier braces need escaping
grep '[0-9]\{3\}-[0-9]\{4\}' contacts.txt
```

Rule of thumb: reach for `-E` so your patterns read like the textbook. Drop to `-P` only when you need lookaround or lazy matching.

items.front: What is the core syntactic difference between BRE and ERE?
items.back: In BRE, the metacharacters +, ?, {, }, (, ), and | are literal and must be backslash-escaped to act as operators. In ERE they work unescaped. `grep` defaults to BRE; `grep -E` selects ERE.
items.hint: Think about which characters need a backslash.

items.front: What do the anchors ^ and $ match?
items.back: ^ matches the start of a line and $ matches the end of a line. They are zero-width: they match a position, not a character. So ^$ matches an empty line.

items.front: What does the POSIX class [[:digit:]] mean, and why prefer it over [0-9]?
items.back: [[:digit:]] matches any decimal digit. It is locale-aware and unambiguous inside a bracket expression. Note the double brackets: the inner [:digit:] is the class, the outer [ ] is the bracket expression.

items.front: What do . , * , + , and ? mean in a regex?
items.back: . matches any single character (except newline). * means 0 or more of the preceding atom, + means 1 or more, ? means 0 or 1 (optional). So .* matches any run of characters including nothing.
items.hint: One of them matches 'any char', three are quantifiers.

items.prompt: Which grep flag enables Perl-compatible regex (PCRE), unlocking features like \d, \b, and lookaround?
items.explanation: -P selects PCRE. -E is ERE (no \d or lookaround), -F is fixed-string literal matching, and -G is the default BRE.

items.prompt: Inside a bracket expression, what does [^0-9] match?
choices: Any single character that is NOT a digit 0-9
The start of a line followed by digits
items.explanation: A ^ as the first character inside [ ] negates the class, so [^0-9] matches any one character that is not a digit. Outside brackets ^ is an anchor.

items.prompt: In a BRE pattern (plain grep), how do you match exactly three digits?
items.explanation: In BRE the interval braces are literal unless escaped, so you must write [0-9]\{3\}. Unescaped {3} only works in ERE (grep -E). \d is a PCRE feature.

items.front: What are egrep and fgrep equivalent to in modern grep?
items.back: egrep == grep -E (extended regex). fgrep == grep -F (fixed strings, no regex interpretation, faster for plain substrings). Both standalone commands are deprecated in favor of the flags.

lessons.title: Grouping, Backreferences, Greedy vs Lazy, and ripgrep
lessons.content:
Beyond single characters, regex lets you capture and reuse chunks of a match.

- **Groups** `(...)` (ERE/PCRE) bundle atoms so a quantifier applies to the whole group: `(ab)+` matches `ababab`. Groups also *capture* the matched text.
- **Alternation** `|` inside a group: `(cat|dog|fox)`.
- **Backreferences** `\1`, `\2` reuse what a group captured. `\b(\w+) \1\b` finds doubled words like "the the". Backreferences need BRE-escaped groups in `sed`/grep (`\(...\)` with `\1`) or PCRE.
- **Greedy vs lazy:** quantifiers are greedy by default, matching as much as possible. In PCRE, appending `?` makes them lazy (minimal). On `<a><b>`, `<.*>` greedily grabs the whole thing; `<.*?>` stops at the first `>`.

```bash
# Find lines with a repeated word (PCRE)
grep -P '\b(\w+)\s+\1\b' essay.txt

# Lazy match: capture the first HTML tag only
grep -oP '<.*?>' page.html | head
```

**ripgrep (`rg`)** is the MODERN replacement for grep: recursive by default, respects `.gitignore`, fast, PCRE2 via `-P`, with great defaults.

```bash
rg -i 'timeout' --type py          # case-insensitive, only .py files
rg -n -A2 -B2 'panic'              # line numbers + 2 lines of context
rg -o '\b\d{1,3}(\.\d{1,3}){3}\b' access.log   # pull out IPv4 addresses
rg -l 'TODO'                       # just the filenames that match
```

Key flags shared with grep: `-i` ignore case, `-v` invert, `-o` only-matching, `-c` count, `-A/-B/-C` context. Use `rg` interactively; keep `grep` for portable scripts.

items.front: Greedy vs lazy: what does <.*> match versus <.*?> on the string <a><b>?
items.back: <.*> is greedy and matches the entire <a><b> (as much as possible). <.*?> is lazy and matches just <a> (the smallest match), stopping at the first >. The trailing ? makes the quantifier minimal in PCRE.
items.hint: The ? after the quantifier flips the behavior.

items.front: What is a backreference and how do you find a doubled word with one?
items.back: A backreference (\1, \2, ...) reuses text captured by an earlier group. To find a repeated word: grep -P '\b(\w+)\s+\1\b' — group 1 captures a word, and \1 requires the same word to appear again.

items.front: Why use a group like (ab)+ instead of ab+?
items.back: A quantifier binds to the single preceding atom. ab+ matches a followed by one-or-more b (ab, abb, abbb). (ab)+ applies + to the whole group, matching repetitions of the pair: ab, abab, ababab.

items.front: Name three things ripgrep (rg) does by default that plain grep does not.
items.back: rg searches recursively by default (no -r needed), automatically skips files listed in .gitignore and hidden/binary files, and shows colored output with grouped per-file results. It is also substantially faster on large trees.

items.prompt: You want matches for 'error' plus the 3 lines before AND after each match. Which flag?
items.explanation: -C3 gives 3 lines of context on both sides. -A3 is After only, -B3 is Before only. -n shows line numbers and takes no count argument.

items.prompt: Which flag prints only the matched portion of each line rather than the whole line?
items.explanation: -o (only-matching) prints just the substring that matched, one per line. -v inverts the match, -l lists filenames, -c counts matching lines.

items.prompt: Which ERE pattern most reasonably matches a dotted IPv4 address (ignoring strict 0-255 range checks)?
items.explanation: \b[0-9]{1,3}(\.[0-9]{1,3}){3}\b captures one to three digits, then a dot-plus-octet group repeated exactly three times, with word boundaries. Note the literal dot must be escaped as \. — an unescaped . matches any character, so the first option is too loose.

items.prompt: You need to search for the literal string 'a.b*c' without regex interpretation. Best approach?
items.explanation: -F (fixed-strings) treats the pattern as a literal, so . and * are not special. The other forms interpret . as any-char and * as a quantifier, matching far more than intended.

lessons.title: sed: Stream Editing, Addresses, and In-Place Edits
lessons.content:
`sed` is a non-interactive stream editor: it reads input line by line, applies commands, and prints the result. Its workhorse is **substitution**.

```bash
sed 's/old/new/'      # replace FIRST 'old' on each line
sed 's/old/new/g'     # replace ALL (global) on each line
sed 's/old/new/gi'    # global + case-insensitive
sed 's/\(foo\)bar/\1baz/'   # backref: capture foo, keep it
```

The `s` command is `s/PATTERN/REPLACEMENT/FLAGS`. In the replacement, `&` means "the whole match" and `\1`..`\9` are captured groups (sed uses BRE, so groups are `\(...\)` unless you pass `-E`). Pick any delimiter to avoid escaping slashes in paths:

```bash
sed 's#/usr/local#/opt#g' paths.conf   # # as delimiter
```

**Addresses** restrict which lines a command touches:

```bash
sed '3s/x/y/'        # only line 3
sed '2,5d'           # delete lines 2 through 5
sed '/^#/d'          # delete comment lines (regex address)
sed -n '10,20p'      # -n suppresses auto-print; p prints range 10-20
sed '/START/,/END/s/foo/bar/'   # only between START and END markers
```

**In-place editing** with `-i` rewrites the file. Always keep a backup until you trust it:

```bash
sed -i.bak 's/DEBUG/INFO/g' app.conf   # edits file, saves app.conf.bak
```

The **hold space** is sed's second buffer (alongside the pattern space). Commands `h`/`H` copy to it, `g`/`G` copy from it, `x` swaps them — enabling multi-line tricks like reversing files (`tac`). It is sed's advanced gear; know it exists.

items.front: What does the g flag do in sed 's/a/b/g' and what happens without it?
items.back: Without g, sed replaces only the FIRST occurrence of the pattern on each line. With g (global), it replaces ALL occurrences on each line. The g is per-line, not per-file (every line is processed regardless).

items.front: In a sed replacement, what do & and \1 refer to?
items.back: & stands for the entire matched text. \1 (through \9) refer to text captured by the corresponding \(...\) group. Example: sed 's/[0-9]\{3\}/[&]/' wraps a 3-digit match in brackets using &.
items.hint: One is the whole match, one is a captured group.

items.front: What is sed's hold space?
items.back: A second buffer separate from the pattern space (the current line). Commands h/H copy pattern->hold, g/G copy hold->pattern, and x swaps them. It lets sed remember data across lines for multi-line operations.

items.front: What does sed -i.bak 's/x/y/' file do?
items.back: It edits the file in place (overwriting it with the substitution applied) AND saves the original as file.bak first. Supplying a suffix to -i creates a backup; bare -i edits with no backup, which is risky.

items.prompt: Which sed command deletes every line that begins with a # (comment lines)?
items.explanation: /^#/d uses a regex address (lines starting with #) plus the d (delete) command. The first option only strips the leading # but keeps the line; /#/p with default printing would duplicate matches.

items.prompt: How do you print only lines 10 through 20 of a file with sed?
items.explanation: -n suppresses the automatic printing of every line, so only the explicit p on the 10,20 address range is output. Without -n, sed would print all lines AND print 10-20 a second time.

items.prompt: Why might you write sed 's#/var/log#/srv/log#g' using # instead of /?
choices: The pattern contains / characters, so a different delimiter avoids escaping them
items.explanation: sed lets you choose any delimiter after s. When the pattern or replacement contains slashes (like file paths), picking # (or any other char) avoids a clutter of escaped \/ sequences. It has no effect on speed or case.

items.prompt: What does sed '/START/,/END/d' do?
choices: Deletes only the lines containing START and END
Deletes every line between the first START and the next END, inclusive
Deletes lines NOT between START and END
Prints the range and deletes the rest
items.explanation: A regex range address /START/,/END/ selects from the first line matching START through the next line matching END (inclusive); d deletes that block. This pattern is common for removing config sections.

lessons.title: awk for Columnar Data, plus join/sort/uniq/jq Pipelines
lessons.content:
`awk` is a column-oriented language built on a **pattern { action }** model: for each input record (line), if the pattern matches, run the action. Fields split on whitespace into `$1`, `$2`, ..., with `$NF` = last field and `$0` = whole line.

Key built-in variables: `NR` (record/line number, running total), `NF` (number of fields on this line), `FNR` (per-file line number), `FS` (input field separator), `OFS` (output field separator). `BEGIN { }` runs before input; `END { }` runs after the last line.

```bash
awk '{print $1, $NF}' data.txt           # first and last column
awk -F: '{print $1}' /etc/passwd          # FS = colon: usernames
awk 'NR>1 && $3 > 100' table.tsv          # skip header, filter col 3
awk 'BEGIN{OFS="\t"} {print $2,$1}' f      # swap cols, tab-separated
```

**Associative arrays** make awk the go-to for summaries. Count requests per IP and produce a top-10:

```bash
awk '{count[$1]++} END {for (ip in count) print count[ip], ip}' access.log \
  | sort -rn | head
```

Mean and max of a numeric column:

```bash
awk '{sum+=$1; if($1>max) max=$1} END {print "mean",sum/NR,"max",max}' nums.txt
```

Supporting tools chain together: `cut -d, -f2` (extract field 2), `sort -k3 -rn` (sort by column 3, reverse numeric), `uniq -c` (count adjacent duplicates — sort first!), `comm` (compare two sorted files), `join` (relational join on a key), `paste` (merge files side by side), and `jq` (MODERN, query JSON):

```bash
# Classic top-talkers idiom
awk '{print $1}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head

# Parse JSON: names of running pods
kubectl get pods -o json | jq -r '.items[].metadata.name'
```

Reach for awk whenever you think "summarize this column."

items.front: Describe awk's pattern-action execution model.
items.back: awk reads input one record (line) at a time. For each record it evaluates each pattern { action } rule: if the pattern is true (or omitted, meaning always), it runs the action. BEGIN{} runs once before input, END{} once after the last record.

items.front: In awk, what are $1, $NF, $0, and NF?
items.back: $1 is the first field, $NF is the LAST field (NF = number of fields, used as an index), and $0 is the entire current record/line. NF itself is the count of fields on the current line.
items.hint: NF is a count; $NF uses it as an index.

items.front: What is the difference between NR and FNR in awk?
items.back: NR is the total record number across ALL input (it keeps counting into the second, third file). FNR resets to 1 at the start of each new file. FNR==NR is the classic test for 'still reading the first file' when processing two files.
items.hint: One resets per file, one does not.

items.front: How do awk associative arrays make counting easy? Give a one-liner.
items.back: Arrays are keyed by arbitrary strings and auto-create entries. To count occurrences of column 1: awk '{count[$1]++} END {for (k in count) print count[k], k}'. Each unique value of $1 becomes a key whose value is its tally.

items.front: What do FS and OFS control, and how do you set FS to a colon on the command line?
items.back: FS is the input field separator (how $1, $2 are split); OFS is the output field separator (what print inserts between comma-separated args). Set FS to colon with awk -F: '...' or BEGIN{FS=":"}. Set OFS in BEGIN, e.g. BEGIN{OFS="\t"}.
items.hint: Input split vs output join; -F sets the input one.

items.prompt: Why must you 'sort' before 'uniq -c' in a pipeline like sort | uniq -c?
choices: uniq -c is faster on sorted input but works either way
uniq only collapses ADJACENT duplicate lines, so identical lines must first be grouped together by sorting
sort removes blank lines that confuse uniq
items.explanation: uniq compares only adjacent lines. Duplicates scattered through the file won't be counted together unless sort first brings identical lines next to each other. This is the basis of the sort | uniq -c | sort -rn top-N idiom.

items.prompt: Which awk program prints the mean of the first numeric column?
items.explanation: Accumulate the column into sum on every line, then in END divide by NR (the total number of records). Printing in BEGIN runs before any input so sum and NR are 0/empty; option 1 prints only the total.

items.prompt: What does the -r flag do in jq -r '.items[].metadata.name'?
choices: Outputs raw strings without surrounding JSON quotes
Restricts output to the root object
items.explanation: -r (raw output) emits string values without JSON double-quotes, which is what you want when feeding names into a shell pipeline. Without -r each name would print wrapped in quotes.

items.prompt: What does sort -k3 -rn do?
choices: Sorts on the 3rd column, in reverse numeric order
Keeps only 3 columns and removes newlines
Sorts the first 3 characters of each line
items.explanation: -k3 selects column 3 as the sort key, -n treats it as a number (so 10 sorts after 2, not before), and -r reverses to descending. Combined, you get largest-value-first ordering by the third field.

lessons.title: Script Structure, set -euo pipefail & IFS
lessons.content:
Every robust bash script opens the same way, and as an ex-technician you can treat this as the pre-flight checklist that separates professional glue from a fragile hack.

Start with a portable shebang: `#!/usr/bin/env bash` finds bash via `PATH` instead of hardcoding `/bin/bash`, which matters because bash lives in different places across distros and macOS.

Next, the safety triad `set -euo pipefail`:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
IFS=$'\n\t'
```

- `-e` (errexit): abort the moment any command returns non-zero, instead of blindly charging ahead on a failed `cd`.
- `-u` (nounset): treat use of an *unset* variable as an error, catching typos like `$flie` before they silently expand to empty.
- `-o pipefail`: make a pipeline fail if *any* stage fails, not just the last. Without it, `false | true` reports success.

Setting `IFS=$'\n\t'` (newline and tab only, dropping the default space) is the classic defense against word-splitting filenames that contain spaces.

These are not magic — `set -e` has well-known blind spots (it won't trigger inside `if`/`&&` tests, and a function's failure can be masked), which is exactly why you still write explicit checks and run ShellCheck. Think of `set -euo pipefail` as turning your script from "keep going and hope" into "fail fast and loud," the same discipline as a built-in test that halts on the first out-of-spec reading rather than shipping the unit.

items.front: Why use `#!/usr/bin/env bash` instead of `#!/bin/bash`?
items.back: `env` resolves `bash` through `PATH`, so the script runs against whichever bash is installed rather than assuming a fixed path. bash lives in different locations across distros and macOS (often a newer one under `/usr/local` or Homebrew), so the env form is more portable.

items.front: Expand the three options in `set -euo pipefail`.
items.back: `-e` (errexit): exit on any command failing. `-u` (nounset): error on referencing an unset variable. `-o pipefail`: a pipeline returns the first non-zero exit of any stage, not just the last command's status.

items.front: What problem does setting `IFS=$'\n\t'` solve?
items.back: The default `IFS` includes the space, so unquoted expansions get word-split on spaces — breaking iteration over filenames like `my file.txt`. Restricting IFS to newline and tab stops space-splitting. (Still prefer quoting and `while read -r` / null-delimited iteration.)

items.front: Give one well-known blind spot of `set -e`.
items.back: `set -e` does NOT trigger when a command runs as the condition of `if`, `while`, `until`, or on the left of `&&`/`||`. A failing command inside those contexts is treated as a normal boolean test, so errors there are silently tolerated.

items.prompt: Without `pipefail`, what does `false | true` return as the pipeline's exit status?
choices: 0, the status of the last command (true)
items.explanation: By default a pipeline's exit status is the status of its LAST command, so `false | true` reports 0 (success) and the upstream failure is hidden. `set -o pipefail` changes this so the pipeline reflects the first failing stage.

items.prompt: With `set -u` active, what happens at `echo "$undefined_var"`?
items.explanation: `set -u` (nounset) makes referencing an unset variable a fatal error ("unbound variable"), catching typos. Use `${undefined_var:-}` to intentionally allow an empty default under nounset.

items.prompt: Which line belongs at the very TOP of a robust bash script?
choices: set +e to keep going on errors
items.explanation: The portable shebang `#!/usr/bin/env bash` must be the first line, immediately followed by `set -euo pipefail` to enable fail-fast behavior before any real work runs. `trap` comes after, once cleanup targets exist.

items.front: If `set -euo pipefail` is so good, why still run ShellCheck and write explicit tests?
items.back: `set -e` has documented blind spots (conditions, command substitutions, functions called in tests) and can lull you into a false sense of safety. ShellCheck statically catches quoting, word-splitting, and logic bugs that runtime flags miss, so the two are complementary, not redundant.

items.front: What is the difference between `set -e` and `set -x`?
items.back: `set -e` controls error behavior (exit on failure). `set -x` is a *debugging* trace: it prints each command (with expansions) to stderr before running it. They are orthogonal — you often add `set -x` temporarily or run `bash -x script.sh`.

lessons.title: Parameter Expansion & Arrays
lessons.content:
Parameter expansion is bash's built-in string toolkit — using it avoids spawning `sed`/`cut`/`basename` subprocesses and keeps scripts fast and quote-safe.

Defaults and assertions:

```bash
name=${1:-world}        # use $1, or 'world' if unset/empty
: "${API_KEY:?must set API_KEY}"  # abort with message if unset
name=${name:=guest}     # assign default into the variable
```

Trimming with patterns (greedy doubles the symbol):

```bash
path=/var/log/app.log
${path##*/}   # app.log   (strip longest leading */)
${path%/*}    # /var/log  (strip shortest trailing /*)
${path%.log}  # /var/log/app (strip suffix)
```

Substitution and slicing:

```bash
${path/log/LOG}   # replace first match
${path//\//_}     # replace all '/' with '_'
${path:5:3}       # substring: offset 5, length 3
${#path}          # string length
```

Arrays are essential for building argument lists safely — never store multiple arguments in a single space-separated string.

```bash
files=(report.txt "my notes.md" data.csv)
echo "${files[@]}"     # each element quoted separately
echo "${#files[@]}"    # element count
files+=(extra.log)     # append
```

Associative arrays (bash 4+, declare them):

```bash
declare -A age
age[alice]=30; age[bob]=25
for k in "${!age[@]}"; do echo "$k=${age[$k]}"; done
```

The golden rule: always expand with `"${arr[@]}"` (quoted, `@`, not `*`) so each element survives spaces intact — the array equivalent of the quoting discipline that keeps filenames safe.

items.front: What does `${var:-default}` do, and how does `${var:=default}` differ?
items.back: `${var:-default}` expands to `default` if `var` is unset or empty but leaves `var` unchanged. `${var:=default}` does the same expansion AND assigns `default` into `var` as a side effect.

items.front: For `f=/var/log/app.log`, what do `${f##*/}` and `${f%/*}` produce?
items.back: `${f##*/}` strips the longest leading match of `*/`, giving `app.log` (basename). `${f%/*}` strips the shortest trailing match of `/*`, giving `/var/log` (dirname). `#` trims from the front, `%` from the back; doubling makes it greedy.

items.front: Difference between `${path/old/new}` and `${path//old/new}`?
items.back: `${path/old/new}` replaces only the FIRST occurrence of `old`; `${path//old/new}` replaces ALL occurrences. Anchors: `${path/#old/new}` matches at the start, `${path/%old/new}` at the end.

items.front: How do you abort a script with a custom message if `API_KEY` is unset?
items.back: Use the error expansion: `: "${API_KEY:?must set API_KEY}"`. If `API_KEY` is unset or empty, bash prints the message to stderr and exits non-zero. The leading `:` is a no-op command so the expansion is the only effect.

items.prompt: Why expand an array as `"${arr[@]}"` rather than `${arr[*]}` or unquoted?
choices: `"${arr[@]}"` yields each element as a separate, intact word even with spaces
They are identical in all cases
items.explanation: Quoted `"${arr[@]}"` produces one shell word per element, preserving spaces inside elements — exactly what you want when passing an argument list. `"${arr[*]}"` joins all elements into one string (separated by the first IFS char), and unquoted forms word-split, breaking elements with spaces.

items.prompt: You need a key/value map keyed by username. What declaration do you use?
items.explanation: `declare -A` creates an associative array (string-keyed map), available in bash 4+. `declare -a` (lowercase) makes an indexed array. Associative arrays MUST be declared with `-A` before use; you cannot create one implicitly.

items.prompt: Given `s=hello`, what does `${s:1:3}` evaluate to?
items.explanation: Substring expansion `${s:offset:length}` starts at offset 1 (the second char, 'e') and takes 3 characters: 'e', 'l', 'l' -> `ell`. `${#s}` would give the length, 5.

items.front: How do you iterate over the KEYS of an associative array `age`?
items.back: Use the `!` (indirection) form: `for k in "${!age[@]}"; do echo "$k -> ${age[$k]}"; done`. `${!age[@]}` lists keys; `${age[@]}` lists values. The quoting keeps keys with spaces intact.

items.front: Why prefer parameter expansion over piping to `sed`/`basename` for simple string work?
items.back: Parameter expansion is built into the shell, so it avoids forking a subprocess per operation (faster in loops) and sidesteps an extra layer of quoting/escaping. For trimming paths, defaults, and simple replacement it is cleaner and safer than external tools.

lessons.title: Control Flow, Functions, getopts & Safe File Loops
lessons.content:
With safety and strings handled, structure the logic. Use `[[ ]]` for tests, not the old `[ ]`/`test`:

```bash
if [[ -f $file && $count -gt 0 ]]; then ...; fi
```

`[[ ]]` is a bash keyword: it does no word-splitting or glob expansion on its operands (so unquoted `$file` is safe), supports `&&`/`||`/`==` pattern matching and `=~` regex. Plain `[ ]` is just the `test` command — it word-splits, needs every variable quoted, and is only worth it for strict POSIX sh. For integer math use the arithmetic context `(( ))`:

```bash
if (( count > 0 )); then ((total += count)); fi
```

`case` beats long if-chains for dispatch, and functions keep things tidy — always declare loop and temp variables `local`:

```bash
log() { printf '%s\n' "$*" >&2; }   # log to stderr
backup() {
  local src=$1 dest=$2
  cp -a -- "$src" "$dest"
}
```

Functions return an *exit code* (0-255) via `return`; emit real data on stdout and capture with `$(func)`.

Parse flags with `getopts` (POSIX, short options):

```bash
while getopts ":o:v" opt; do
  case $opt in
    o) out=$OPTARG ;;
    v) verbose=1 ;;
    *) echo "usage: ..." >&2; exit 2 ;;
  esac
done
shift $((OPTIND - 1))
```

The single most important loop idiom is safe filename iteration — never `for f in $(ls)`. Use `while read -r` with a null delimiter:

```bash
while IFS= read -r -d '' f; do
  printf 'processing %s\n' "$f"
done < <(find . -type f -print0)
```

`-r` keeps backslashes literal, `IFS=` preserves leading/trailing whitespace, `-d ''` splits on NUL so spaces and newlines in names are harmless.

items.front: Name two concrete advantages of `[[ ]]` over `[ ]` in bash.
items.back: 1) `[[ ]]` does no word-splitting or glob expansion on its operands, so unquoted `$var` is safe and empty values don't break the test. 2) It supports `&&`, `||`, `==` glob pattern matching and `=~` regex directly. `[ ]` is the external/builtin `test` and requires careful quoting.

items.front: When would you still use `[ ]` instead of `[[ ]]`?
items.back: When the script must be portable POSIX `sh` (e.g. `#!/bin/sh`, running under dash/busybox), since `[[ ]]` is a bash/ksh extension not in POSIX. In that case use `[ ]` and quote every variable.

items.front: Why declare variables `local` inside functions?
items.back: Bash variables are global by default, so an unmarked assignment inside a function can clobber a caller's variable (especially loop counters like `i`). `local` scopes the variable to the function, preventing action-at-a-distance bugs.

items.front: How does a bash function return data vs. a status?
items.back: `return N` sets the exit STATUS (0-255), used for success/failure, read via `$?`. To return DATA, print it to stdout and capture with command substitution: `result=$(myfunc)`. Conflating the two (e.g. `return $bignum`) truncates mod 256.
items.hint: stdout for data, return for status.

items.prompt: Why is `for f in $(ls)` an unsafe way to loop over files?
choices: The unquoted command substitution word-splits on IFS and glob-expands, breaking on filenames with spaces, newlines, or shell metacharacters
items.explanation: `$(ls)` output is word-split on IFS (spaces, tabs, newlines) and then pathname-expanded, so any filename containing whitespace or glob chars is mangled or split into multiple iterations. Use `while IFS= read -r -d '' f; do ...; done < <(find . -print0)` or a plain glob `for f in *`.

items.prompt: In the safe loop `while IFS= read -r -d '' f`, what does the `-r` flag do?
choices: Prevents backslashes from being interpreted as escape characters
items.explanation: `-r` (raw) tells `read` not to treat backslashes as escapes, so a filename like `a\b` is read literally. `IFS=` preserves leading/trailing whitespace, and `-d ''` sets a NUL delimiter to match `find -print0`. You should essentially always use `read -r`.

items.prompt: After a `getopts` parsing loop, why run `shift $((OPTIND - 1))`?
choices: To discard the parsed options so the remaining positional arguments start at $1
items.explanation: `getopts` advances `OPTIND` past the options it consumed. `shift $((OPTIND - 1))` drops those, so `$1`, `$2`, ... and `$@` now refer to the non-option (positional) arguments — e.g. the file paths after the flags.

items.prompt: Which construct is the right tool for integer comparison in bash?
items.explanation: The arithmetic context `(( a > b ))` does numeric comparison and lets you write `count`, `total += 1`, etc. naturally. Inside `[[ ]]`, `>` is a STRING (lexical) comparison, so `[[ 9 > 10 ]]` is true lexically — a classic bug. Use `(( ))` for math, `[[ ]]` for strings/files.

items.front: Why send log/diagnostic output to stderr (`>&2`) in a function meant to also return data?
items.back: If the function's data is captured via `$(func)`, anything printed to stdout becomes part of the captured value. Routing logs to stderr (`printf ... >&2`) keeps them visible on the terminal / journald without polluting the captured result.

lessons.title: Traps, ShellCheck, Process Substitution & the Python Boundary
lessons.content:
Production scripts clean up after themselves. `trap` registers a handler that runs on signals or pseudo-signals:

```bash
tmp=$(mktemp -d)
cleanup() { rm -rf -- "$tmp"; }
trap cleanup EXIT          # runs on normal exit, error, or Ctrl-C
trap 'echo "failed at line $LINENO" >&2' ERR
```

`trap ... EXIT` is the workhorse: it fires no matter how the script ends (success, `set -e` abort, or signal), so a temp dir is always removed — the scripting analog of a `finally` block, and the same discipline as powering down a test rig cleanly.

Process substitution `<(...)` lets a command read another command's output as if it were a file, avoiding temp files and the subshell-variable-loss of pipes:

```bash
diff <(sort a.txt) <(sort b.txt)
while read -r line; do count=$line; done < <(wc -l < big.log)
```

Before shipping, **run ShellCheck** (`shellcheck script.sh`) — it statically flags unquoted expansions, useless `cat`, missing `read -r`, and `set -e` gotchas; treat a clean ShellCheck pass as mandatory, like a clean lint. For runtime issues, `bash -x script.sh` or `set -x` traces execution.

The most important judgment call is **when to abandon bash for Python**. Reach for Python (`subprocess`, `pathlib`, `argparse`, `json`, `venv`) once a script: grows past ~50-100 lines; needs real data structures (lists of dicts, nested JSON); does HTTP/API calls or parses structured data; needs proper error handling, retries, or unit tests; or has quoting that keeps biting you. Bash excels at gluing CLI tools and short pipelines; Python excels at logic, data, and testability. Recognizing that boundary early is itself a senior skill.

items.front: Why is `trap cleanup EXIT` preferred over putting `rm -rf "$tmp"` at the end of the script?
items.back: `EXIT` fires on EVERY exit path — normal completion, a `set -e` abort, an error, or a caught signal — so the temp dir is removed even when the script dies early. A cleanup line at the bottom is skipped on any premature exit. It is bash's `finally`.
items.hint: What happens if the script aborts halfway?

items.front: What does the `ERR` trap give you that `EXIT` does not?
items.back: `trap '...' ERR` runs specifically when a command fails (returns non-zero) under `set -e`, letting you log context like `$LINENO` or the failing command before exiting. `EXIT` runs unconditionally and can't distinguish success from failure on its own (you check `$?`).

items.front: What does process substitution `<(cmd)` provide?
items.back: It runs `cmd` and exposes its output via a filename (a `/dev/fd/...` path or FIFO) so another command can read it as a file — e.g. `diff <(sort a) <(sort b)`. It also avoids the pipe pitfall where `cmd | while read` runs the loop in a subshell and loses variable changes.

items.front: What is ShellCheck and why run it on every script?
items.back: ShellCheck is a static analyzer for shell scripts. It catches unquoted variables, missing `read -r`, useless `cat`, `set -e` blind spots, and dozens of common bugs before runtime. A clean ShellCheck pass is the shell equivalent of a clean lint and should be treated as mandatory.

items.prompt: Which is the STRONGEST signal that you should rewrite a bash script in Python?
choices: It uses more than one pipe
It needs to parse and manipulate nested JSON / structured data with real error handling
items.explanation: Nested data structures, JSON/HTTP, real error handling, and testability are exactly where bash gets painful and Python (json, requests/urllib, dataclasses, try/except, unit tests) shines. Pipes, loops, and calling grep are bread-and-butter bash and not reasons to switch.

items.prompt: Which Python standard-library trio replaces the typical 'shell out + manipulate paths + parse flags' job when porting a script?
items.explanation: `subprocess` (run external commands safely with argument lists, no shell quoting), `pathlib` (object-oriented path/file handling), and `argparse` (declarative flag parsing with help and validation) are the modern idiomatic replacements for shelling out, path munging, and getopts.

items.prompt: How do you trace exactly which commands a script runs, with their expanded arguments?
items.explanation: `bash -x script.sh` (or `set -x` within the script) prints each command to stderr after expansion, just before executing it — the go-to runtime trace. ShellCheck is static (no execution); `set -e` controls error behavior, not tracing.

items.prompt: Why does `count=0; ... | while read x; do ((count++)); done; echo $count` print 0?
choices: The piped `while` runs in a SUBSHELL, so its `count` changes don't reach the parent
items.explanation: In a pipeline, each stage runs in a subshell, so the loop's `count` increments are lost when the subshell exits. Use process substitution `done < <(cmd)` (loop runs in the current shell) so the variable survives — a key reason `<(...)` exists.

items.front: Summarize the bash-vs-Python boundary heuristic in one sentence.
items.back: Stay in bash for short scripts that glue CLI tools and pipelines together; switch to Python once you need data structures, HTTP/JSON, robust error handling/retries, unit tests, or you've passed roughly 50-100 lines and quoting keeps biting.

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Decision diagram contrasting when to stay in bash (gluing CLI tools, short pipelines, always set -euo pipefail and ShellCheck) versus when to switch to Python (JSON/data structures, HTTP, error handling, testing, past ~50-100 lines).

lessons.title: The Native Build Pipeline & Libraries
lessons.content:
Turning `hello.c` into a runnable program is four stages, not one. `gcc` orchestrates them: the **preprocessor** (`cpp`) expands `#include`/`#define` into a single translation unit; the **compiler** (`cc1`) turns C into target assembly; the **assembler** (`as`) turns assembly into a relocatable object file (`.o`); the **linker** (`ld`) resolves symbols across objects and libraries into the final ELF executable. You can stop at any stage:

```bash
gcc -E hello.c -o hello.i   # preprocess only
gcc -S hello.c -o hello.s   # compile to assembly
gcc -c hello.c -o hello.o   # assemble to object
gcc hello.o -o hello        # link
```

Libraries come in two flavors. A **static library** (`.a`) is just an archive of `.o` files; the linker copies the needed code *into* your binary at link time, so the result is self-contained but larger and frozen. A **shared library** (`.so`) is loaded at *run time* by the dynamic linker; many programs share one copy in memory, and you can patch the library without relinking. Build and link a shared lib:

```bash
gcc -fPIC -c mathutil.c -o mathutil.o
gcc -shared mathutil.o -o libmathutil.so
gcc main.c -L. -lmathutil -o app
```

`-fPIC` (position-independent code) is mandatory for shared objects because they can be mapped at any address. Inspect symbols with `nm` and `readelf`. The trade-off is the classic one: static = portability and no runtime dependency hunting; dynamic = smaller binaries, shared memory, and security updates without recompiling every consumer.

items.front: Name the four stages of the native C build pipeline, in order.
items.back: Preprocess (cpp) -> Compile (cc1, C to assembly) -> Assemble (as, assembly to .o object) -> Link (ld, resolve symbols into the final executable).

items.front: What is the difference between a `.a` and a `.so` file?
items.back: A `.a` is a static library: an archive of `.o` files whose code is copied into the binary at link time. A `.so` is a shared object: loaded at run time by the dynamic linker and shared among processes.

items.front: Why must shared library code be compiled with `-fPIC`?
items.back: Position-Independent Code uses relative addressing so the `.so` can be mapped at any virtual address at load time, allowing it to be shared between processes without fixed-address relocation.
items.hint: It can be mapped anywhere in memory.

items.front: What does `gcc -c file.c` produce, and what does it skip?
items.back: It produces a relocatable object file (`file.o`) by running preprocess + compile + assemble. It skips the link stage, so the result is not yet executable.

items.front: Give one advantage each for static and dynamic linking.
items.back: Static: self-contained binary, no runtime dependency resolution, easy to ship. Dynamic: smaller binaries, one shared copy in RAM across processes, and library security patches without recompiling consumers.

items.prompt: Which command stops the build after generating assembly but before assembling to an object file?
items.explanation: `-S` runs the preprocessor and compiler and emits a `.s` assembly file. `-E` stops after preprocessing; `-c` continues through assembly to a `.o`; `-shared` produces a shared library.

items.prompt: In `gcc main.c -L. -lmathutil -o app`, what does `-lmathutil` tell the linker to find?
choices: A library file named libmathutil.so or libmathutil.a
An object file named mathutil.o only
A source file named mathutil.c to recompile
items.explanation: The `-l<name>` flag expands to `lib<name>.so` (preferred) or `lib<name>.a`, searched in the paths given by `-L`. It does not pull in headers or source.

items.prompt: A security flaw is found in a widely used library. Which linking model lets you fix every program that uses it WITHOUT recompiling them?
choices: Static linking, by editing the .a archive
items.explanation: With dynamic linking the code lives in a shared `.so` loaded at runtime, so replacing that one file patches all consumers. Statically linked programs each baked in a copy and must be relinked/recompiled.

items.prompt: Which tool would you use to list the symbols defined and referenced in an object file or library?
items.explanation: `nm` lists symbols (T = defined text, U = undefined/referenced). `ldd` lists shared-library dependencies of an executable, not its symbol table; `make` builds; `cpp` preprocesses.

lessons.title: Dynamic Linker, make, and Modern Build Systems
lessons.content:
At run time the **dynamic linker/loader** (`ld.so` / `ld-linux.so`) maps every `.so` your program needs into memory and binds symbols. Use `ldd ./app` to list those dependencies and see which resolve to a path and which print `not found`. The search order is: paths baked into the binary's **rpath/runpath**, then the `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` environment variable, then the system cache `/etc/ld.so.cache` (rebuilt by `ldconfig` from `/etc/ld.so.conf` and `/usr/lib`). The classic 'library not found' bug:

```bash
ldd ./app                 # libmathutil.so => not found
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=.  # quick dev fix
./app                     # now resolves
```

`LD_LIBRARY_PATH` is a development crutch, not a deployment fix; for shipping, bake an **rpath** (`gcc ... -Wl,-rpath,'$ORIGIN/lib'`) or install the library into a standard dir and run `ldconfig`.

**Build orchestration** keeps you from retyping `gcc`. `make` reads a Makefile of targets, prerequisites, and recipes; it rebuilds a target only when a prerequisite is newer:

```make
CC ?= gcc
CFLAGS = -Wall -O2
app: main.o util.o
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) $^ -o $@
%.o: %.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@
clean:
	rm -f *.o app
```
