
Note recipes must be **tab-indented**, and `$@`/`$^`/`$<` are the target, all prerequisites, and first prerequisite. Hand-written Makefiles don't scale, so modern projects use **CMake** (the de facto C/C++ standard) as a meta-build that *generates* Makefiles or **Ninja** files; **Meson** is a newer, fast alternative that also targets Ninja. You write `CMakeLists.txt` once and build portably with `cmake -G Ninja .. && ninja`.

items.front: What does `ldd ./app` show you?
items.back: The shared libraries (`.so`) the dynamic linker will load for that executable, and where each resolves to on disk -- or `not found` if it cannot be located.

items.front: List the dynamic linker's library search order.
items.back: 1) rpath/runpath embedded in the binary, 2) LD_LIBRARY_PATH env var, 3) the system cache /etc/ld.so.cache (built by ldconfig from /etc/ld.so.conf and standard lib dirs).

items.front: What does `ldconfig` do?
items.back: It scans the directories in /etc/ld.so.conf (and trusted defaults), updates the cache /etc/ld.so.cache, and refreshes the soname symlinks so the dynamic linker can find newly installed shared libraries.

items.front: Why is setting LD_LIBRARY_PATH a poor deployment fix, and what is better?
items.back: It is environment-global, fragile, and can shadow system libraries; it leaks to child processes. Better: embed an rpath/runpath (e.g. $ORIGIN/lib) at link time, or install the lib to a standard dir and run ldconfig.

items.front: In a Makefile, what do the automatic variables $@, $^, and $< mean?
items.back: $@ = the target being built; $^ = all prerequisites; $< = the first prerequisite. Recipe lines must also be indented with a real TAB, not spaces.

items.prompt: `ldd ./app` prints `libmathutil.so => not found`. Which is the cleanest production fix?
choices: Permanently export LD_LIBRARY_PATH in everyone's shell profile
Install the .so to a standard lib dir and run ldconfig (or link with an rpath)
Recompile app as a static binary every time it runs
items.explanation: Installing into a known directory plus `ldconfig`, or baking an rpath at link time, makes resolution deterministic. `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` is a dev crutch; deleting the cache breaks everything until rebuilt.

items.prompt: What is the primary role of CMake?
choices: It is a C compiler that replaces gcc
It is a meta-build system that generates Makefiles or Ninja build files from CMakeLists.txt
It is the runtime dynamic linker
It is a static-analysis linter for C++
items.explanation: CMake is a generator: from a portable `CMakeLists.txt` it produces native build files (Unix Makefiles, Ninja, IDE projects). It does not compile code itself or load libraries at runtime.

items.prompt: A Makefile recipe fails with 'missing separator'. The most common cause is:
choices: The recipe line is indented with spaces instead of a TAB
The file is named makefile instead of Makefile
items.explanation: make requires recipe lines to begin with a literal tab character. Editors that convert tabs to spaces cause the classic 'missing separator' error.

items.prompt: Both Meson and CMake are commonly configured to emit build files for which fast low-level build tool?
items.explanation: Ninja is a minimal, fast build backend designed to be generated by higher-level tools. Both CMake (`-G Ninja`) and Meson target it. autoconf is legacy autotools; ld.so is the loader; bash is a shell.

lessons.title: Toolchains, Triplets & Cross-Compiling for ARM
lessons.content:
A **toolchain** is the full set of tools that turns source into a runnable binary for some platform: the **compiler** (gcc/clang), **binutils** (`as`, `ld`, `ar`, `objcopy`...), the **C library** (glibc or musl), and the **kernel/userspace headers**. Each platform is named by a **triplet** (really up to four fields): `arch-vendor-os-abi`, e.g. `aarch64-linux-gnu` or `arm-linux-gnueabihf` (the `hf` = hard-float ABI). Three triplets matter: **build** (where you compile), **host** (where the binary runs), and **target** (what a compiler you build will itself emit for). When build == host you are compiling natively; when host differs, you are **cross-compiling**.

Cross-compiling for an ARM SBC on an x86_64 laptop uses a cross-toolchain whose tools are triplet-prefixed:

```bash
sudo apt install gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu
aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc hello.c -o hello-arm
file hello-arm   # ELF 64-bit LSB executable, ARM aarch64
```

For anything linking real libraries you point the toolchain at a **sysroot** -- a tree holding the target's headers and `.so`/`.a` files -- with `--sysroot=`. Build systems take the toolchain via the `CC`/`CXX` variables or the kernel-style `CROSS_COMPILE=aarch64-linux-gnu-` prefix; CMake uses a toolchain file.

You can't run an ARM binary directly on x86, so you have three options: **cross-compile** then copy to real hardware; run it under **QEMU user-mode emulation** (`qemu-aarch64 ./hello-arm`) which translates instructions per-process; or do a slow **native build on the target** itself. Watch the **ABI/libc pitfall**: a binary linked against **glibc** will not run on a **musl**-based system (Alpine) and vice versa -- the dynamic loader path and symbol versions differ -- producing 'No such file or directory' on the loader or missing-symbol errors at runtime.

items.front: What four components make up a toolchain?
items.back: 1) Compiler (gcc/clang), 2) binutils (as, ld, ar, objcopy, etc.), 3) C library (glibc or musl), 4) kernel/userspace headers. Together they turn source into a binary for a given platform.

items.front: Define the build, host, and target triplets.
items.back: Build = the machine you compile on. Host = the machine the produced binary runs on. Target = (for a compiler you are building) the platform that compiler will emit code for. build==host means native; build!=host means cross-compiling.

items.front: Decode the triplet `arm-linux-gnueabihf`.
items.back: arch = arm (32-bit ARM), os/kernel = linux, ABI = gnueabihf, i.e. the GNU EABI with hard-float (hf) -- floating point passed in FPU registers. (aarch64-linux-gnu is the 64-bit counterpart.)

items.front: What is a sysroot in cross-compilation?
items.back: A directory tree containing the TARGET system's headers and libraries (.so/.a). Passed via --sysroot=, it tells the cross-compiler/linker where to find target headers and libs instead of the host's.

items.front: What do the CC and CROSS_COMPILE variables do for a build?
items.back: CC names the compiler binary a build system should invoke (e.g. CC=aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc). CROSS_COMPILE (kernel/Makefile convention) sets the tool prefix, so as/ld/gcc become CROSS_COMPILE+gcc, etc., e.g. CROSS_COMPILE=aarch64-linux-gnu-.
items.hint: One names the compiler, one is a tool prefix.

items.front: Why might a glibc-built binary fail on a musl (Alpine) system?
items.back: They are different C libraries with different dynamic-loader paths and symbol versions. The glibc binary requests /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2 which musl lacks, often giving a confusing 'No such file or directory' on the loader, or missing/incompatible symbols.

items.prompt: You compile on an x86_64 laptop to produce a binary that runs on an aarch64 SBC. What are the build and host?
items.explanation: Build is where compilation happens (x86_64 laptop); host is where the resulting binary runs (aarch64 SBC). Because they differ, this is cross-compilation.

items.prompt: Which command cross-compiles hello.c for 64-bit ARM using a standard Debian cross-toolchain?
items.explanation: The cross-toolchain ships triplet-prefixed tools, so `aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc` is the cross compiler. Plain `gcc -march` still emits host (x86) code; qemu runs binaries, it is not a compiler; cmake is not invoked that way.

items.prompt: You cross-built an aarch64 binary but have no ARM hardware handy. How can you run it on your x86_64 box?
choices: Just run ./hello-arm directly; Linux translates it automatically
Run it under QEMU user-mode: qemu-aarch64 ./hello-arm
Re-link it with -m32 to make it x86
Set LD_LIBRARY_PATH to the ARM sysroot
items.explanation: QEMU user-mode emulation translates the foreign ELF's instructions per-process so a non-native binary runs on the host. The kernel will not run a foreign-arch ELF unaided; -m32 and LD_LIBRARY_PATH do not change the instruction set.

items.prompt: An aarch64 binary built against glibc fails on an Alpine (musl) target with a loader 'No such file or directory' error even though the file exists. The root cause is most likely:
choices: A C library / ABI mismatch -- the requested glibc dynamic loader is absent on musl
The binary was compiled with -O2 instead of -O0
items.explanation: The message refers to the missing dynamic loader the glibc binary names in its INTERP, not the binary itself -- a classic glibc-vs-musl ABI pitfall. Build against musl (or statically) for Alpine targets.

items.front: Cross-compilation: x86_64 host to aarch64 target
items.back: Diagram showing an x86_64 host with a cross-toolchain (aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc) and a sysroot of target libc/headers emitting an ARM ELF binary that runs on an aarch64 SBC or under qemu-aarch64.

lessons.title: A Container Is Not a VM
lessons.content:
The single most important mental correction: **a container is not a lightweight virtual machine.** A VM runs its own *guest kernel* on top of a hypervisor that virtualizes hardware; you can boot Windows on a Linux host because the guest brings its own kernel. A container brings *no kernel at all*. Every container on a host shares the **one host kernel**; what differs is each container's *view* of that kernel.

So what actually isolates a container? Three kernel features you already half-know from earlier modules:

- **Namespaces** give a process its own view of a resource class (PIDs, mounts, network, hostname, IPC, users). Inside, it sees PID 1 and its own `/`; outside, it is just a normal process.
- **cgroups v2** *limit and account* resources — CPU shares, a memory ceiling, IO and PID caps.
- A **layered (union) filesystem** (overlayfs) supplies the root filesystem cheaply by stacking read-only image layers under a thin writable layer.

Memorize the one-line definition you will be tested on:

> **A container = namespaces + cgroups + a layered filesystem over a shared kernel.**

Consequences fall out immediately. A Linux container cannot run a Windows kernel workload — there is no guest kernel. A kernel bug or `sysctl` is *shared* across all containers, so isolation is weaker than a VM's. Boot is near-instant because nothing boots: the runtime just `clone()`s a process into fresh namespaces and `exec`s your binary.

```bash
# Same kernel, host and container:
uname -r            # 6.8.0-host on the host
docker run --rm alpine uname -r   # 6.8.0-host (identical!)
```

items.front: How does a container differ from a virtual machine at the kernel level?
items.back: A VM runs its own guest kernel on a hypervisor that virtualizes hardware. A container has no kernel of its own — all containers share the single host kernel and are isolated only by namespaces, cgroups, and a layered filesystem.

items.front: Give the one-line definition of a container.
items.back: A container = namespaces + cgroups + a layered filesystem, all running over a single shared host kernel.

items.front: What is each primitive's job: namespaces, cgroups, overlayfs?
items.back: Namespaces isolate the *view* of a resource (PID, mount, net, etc.). cgroups *limit and account* resources (CPU, memory, IO, PIDs). overlayfs supplies the root filesystem by layering read-only image layers under a writable layer.

items.front: Why does a container start in milliseconds while a VM takes seconds?
items.back: Nothing boots. There is no guest kernel or firmware to initialize — the runtime just clones a process into new namespaces and execs the entrypoint binary on the already-running host kernel.
items.hint: What does a VM do that a container skips?

items.prompt: Why can you NOT run a genuine Windows application inside a standard Linux container on a Linux host?
choices: Containers cannot allocate enough memory for Windows
The container shares the host's Linux kernel and has no Windows kernel of its own
Docker blocks non-Linux binaries by policy
Windows binaries are too large for overlayfs layers
items.explanation: A container shares the host kernel rather than booting its own. A Windows binary needs the Windows kernel ABI, which a Linux host kernel does not provide — so it cannot run without an actual VM/guest kernel.

items.prompt: Compared with a VM, container isolation is generally considered:
choices: Stronger, because each container has its own kernel
Identical, because both use hardware virtualization
Weaker, because all containers share one kernel and a kernel exploit crosses the boundary
Irrelevant, since containers have no security boundary at all
items.explanation: Sharing one kernel means a kernel-level vulnerability or a misconfigured capability can escape a container, so the isolation boundary is thinner than a VM's hardware-virtualized one.

items.prompt: On a host running kernel 6.8.0-host, what does `docker run --rm alpine uname -r` print?
choices: The Alpine distribution's own kernel version
6.8.0-host — the host kernel, because it is shared
An error, because Alpine ships no kernel
The Docker daemon's internal version string
items.explanation: `uname -r` reports the running kernel. Since the container shares the host kernel, it reports the host's version, not anything from the Alpine image (which contains no kernel).

items.prompt: A process inside a container sees itself as PID 1. From the host's perspective it is:
choices: Also PID 1 — there is only one PID 1 per machine
An ordinary process with some other host PID, viewed through a separate PID namespace
items.explanation: A PID namespace remaps PIDs: the container's PID 1 is just a normal host process with a different host-side PID. Namespaces change the *view*, not the underlying single kernel.

lessons.title: The Primitives: Namespaces, cgroups, Capabilities, Seccomp, overlayfs
lessons.content:
Now go beneath the runtime. Containers are assembled from syscalls (Module 7), so you can build one by hand.

**Namespaces** (created via `clone()`/`unshare()`), one per isolated resource class:
- **PID** — own process-number space; first process becomes PID 1.
- **mount** — own filesystem mount tree.
- **net** — own interfaces, routes, ports (containers talk via veth pairs).
- **UTS** — own hostname/domainname.
- **IPC** — own System V IPC / POSIX message queues.
- **user** — own UID/GID mapping; the basis of *rootless* containers (root inside maps to an unprivileged host UID).
- Plus **cgroup** and **time** namespaces.

**cgroups v2** form a single unified hierarchy under `/sys/fs/cgroup`. Controllers cap and meter `cpu`, `memory`, `io`, and `pids`. This is what enforces `docker run -m 256m`.

**capabilities** split root's power into ~40 bits (`CAP_NET_RAW`, `CAP_SYS_ADMIN`, ...); runtimes drop most by default. **seccomp** filters which syscalls a process may make (the default Docker profile blocks ~44 dangerous calls). **overlayfs** unions read-only `lowerdir` image layers with a writable `upperdir` so copies are cheap and copy-on-write.

```bash
# Make a new PID + mount namespace; your shell becomes PID 1:
sudo unshare --pid --fork --mount-proc bash
ps -ef        # only this shell + ps; you are PID 1

# Inspect a running container's memory ceiling (cgroup v2):
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/.../memory.max
```

That is the whole trick: `unshare`/`clone` for namespaces, write a cgroup file for limits, `pivot_root` onto an overlay mount, drop capabilities, apply a seccomp filter, `exec` the app.

items.front: Name the main Linux namespace types and what each isolates.
items.back: PID (process numbers), mount (filesystem tree), net (interfaces/ports), UTS (hostname), IPC (System V/POSIX IPC), user (UID/GID mapping), plus cgroup and time. Each isolates one resource class' view.
items.hint: PID, mount, net, UTS, IPC, user (+cgroup, time).

items.front: What do cgroups v2 do, and where do they live?
items.back: cgroups v2 limit and account resource usage (cpu, memory, io, pids controllers) in a single unified hierarchy under /sys/fs/cgroup. They enforce limits like `docker run -m 256m`.

items.front: How does the user namespace enable rootless containers?
items.back: A user namespace maps UIDs/GIDs: UID 0 (root) *inside* the container maps to an unprivileged UID on the host. The process feels like root within its namespace but holds no real host privileges.
items.hint: Root inside maps to non-root outside.

items.front: What is the difference between capabilities and seccomp?
items.back: Capabilities split root's authority into ~40 fine-grained bits (e.g. CAP_NET_RAW) that can be granted/dropped. seccomp filters which *syscalls* a process is allowed to invoke. They are complementary defense layers.

items.front: How does overlayfs provide a container's root filesystem?
items.back: It unions read-only image layers (lowerdir) beneath a thin writable layer (upperdir). Reads fall through to lower layers; writes go to upperdir via copy-on-write, so containers share layers and start cheaply.

items.prompt: What does `sudo unshare --pid --fork --mount-proc bash` demonstrate?
choices: It boots a tiny virtual machine with its own kernel
It creates a new PID namespace so the shell becomes PID 1 and `ps` shows only that namespace's processes
It permanently changes the host's PID numbering
It mounts an overlay filesystem for image layers
items.explanation: `unshare --pid --fork` creates a new PID namespace; `--mount-proc` mounts a fresh /proc reflecting it. The forked bash is PID 1 inside and sees only namespace-local processes — a hand-built piece of a container.

items.prompt: Which primitive actually enforces `docker run -m 256m` (a 256 MB memory cap)?
items.explanation: Resource limits are cgroups, not namespaces. The memory controller writes the ceiling to memory.max; the kernel then refuses allocations / OOM-kills the cgroup when exceeded.

items.prompt: At the syscall level, which call creates the new namespaces that make a container?
choices: clone() (or unshare()) with namespace flags such as CLONE_NEWPID
items.explanation: Namespaces are created by passing CLONE_NEW* flags to clone() (for a new process) or unshare() (for the current one). This ties containers directly back to the process/syscall model of Module 7.

items.prompt: By default, a Docker container's process runs with:
choices: Every Linux capability, identical to host root
A reduced capability set with dangerous ones dropped, plus a seccomp profile blocking risky syscalls
No capabilities and no syscalls at all
items.explanation: Runtimes harden by default: they drop most capabilities and apply a seccomp allowlist/denylist (Docker blocks ~44 syscalls). You can add back specific caps with --cap-add when truly needed.

lessons.title: Docker: Images, Layers, Dockerfiles, Networking & Compose
lessons.content:
**Image vs container:** an *image* is an immutable, layered, read-only template (identified by a tag like `nginx:1.27` and an immutable `sha256:` digest). A *container* is a running (or stopped) instance — the image plus a writable overlay layer and a process. One image, many containers.

**Layers & cache:** each Dockerfile instruction that changes the filesystem (`RUN`, `COPY`, `ADD`) creates a layer. Docker caches layers and reuses them if the instruction and its inputs are unchanged. So **order matters**: copy dependency manifests and install deps *before* copying source, or every code edit busts the dependency cache.

```dockerfile
# Multi-stage: heavy builder, tiny runtime
FROM golang:1.22 AS build
WORKDIR /src
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download            # cached unless deps change
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o /app ./cmd/server

FROM gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot   # minimal, no shell
COPY --from=build /app /app
USER nonroot:nonroot           # never run as root
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["/app"]            # the fixed executable
CMD ["--port=8080"]            # default args, overridable
```

`ENTRYPOINT` is the command that always runs; `CMD` supplies default arguments (or a default command) that the user can override at `docker run`. **Multi-stage** keeps compilers out of the shipped image.

**Volumes vs bind mounts:** a named *volume* is Docker-managed persistent storage; a *bind mount* maps a host path straight in. **Networking:** `bridge` (default, private network + `-p 8080:80` port mapping), `host` (shares host netns, no isolation), `none` (no network). **Compose** declares a multi-container app in YAML:

```bash
docker compose up -d        # bring up app + db together
```

items.front: What is the difference between a Docker image and a container?
items.back: An image is an immutable, layered, read-only template (tag + sha256 digest). A container is a runnable instance of an image: the image's layers plus a writable overlay layer and a running process. One image -> many containers.

items.front: Why should you `COPY` dependency manifests and install dependencies before copying application source?
items.back: Each instruction is a cached layer. Copying source first makes any code change invalidate the cache for every later layer — including dependency install. Installing deps first lets that expensive layer stay cached across code edits.

items.front: ENTRYPOINT vs CMD in a Dockerfile?
items.back: ENTRYPOINT is the executable that always runs. CMD supplies default arguments (or a default command) appended to ENTRYPOINT, and is easily overridden by args passed to `docker run`. Common pattern: ENTRYPOINT = the binary, CMD = default flags.
items.hint: Fixed program vs overridable default args.

items.front: What problem do multi-stage builds solve?
items.back: They let a heavy build stage (compilers, dev headers, full toolchain) produce an artifact that is COPY --from into a tiny final stage. The shipped image contains only the runtime artifact, shrinking size and attack surface.

items.front: Volume vs bind mount?
items.back: A named volume is Docker-managed storage (lives in Docker's area, portable, good for persistent data). A bind mount maps a specific host directory into the container (good for dev / host config), tying the container to the host's filesystem layout.

items.prompt: On the default bridge network, what does `-p 8080:80` do in `docker run -p 8080:80 nginx`?
choices: Maps host port 8080 to container port 80
Maps container port 8080 to host port 80
Opens ports 80 through 8080 inside the container
items.explanation: The format is host:container. Traffic to the host's 8080 is forwarded to port 80 inside the container. With host networking there is no mapping — the container uses host ports directly.

items.prompt: Which network mode shares the host's network namespace, giving the container no network isolation?
items.explanation: `--network host` puts the container in the host's net namespace: it sees host interfaces and binds host ports directly with no NAT or port mapping. `bridge` isolates, `none` removes networking.

items.prompt: Given `ENTRYPOINT ["/app"]` and `CMD ["--port=8080"]`, what runs for `docker run img --port=9090`?
choices: /app --port=9090 (the run args replace CMD, appended to ENTRYPOINT)
An error, because you cannot override CMD
items.explanation: Arguments after the image name replace CMD and are appended to ENTRYPOINT. So ENTRYPOINT /app stays fixed and the args become --port=9090, overriding the default.

items.prompt: What is the primary purpose of `docker compose`?
choices: To build a single image faster
To declare and run a multi-container application (e.g. app + database) from one YAML file
To replace the Linux kernel's namespaces
items.explanation: Compose describes multiple services, their networks, and volumes declaratively in YAML so `docker compose up` brings the whole app stack up together. It is single-host orchestration; Kubernetes handles multi-host scale.

lessons.title: Podman, OCI, Best Practices & the Kubernetes Horizon
lessons.content:
**Podman** is a Docker-CLI-compatible engine with a different architecture. Docker uses a long-running **daemon** (`dockerd`) running as root that owns all containers — a single privileged process and a security/availability concern. Podman is **daemonless**: it `fork`/`exec`s each container as a direct child process, so there is no central root daemon. Combined with **user namespaces**, this makes **rootless** containers natural — an ordinary user runs containers where in-container root maps to their unprivileged host UID. Most commands are aliases: `alias docker=podman` usually just works.

```bash
podman run -d --name web -p 8080:80 nginx        # rootless, no daemon
# Ship it as a real systemd service via Quadlet:
# /etc/containers/systemd/web.container  -> generates web.service
systemctl --user start web.service
```

**Quadlet** lets you describe a container in a `.container` unit file; systemd generates and supervises a proper service — the modern replacement for the old `podman generate systemd`.

**OCI** (Open Container Initiative) standardizes the *image format* and *runtime* (`runc`/`crun`), so images built by Docker run under Podman and vice versa — they are interchangeable.

**Best practices** (the ones interviewers probe):
- **Minimal base image** — `distroless`, `alpine`, or scratch: smaller, fewer CVEs, smaller attack surface.
- **Run as non-root** — add a `USER` directive; never default to root.
- **No baked secrets** — secrets in `ENV`/layers persist in the image history; inject at runtime (env, mounts, secret stores).
- Pin by digest, scan images, keep layers few.

**Kubernetes horizon:** a single host runs containers fine, but production scale (scheduling across many nodes, self-healing, rolling updates, service discovery) is **Kubernetes** — it orchestrates OCI containers across a cluster. Know that it exists and consumes these same images; deep K8s is its own topic.

items.front: How does Podman's architecture differ from Docker's?
items.back: Docker uses a long-running root daemon (dockerd) that owns all containers. Podman is daemonless: it fork/execs each container as a direct child process. No central privileged daemon, which improves security and avoids a single point of failure.

items.front: What makes Podman rootless containers possible, and why is it safer?
items.back: User namespaces map in-container root (UID 0) to the invoking user's unprivileged host UID. An ordinary user runs containers with no real host root, so a container escape yields only that user's privileges, not host root.

items.front: What is Quadlet?
items.back: Quadlet lets you declare a Podman container in a systemd .container unit file; systemd generates and supervises the corresponding .service. It is the modern, declarative way to run containers under systemd (replacing `podman generate systemd`).

items.front: What does the OCI standardize, and why does it matter?
items.back: The Open Container Initiative standardizes the container image format and the runtime spec (implemented by runc/crun). Because of OCI, an image built with Docker runs under Podman/Kubernetes and vice versa — tools are interchangeable.

items.front: Why must you never bake secrets into an image with ENV or COPY?
items.back: Image layers are immutable and inspectable: anything in ENV or a COPYed file persists in the image history and is readable by anyone who pulls it, even if a later layer 'removes' it. Inject secrets at runtime (env, mounts, secret stores) instead.

items.prompt: Why does an image built by `docker build` run unchanged under Podman?
choices: Podman secretly bundles the Docker daemon
Both produce and consume OCI-compliant images and use an OCI runtime (runc/crun)
Podman recompiles the image at first run
It does not — images must be rebuilt for each engine
items.explanation: The OCI image and runtime specs make images portable across engines. Docker and Podman both emit/consume OCI images, so the same image runs under either without modification.

items.prompt: What is the main security benefit of a minimal base image like distroless or scratch?
choices: It runs the container with a newer kernel
Fewer packages mean fewer CVEs and a smaller attack surface (often no shell to exploit)
It automatically runs the process as root
items.explanation: Minimal images strip out unneeded packages, package managers, and often the shell. Less software means fewer vulnerabilities and less for an attacker to use after a compromise.

items.prompt: A Dockerfile ends without a USER directive. What is the practical risk?
choices: The process runs as root inside the container, widening impact if the container is compromised
The container cannot open any network ports
items.explanation: Without USER, the default is root. A compromised root process plus any misconfigured capability or shared mount is far more dangerous than an unprivileged one. Add a non-root USER as a best practice.

items.prompt: When does a team typically reach for Kubernetes rather than plain Docker/Podman + compose?
choices: To scale and self-heal containerized workloads across many nodes with scheduling, rolling updates, and service discovery
To replace the Linux kernel namespaces
items.explanation: Compose is single-host. Kubernetes orchestrates the same OCI containers across a cluster: scheduling onto nodes, restarting failed pods, rolling updates, and service discovery — the production scale-out endpoint.

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Side-by-side stacks: VMs run multiple guest kernels over a hypervisor; containers share one host kernel, isolated by namespaces, cgroups, seccomp, capabilities, and overlayfs.

lessons.title: The Block-Device Stack & Partitioning
lessons.content:
Linux storage is a stack of layers, and almost every storage task is just deciding which layer to operate on. From the bottom up: a physical disk (`/dev/sda`, an NVMe namespace `/dev/nvme0n1`, or a loopback file) is a stream of fixed-size sectors (512 B or 4 KiB). On top sits an optional partition table that carves the disk into ranges; on top of partitions you may stack LVM, RAID (`md`), or encryption (`dm-crypt`); finally a *filesystem* turns one of those into named files, and `mount` grafts it into the single unified directory tree.

`lsblk` is your map of this stack. `lsblk -f` adds filesystem type, LABEL, and UUID:

```bash
lsblk -f                 # tree of disks -> parts -> dm/md -> mountpoint
lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,TYPE,FSTYPE,MOUNTPOINTS
sudo blkid               # UUIDs/types from superblocks
cat /proc/partitions     # kernel's raw view
```

**MBR vs GPT.** The old MBR (msdos) scheme lives in the first 512 bytes, caps disks at 2 TiB, and allows only 4 primary partitions (hence "extended/logical" hacks). **GPT** (GUID Partition Table) is the modern default: 128 partitions by default, disks far beyond 2 TiB, a backup table at the end of the disk, and CRC protection. Use GPT unless you have a specific legacy-BIOS reason not to.

Tooling: `fdisk` and `gdisk` are interactive (fdisk now handles both MBR and GPT); `parted` (and scriptable `sgdisk`) are good for automation. The radar-tech instinct applies: a disk is just addressable hardware, and a partition table is a tiny map at the front telling everyone where the regions begin and end. Mislabel that map and the bits underneath are unchanged but suddenly unreadable.

items.front: Name the layers of the Linux block-device stack from raw hardware up to a file you can open.
items.back: Physical disk/sectors -> (optional) partition table -> (optional) LVM / md-RAID / dm-crypt mapping -> filesystem -> mountpoint in the unified directory tree.
items.hint: Disk, partition, mapper layer, filesystem, mount.

items.front: What does `lsblk -f` show that plain `lsblk` does not, and why is it the first command to run?
items.back: It adds FSTYPE, LABEL, UUID, and mountpoint per node, so you see the whole stack (disk -> partition -> dm/md -> mount) with filesystem identity at a glance before touching anything.

items.prompt: Which statement about MBR vs GPT partition tables is correct?
choices: MBR supports disks larger than 2 TiB and 128 partitions by default
GPT caps a disk at 4 primary partitions and 2 TiB
GPT supports >2 TiB disks, 128 partitions by default, and keeps a backup table at the disk's end
MBR stores a CRC-protected backup table at both ends of the disk
items.explanation: GPT is the modern scheme: large disks, 128 partitions by default, CRC-checked primary plus backup tables. MBR is the legacy 512-byte scheme limited to ~2 TiB and 4 primary partitions.

items.prompt: You want a scriptable, non-interactive way to partition a disk in an automation pipeline. Best fit?
choices: Interactive `fdisk` with a human at the keyboard
items.explanation: `parted` and `sgdisk` accept full operations on the command line, making them suitable for scripts. `fdisk` is interactive, mkfs makes a filesystem (not a partition table), and /proc/partitions is read-only kernel state.

items.front: Why can you stack LVM, md-RAID, or dm-crypt either on whole disks OR on partitions?
items.back: Each is just a device-mapper/kernel layer that consumes a block device and presents a new one. A partition is itself a block device, so any layer can sit on a whole disk or on a partition of it.
items.hint: Everything in the stack is a block device feeding the next layer.

items.front: Where does an MBR live physically, and what is the practical consequence of that location?
items.back: In the first 512-byte sector (LBA 0). It is tiny and singular, so it has no redundancy and a single bad write to sector 0 corrupts the whole partition map.

items.prompt: `lsblk` shows `nvme0n1` with children `nvme0n1p1` and `nvme0n1p2`. What are the children?
items.explanation: The `p1`/`p2` suffix denotes partitions of the NVMe namespace `nvme0n1`. (SATA disks use sda1/sda2 without the 'p'.)

items.prompt: Which command reports the UUID and filesystem type read directly from on-disk superblocks?
items.explanation: `blkid` (and `lsblk -f`) read identifying metadata (UUID, LABEL, TYPE) from superblocks. `df` reports mounted-FS usage, `du` reports directory usage, `free` reports memory.

items.front: What is a loopback (loop) device and why is it perfect for practicing storage?
items.back: A regular file presented as a block device via `losetup` (e.g. `losetup -f --show disk.img`). You can partition it, mkfs it, build LVM/RAID on it, and mount it exactly like a real disk -- with zero hardware risk.

lessons.title: Filesystems: Journaling vs Copy-on-Write, mkfs & Mounting
lessons.content:
A filesystem is the bookkeeping that turns a flat block device into files, directories, and metadata. The split that matters today is **journaling vs copy-on-write (CoW)**.

**Journaling (ext4, XFS).** Before changing the live structures, the FS writes its intent to a journal, then replays or discards it after a crash so the FS stays consistent. ext4 has three modes set via `data=`: `ordered` (default -- journals metadata, but forces data blocks to disk before the metadata commit), `writeback` (journals metadata only, fastest, data may lag -> stale blocks after crash), and `journal` (journals data too -- safest, slowest). XFS journals metadata only, scales superbly to huge files and parallel I/O, and **cannot shrink** -- only grow.

**Copy-on-write (Btrfs, ZFS).** CoW never overwrites live data; it writes new blocks and atomically flips pointers. This gives nearly free **snapshots**, **checksums** on data+metadata (detecting silent corruption/bit-rot -- a real concern your radar background will appreciate), built-in multi-device/RAID, and compression. The cost is fragmentation and more tuning.

Special filesystems: **tmpfs** is RAM-backed (`/tmp`, `/run`) -- fast and volatile. **overlayfs** unions a read-only lower dir with a writable upper dir and is the backbone of container images (Module 14).

```bash
sudo mkfs.ext4 -L data /dev/vg0/lv_data
sudo mkfs.xfs  /dev/vg0/lv_data
sudo fsck.ext4 -f /dev/sdb1     # NEVER fsck a mounted rw fs
sudo tune2fs -l /dev/sdb1       # ext4 superblock + UUID
```

Rule: `fsck` only on an unmounted (or read-only) filesystem; checking a live read-write FS can corrupt it. XFS uses `xfs_repair` instead, only when unmounted.

items.front: Explain journaling vs copy-on-write in one sentence each.
items.back: Journaling: record the intended change in a journal first, then apply it, so a crash leaves the FS consistent (replay/rollback). Copy-on-write: never overwrite live blocks -- write new ones and atomically swap pointers, which makes snapshots and checksums cheap.

items.prompt: ext4 is mounted with `data=writeback`. After a crash, what is the risk relative to the default `data=ordered`?
choices: No risk; writeback is fully data-journaled
Metadata may point at file blocks that were never written, exposing stale/garbage data
The whole partition table is rewritten
items.explanation: `writeback` journals only metadata and does not order data writes before metadata commit, so after a crash a file's metadata can reference blocks whose data never hit disk -- you may read stale content. `ordered` forces data out before the metadata commit; `journal` logs data too.

items.front: Which two desirable properties fall out 'for free' from copy-on-write, and why?
items.back: Snapshots (just keep the old block pointers around -- nothing was overwritten) and data checksums (every write is new, so a checksum can be computed and verified, catching silent bit-rot).
items.hint: Old pointers survive; new writes get checksummed.

items.prompt: A teammate runs `fsck` on a mounted, read-write ext4 root filesystem. What is the danger?
choices: None -- fsck is always safe online
It only prints a warning and exits with no effect
The kernel and fsck have inconsistent views of the FS, so fsck's 'repairs' can corrupt live data
It silently converts the FS to XFS
items.explanation: fsck assumes exclusive access. On a live rw filesystem the kernel is still mutating structures, so fsck's idea of what is correct is stale and its writes can corrupt the FS. Run it only when unmounted or mounted read-only.

items.front: When would you pick XFS, and what is its notable limitation?
items.back: Pick XFS for large files and high-parallelism workloads (big databases, media, many cores) where its allocation and scalability shine. Limitation: it can be grown (`xfs_growfs`) but NEVER shrunk.

items.prompt: Why is overlayfs central to container images?
choices: It encrypts every layer of the image
It unions a read-only lower directory with a writable upper directory, so containers share immutable base layers and write only their diffs
It is the only filesystem that supports UUIDs
It stores everything in RAM and loses it on reboot
items.explanation: overlayfs merges a read-only lower dir (shared base image layers) with a writable upper dir (container's changes), enabling layer sharing and copy-on-write semantics at the directory level. tmpfs is the RAM-backed, volatile one.

items.front: What is tmpfs and what is the consequence of putting /tmp or build artifacts on it?
items.back: A RAM-backed filesystem (may spill to swap). It is very fast but volatile -- contents vanish on reboot/unmount and consume RAM. Great for scratch/caches, wrong for anything you must keep.
items.hint: RAM filesystem -> fast but gone on reboot.

items.prompt: Which command set correctly creates an ext4 filesystem with label `data` and then inspects its superblock/UUID?
items.explanation: `mkfs.ext4 -L data` formats and labels; `tune2fs -l` dumps the ext4 superblock including the UUID and label. The other options confuse mounting, partitioning, and LV resizing with formatting/inspection.

items.front: Data-integrity angle: how does a CoW filesystem like ZFS/Btrfs catch silent bit-rot that ext4/XFS cannot?
items.back: It stores checksums of data and metadata and verifies them on every read; a mismatch flags corruption (and with redundancy can self-heal from a good copy). Traditional journaling FSs only protect consistency of structure, not the correctness of stored bytes.
items.hint: Checksums verified on read, not just a consistency journal.

lessons.title: Mounting, /etc/fstab, UUIDs & systemd Mount Units
lessons.content:
Mounting attaches a filesystem to a point in the tree. Doing it by hand is `mount /dev/vg0/lv_data /data`; making it survive reboot is `/etc/fstab` (or a systemd mount unit).

**Why UUIDs, not /dev/sdX.** Kernel device names are *not stable*: add a disk, change a controller, or boot with USB attached and yesterday's `/dev/sdb` is today's `/dev/sdc`. An fstab entry keyed on `/dev/sdb1` can then mount the wrong filesystem -- or fail to boot. UUIDs (or LABELs) name the *filesystem itself*, so they follow the data regardless of enumeration order. Get them with `lsblk -f` or `blkid`.

**fstab fields (six, space-separated):** `<spec> <mountpoint> <fstype> <options> <dump> <pass>`.

```bash
# /etc/fstab
UUID=4d2a-... /data ext4 defaults,noatime,nosuid 0 2
```

Key options: `noatime` (don't write an access timestamp on every read -- a real I/O win), `ro` (read-only -- protects reference data and forensic images), `nosuid`/`nodev`/`noexec` (harden untrusted mounts), `defaults` (rw,suid,dev,exec,auto,nouser,async). The 5th field is dump (almost always 0); the 6th is fsck order: `1` for root, `2` for other ext-family FSs, `0` to skip. Validate without rebooting: `sudo mount -a` then `findmnt --verify`.

**systemd mount units.** systemd generates a `.mount` unit per fstab line, but you can also write `data.mount` directly (the filename must be the escaped mountpoint, e.g. `data.mount` for `/data`). Use `x-systemd.automount` for mount-on-first-access, and `nofail`/`x-systemd.device-timeout=` so a missing device doesn't block boot. `findmnt` shows the live mount tree.

items.front: Why should fstab entries use UUID= (or LABEL=) instead of /dev/sdX?
items.back: Kernel device names depend on enumeration order and can change when hardware is added/moved, so /dev/sdb1 may later point at a different disk. A UUID names the filesystem itself, so it always follows the correct data -- preventing wrong-filesystem mounts or boot failures.
items.hint: Device names drift; UUIDs are stable identifiers.

items.prompt: What are the six fields of an /etc/fstab line, in order?
choices: mountpoint, spec, options, fstype, pass, dump
spec, mountpoint, fstype, options, dump, pass
fstype, spec, mountpoint, dump, options, pass
spec, fstype, options, mountpoint, pass, dump
items.explanation: Order is: spec (UUID/LABEL/device), mountpoint, filesystem type, mount options, dump flag, and fsck pass number.

items.front: What does the mount option `noatime` do and why is it commonly used?
items.back: It suppresses updating a file's access-time metadata on every read. That eliminates a metadata write per read, cutting I/O and wear -- especially valuable on read-heavy workloads and flash/SSD.

items.prompt: You add a new data disk's fstab entry and want to confirm it is correct WITHOUT rebooting into a possibly broken boot. Best approach?
choices: Reboot immediately and watch the console
`sudo mount -a` then `findmnt --verify` to test the entries live
Run `fsck` on the running root filesystem
items.explanation: `mount -a` mounts everything in fstab (surfacing typos), and `findmnt --verify` checks the file for problems. This catches mistakes before a reboot can leave you at an emergency prompt.

items.front: What is the purpose of the 6th fstab field (fsck pass), and what values are typical?
items.back: It sets boot-time fsck order: 1 for the root filesystem (checked first), 2 for other ext-family filesystems (checked after root, in parallel across disks), and 0 to skip the check (used for swap, network mounts, XFS/Btrfs which self-check).

items.prompt: A removable/secondary disk may be absent at boot. Which fstab option prevents a missing device from dropping the system into emergency mode?
items.explanation: `nofail` tells systemd not to fail the boot if the device is missing; pairing it with `x-systemd.device-timeout=` bounds how long boot waits. noatime/ro/nosuid affect behavior, not the missing-device boot blocking.

items.prompt: Which set of options best hardens an automounted USB stick containing untrusted files?
items.explanation: `ro` blocks writes; `nosuid` ignores setuid bits; `nodev` ignores device nodes; `noexec` blocks running binaries -- the standard hardening set for untrusted media. The others permit execution and privilege escalation vectors.

items.front: How does systemd relate to /etc/fstab, and what does x-systemd.automount add?
items.back: systemd's generator turns each fstab line into a .mount unit at boot. The `x-systemd.automount` option makes the filesystem mount lazily on first access (via an .automount unit) instead of eagerly at boot -- useful for slow or rarely used mounts.
items.hint: fstab -> generated .mount units; automount = mount on first access.

items.front: Which command displays the current live mount tree (and can verify fstab), replacing eyeballing /proc/mounts?
items.back: `findmnt` -- it shows the hierarchical mount tree with sources, types, and options; `findmnt --verify` sanity-checks /etc/fstab.

lessons.title: LVM, Online Resize, RAID, Swap & Encryption
lessons.content:
**LVM** inserts a flexible abstraction between disks and filesystems. Physical disks/partitions become **PVs** (`pvcreate`); PVs pool into a **VG** (`vgcreate vg0 ...`), which is just a bag of fixed-size *physical extents*; you carve **LVs** out of that pool (`lvcreate -L 20G -n lv_data vg0`). Because the VG is a pool, you can grow an LV by handing it free extents from any PV -- no repartitioning, no downtime.

**Growing an LV and its filesystem online -- the headline skill:**

```bash
sudo lvextend -L +10G /dev/vg0/lv_data    # add space to the LV
sudo resize2fs /dev/vg0/lv_data           # ext4: grow FS to fill it
# or, for XFS (must be mounted):
sudo xfs_growfs /data
# shortcut: lvextend -r resizes the FS for you
```

Order matters: grow the LV first, then the filesystem. (Shrinking is the reverse and is risky -- shrink the FS first, and XFS can't shrink at all.)

**Snapshots** are CoW: `lvcreate -s -L 2G -n snap0 /dev/vg0/lv_data` freezes a point-in-time view for backups; **thin provisioning** (thin pools) lets you allocate LVs larger than the backing store, consuming extents only on write -- powerful but you must monitor the pool or writes fail when it fills.

**RAID (mdadm):** 0 = stripe (speed, zero redundancy), 1 = mirror (survive one disk), 5 = stripe+parity (n-1 capacity, survive one disk), 10 = mirrored stripes (speed + redundancy). **Swap:** a partition or file (`mkswap`, `swapon`); `vm.swappiness` (0-100, default ~60) tunes how eagerly the kernel swaps. **LUKS/dm-crypt** gives block-level encryption: `cryptsetup luksFormat`, then `luksOpen` exposes a decrypted mapper device you put a FS or PV on.

Monitoring: `df -h` (free space), `du -sh *` (what's using it), `iostat -xz 1` (per-device I/O), `smartctl -a /dev/sda` (drive health/SMART). **The cardinal danger:** `dd`/`mkfs`/`wipefs` take a target device and do not ask twice -- `dd if=img of=/dev/sda` to the wrong disk instantly destroys it. Always confirm the device with `lsblk` first, and prefer UUIDs and `lsblk -f` over guessed `/dev/sdX` names.

items.front: Define PV, VG, and LV in the LVM model.
items.back: PV (Physical Volume): a disk/partition tagged for LVM and sliced into physical extents. VG (Volume Group): a pool of extents aggregated from one or more PVs. LV (Logical Volume): a virtual block device carved from the VG's extents, on which you put a filesystem.

items.prompt: You must grow `/data` (ext4 on /dev/vg0/lv_data) on a live system with no downtime. Correct sequence?
items.explanation: Enlarge the container (LV) first with lvextend, then grow the filesystem into the new space with resize2fs (ext4 grows online). Doing resize2fs first has no room to grow into; mkfs would destroy the data; lvreduce shrinks.

items.front: Why does the VG-as-extent-pool design make online growth possible at all?
items.back: An LV is just a mapping over physical extents in the VG. Growing it means assigning more free extents from the pool and updating the map -- a metadata change with no data movement -- so the LV (and an online-growable FS like ext4/XFS) can expand while mounted.
items.hint: Growth = handing out more extents + remap, no copy.

items.prompt: What characterizes an LVM thin pool, and what operational duty does it impose?
choices: It mirrors data across two disks automatically; no monitoring needed
It lets you provision LVs whose total size exceeds the backing storage, allocating extents only on write -- so you must monitor pool usage or writes fail when it fills
It encrypts the volume group transparently
items.explanation: Thin provisioning overcommits: LVs can sum to more than physical space, with extents consumed lazily on write. If the pool fills, writes to thin volumes fail or freeze -- so monitoring/alerting on pool usage is mandatory.

items.prompt: Match the mdadm RAID level to its property: which is correct?
choices: RAID 0 survives a single disk failure
RAID 5 stripes with distributed parity, gives ~n-1 usable capacity, and survives one disk failure
RAID 1 stripes data with no redundancy for maximum speed
items.explanation: RAID 5 = striping plus distributed parity: usable capacity ~ (n-1) disks, tolerates one failed disk. RAID 0 is a no-redundancy stripe, RAID 1 is a mirror, RAID 10 is mirrored stripes (speed + redundancy).

items.front: How is an LVM snapshot created, what makes it cheap, and what is the gotcha?
items.back: `lvcreate -s -L <size> -n snap0 /dev/vg0/lv_data` creates a CoW snapshot: only blocks that change after creation are copied, so it starts near-free. Gotcha: a classic (non-thin) snapshot has a fixed size and is dropped/invalidated if too many writes overflow it -- size it for the expected churn.
items.hint: CoW snapshot; size it for write churn or it drops.

items.front: What is vm.swappiness and what do low vs high values mean?
items.back: A 0-100 knob (default ~60) controlling how aggressively the kernel reclaims anonymous memory to swap vs reclaiming page cache. Low (e.g. 10) = avoid swapping, keep processes in RAM; high = swap more readily, favoring page cache. Tune low for latency-sensitive/large-RAM hosts.
items.hint: Higher = swap sooner; lower = keep RAM resident.

items.prompt: What is the single biggest irreversible danger when imaging or formatting devices, and how do you avoid it?
choices: `dd`/`mkfs` ask for confirmation, so mistakes are recoverable
Writing to the wrong /dev/sdX (e.g. `dd of=/dev/sda` instead of /dev/sdb) instantly destroys that disk; verify with `lsblk -f` first
Using UUIDs in fstab can wipe a disk
items.explanation: `dd`, `mkfs`, and `wipefs` write to the named device with no second prompt; aiming at the wrong /dev/sdX overwrites a live disk irrecoverably. Always confirm the target with `lsblk -f`/`blkid` before writing. df is read-only.

items.front: Outline the LUKS/dm-crypt workflow to get an encrypted, usable block device.
items.back: `cryptsetup luksFormat /dev/sdb1` (set up the LUKS header + passphrase), then `cryptsetup luksOpen /dev/sdb1 cryptdata` exposes a decrypted device at /dev/mapper/cryptdata. You then mkfs/mount it (or use it as an LVM PV). luksClose locks it again.

items.prompt: A volume is slow and you suspect either a failing disk or saturated I/O. Which pair of tools targets those two questions?
choices: `du` for disk health, `df` for I/O latency
`smartctl -a /dev/sda` for drive health/SMART, `iostat -xz 1` for per-device I/O load and latency
items.explanation: `smartctl` reads SMART attributes (reallocated sectors, errors, health) to assess a failing drive; `iostat -xz 1` shows per-device utilization, queue, and await to spot I/O saturation. The others measure space or memory, not health/throughput.

items.front: Diagram: the LVM stack from physical disks up to mounted filesystem, including where snapshots and online growth fit.
items.back: Disks/partitions -> pvcreate -> PVs -> vgcreate -> VG (extent pool) -> lvcreate -> LVs (lv_root, lv_data, snapshot) -> mkfs -> filesystem -> mount. lvextend hands free VG extents to an LV, then resize2fs/xfs_growfs grows the FS online; snapshots are CoW LVs off the same VG.

lessons.title: Inspecting the Stack with iproute2 and ss
lessons.content:
Forget `ifconfig`, `route`, and `netstat` — they are deprecated, often not even installed on modern distros, and they hide things (e.g. multiple IPs per interface, policy routing). The `iproute2` suite is the single front door to the kernel's networking, and `ss` replaces `netstat` for sockets. Learn these four verbs reflexively.

```bash
ip addr show          # IPs per interface (alias: ip a)
ip -br addr           # brief, one line per iface
ip link show          # L2 devices, MAC, MTU, up/down state
ip route show         # the routing table (default gw, subnets)
ip neigh show         # the ARP/ND cache (L2<->L3 mappings)
```

For sockets and who owns them, `ss` is faster than `netstat` because it reads kernel structures directly:

```bash
ss -tulpn             # TCP+UDP, listening, numeric, with PID/program
ss -tp state established   # active TCP connections + process
lsof -i :8080         # which process holds port 8080
lsof -i -nP           # all network files, no name/port resolution
```

The mental model: `ip link` is layer 2 (the wire/MAC), `ip addr` is layer 3 (IP), `ip route` decides where a packet goes, and `ip neigh` is the cache that turns the next-hop IP into a MAC. `ss`/`lsof` answer "what is talking, and which process is it." Coming from radar, this is your signal chain made inspectable: link state, address assignment, routing decision, and the active connections all visible from the command line.

items.front: Which modern command replaces all of `ifconfig`, `route`, and `arp`?
items.back: The `ip` command from the `iproute2` suite: `ip addr` (was ifconfig), `ip route` (was route), `ip neigh` (was arp). `ifconfig`/`route`/`netstat` are deprecated and may be absent.

items.front: What does `ss -tulpn` show, and why prefer it over `netstat`?
items.back: TCP+UDP listening sockets, numeric addresses/ports, and the owning PID/program. `ss` reads kernel socket structures directly so it is faster and the modern replacement for `netstat`.

items.front: What lives in `ip neigh`?
items.back: The neighbor cache: IPv4 ARP and IPv6 NDP entries mapping next-hop IP addresses to MAC addresses on the local link — the L3-to-L2 lookup table.

items.front: Which `ip` subcommand operates at layer 2 versus layer 3?
items.back: `ip link` = layer 2 (device state, MAC, MTU). `ip addr` = layer 3 (IP address assignment). One interface can hold several addresses, which `ifconfig` hid.

items.front: How do you find which process is listening on TCP port 8080?
items.back: `ss -tlpn 'sport = :8080'` or `lsof -i :8080`. Both map the socket to a PID/program; use `-nP` on lsof to skip name/port resolution.
items.hint: ss with a filter, or lsof -i.

items.prompt: You run `ifconfig` on a fresh Debian/Ubuntu container and get 'command not found.' What is the correct response?
choices: Install net-tools; ifconfig is the standard tool
Use `ip addr` / `ip link` — ifconfig is deprecated and net-tools is often not installed
The network stack is broken and must be reconfigured
Reboot, since networking failed to initialize
items.explanation: `ifconfig` ships in the legacy `net-tools` package, which is deprecated and increasingly absent from minimal images. The supported tool is `ip` from iproute2; reach for it reflexively instead of reinstalling old tools.

items.prompt: Which command shows the default gateway the kernel will use for off-subnet traffic?
items.explanation: `ip route show` prints the routing table, including the `default via <gw>` entry. `addr` shows IPs, `link` shows L2 device state, and `neigh` shows the ARP/ND cache.

items.prompt: Why can `ip addr` show information that `ifconfig` simply could not?
choices: ifconfig could not display IPv6 at all under any circumstances
ifconfig assumed one primary IP per interface and hid additional addresses and policy details
ifconfig required root while ip does not
ip queries a different kernel than ifconfig
items.explanation: The legacy ifconfig model assumed a single primary address per interface and could not cleanly represent multiple addresses, scopes, and modern policy routing — a key reason iproute2 superseded it.

lessons.title: Configuration Layers and the DNS Resolution Path
lessons.content:
`ip` commands are runtime and vanish on reboot. Persistent configuration is owned by one of several competing managers — know which one your box uses before editing files by hand:

- **NetworkManager** (`nmcli`): desktops, laptops, many servers (RHEL family).
- **systemd-networkd** (`networkctl`, `/etc/systemd/network/*.network`): minimal/server/container hosts.
- **netplan** (`/etc/netplan/*.yaml`, `netplan apply`): Ubuntu's YAML front end that renders to networkd or NetworkManager.

```bash
nmcli device status            # NetworkManager view
networkctl list                # systemd-networkd view
netplan get                    # Ubuntu's merged config
```

The **DNS resolution path** is a separate, layered question — a name lookup walks `/etc/nsswitch.conf` (the `hosts:` line picks order, e.g. `files dns`), then `/etc/hosts` (static overrides), then the resolver in `/etc/resolv.conf`. On systemd-resolved hosts, `/etc/resolv.conf` is a symlink to a stub (`127.0.0.53`); query the real state with `resolvectl`:

```bash
resolvectl status              # per-link DNS servers + DNSSEC
resolvectl query example.com   # use the system resolver path
getent hosts example.com       # honors nsswitch, not just DNS
```

The trap: editing `/etc/resolv.conf` directly when it is a managed symlink — your change gets overwritten. Find the owning layer first. `getent hosts` is the honest test because it follows `nsswitch` exactly the way real programs do, whereas `dig`/`nslookup` talk straight to a DNS server and skip `/etc/hosts`.

items.front: Name the three competing modern network configuration layers and their CLIs.
items.back: NetworkManager (`nmcli`), systemd-networkd (`networkctl`, .network files), and netplan (Ubuntu YAML, `netplan apply`) which renders to one of the other two.
items.hint: Desktop, server/minimal, and Ubuntu's YAML wrapper.

items.front: What does `/etc/nsswitch.conf` control for name lookups?
items.back: The source order for resolution. The `hosts:` line (e.g. `files dns`) tells glibc to check `/etc/hosts` first, then DNS — so it governs whether static entries win over the resolver.
items.hint: Name Service Switch — ordering of sources.

items.front: On a systemd-resolved system, what is `/etc/resolv.conf` typically?
items.back: A symlink to a stub file pointing at the local resolver `127.0.0.53`. Editing it directly is futile — it is regenerated. Use `resolvectl` to inspect/configure real per-link DNS.

items.front: Why use `getent hosts <name>` instead of `dig` to debug resolution?
items.back: `getent hosts` follows `/etc/nsswitch.conf` exactly like real applications (files then DNS), so it reflects what programs actually see. `dig`/`nslookup` query a DNS server directly and ignore `/etc/hosts`.

items.front: Why is editing `/etc/resolv.conf` by hand often a mistake?
items.back: It is frequently a managed symlink or auto-generated by systemd-resolved, NetworkManager, or DHCP, so manual edits get overwritten. Configure DNS at the owning layer instead.
items.hint: Find who owns the file first.

items.prompt: On Ubuntu, you edit `/etc/netplan/01-netcfg.yaml`. What makes the change take effect?
choices: `netplan apply`, which renders the YAML to the active backend (networkd or NetworkManager)
`ip addr add`, since netplan is read-only
items.explanation: netplan is a declarative front end. `netplan apply` generates the backend config (systemd-networkd or NetworkManager) and reloads it. No reboot is needed.

items.prompt: A name in `/etc/hosts` resolves but `dig` returns NXDOMAIN. Why?
choices: dig queries DNS directly and bypasses nsswitch/`/etc/hosts`
items.explanation: `dig` talks straight to a DNS server and does not consult `/etc/hosts` or nsswitch. Static host entries only show up through the glibc resolver path — use `getent hosts` to see them.

items.prompt: Which tool shows per-link DNS servers and DNSSEC status on a systemd-resolved host?
items.explanation: `resolvectl status` reports the actual resolver state systemd-resolved is using per interface, including DNS servers and DNSSEC mode. The stub `/etc/resolv.conf` only shows 127.0.0.53.

lessons.title: Network Namespaces and veth Pairs — the Container Primitive
lessons.content:
A **network namespace** is an isolated copy of the entire network stack: its own interfaces, routing table, ARP/ND cache, and firewall rules. This is the kernel mechanism containers use — a Docker/Podman container is (in part) just a process living in its own netns. A **veth pair** is a virtual cable: two linked interfaces where a packet entering one end exits the other. Put one end in each namespace and you have wired them together.

```bash
ip netns add red
ip netns add blue
ip link add veth-r type veth peer name veth-b
ip link set veth-r netns red
ip link set veth-b netns blue
# configure each end inside its namespace
ip -n red  addr add 10.0.0.1/24 dev veth-r
ip -n blue addr add 10.0.0.2/24 dev veth-b
ip -n red  link set veth-r up
ip -n blue link set veth-b up
ip netns exec red ping -c1 10.0.0.2   # ping across the pair
```

`ip -n red ...` is shorthand for `ip netns exec red ip ...`. To connect more than two namespaces, replace the point-to-point cable with a **Linux bridge** (`ip link add br0 type bridge`) and attach each veth's host end to it — which is exactly the `docker0` bridge model. This single exercise is the from-scratch version of container networking: namespaces for isolation, veth pairs for connectivity, a bridge for the shared LAN, and (next lesson) NAT to reach the outside. Build it once by hand and container networking stops being magic.

items.front: What does a Linux network namespace isolate?
items.back: An entire independent network stack: its own interfaces, routing table, neighbor (ARP/ND) cache, and netfilter/firewall rules. It is the kernel primitive behind container networking.
items.hint: A private copy of the whole stack.

items.front: What is a veth pair and how does it behave?
items.back: A virtual Ethernet cable: two linked interfaces created together where a frame sent into one end emerges from the other. Used to connect a namespace (container) to the host or a bridge.
items.hint: Created in pairs; a virtual patch cable.

items.front: What does `ip -n red <cmd>` do?
items.back: Runs the `ip` command inside the `red` network namespace — shorthand for `ip netns exec red ip <cmd>`. Lets you configure/inspect a namespace's stack from the host.

items.front: How is a Docker/Podman container related to network namespaces and veth?
items.back: Each container runs in its own network namespace; one end of a veth pair lives inside it and the other attaches to a host bridge (e.g. docker0). That is the actual container networking model.

items.front: How do you connect three or more namespaces on one shared LAN?
items.back: Create a Linux bridge (`ip link add br0 type bridge`) and attach the host end of each namespace's veth to it. The bridge acts as a virtual switch — the docker0 pattern.
items.hint: Replace the cable with a virtual switch.

items.front: How do you run a command, like ping, inside a network namespace called red?
items.back: `ip netns exec red ping -c1 10.0.0.2`. `ip netns exec <name> <cmd>` enters the namespace and runs the command with that isolated stack.

items.prompt: You create a veth pair and put each end in a separate namespace but ping fails. Most likely cause?
choices: The interfaces are still DOWN and/or have no IP assigned in their namespace
Namespaces block ICMP by default and cannot be changed
You must reboot to register a new veth
items.explanation: Moving a veth end into a namespace does not bring it up or address it. You must `ip ... addr add` and `ip ... link set <dev> up` inside each namespace (and bring up `lo` if needed) before traffic flows.

items.prompt: Why are veth pairs central to container networking?
choices: They encrypt container traffic by default
They provide a virtual cable linking a container's namespace to the host/bridge, giving the container connectivity
They replace the need for a routing table
They are required to share the host's IP directly
items.explanation: A container lives in its own network namespace; a veth pair bridges that isolated stack to the host (typically via a bridge like docker0), which is how packets get in and out. veth + netns + bridge is the core model.

items.prompt: What does a Linux bridge act as when host ends of several veth pairs are attached to it?
choices: A layer-3 router that NATs each namespace
A virtual layer-2 switch forwarding frames among the attached interfaces
A DNS resolver for the namespaces
A firewall that isolates each namespace
items.explanation: A Linux bridge is a software L2 switch: it learns MACs and forwards frames between its ports. Attaching veth host ends to it puts those namespaces on one virtual LAN — the docker0 design.

lessons.title: Firewalling with nftables, Routing, and Diagnostics
lessons.content:
Beneath every Linux firewall is **netfilter**, the kernel's packet-processing framework. `iptables` is the legacy front end; **nftables** (`nft`) is the modern replacement — one unified tool with explicit **tables -> chains -> rules**, better performance, and IPv4/IPv6 together. Higher-level wrappers (`ufw`, `firewalld`) generate rules for you, but knowing `nft` is expected.

```bash
nft add table inet filter
nft 'add chain inet filter input { type filter hook input priority 0; policy drop; }'
nft add rule inet filter input ct state established,related accept
nft add rule inet filter input iif lo accept
nft add rule inet filter input tcp dport 22 accept   # SSH only
nft list ruleset                                      # show everything
```

That is a basic **stateful** firewall: allow return traffic, allow loopback, allow SSH, drop the rest. **NAT** is just another chain with a `nat` hook (`masquerade` for outbound). To make the box a **router**, enable forwarding: `sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1`. Related building blocks: **bridges** (virtual switch), **VLANs** (`ip link add link eth0 name eth0.10 type vlan id 10`), and **bonding/teaming** for link aggregation.

Diagnostics, in order of depth:

```bash
ping host            # reachability + RTT
mtr host             # continuous traceroute + loss per hop
tcpdump -ni eth0 port 53   # capture packets on the wire
ethtool eth0         # link speed, duplex, driver, offloads
```

Hand-off to topic 06: Linux gives you the interfaces, sockets, routing table, and firewall; topic 06 explains TCP/IP, routing protocols, and what is actually on the wire that `tcpdump` shows you.

items.front: What framework sits beneath both iptables and nftables?
items.back: netfilter — the kernel's packet-filtering/NAT framework with hooks in the network path. `iptables` (legacy) and `nftables` (modern `nft`) are both front ends that program netfilter.
items.hint: The kernel layer, not the userspace tool.

items.front: Describe the nftables hierarchy.
items.back: tables -> chains -> rules. A table (e.g. `inet filter`) holds chains; a chain is attached to a hook with a priority and policy; rules inside match traffic and take actions (accept/drop/etc.).

items.front: What makes a firewall rule 'stateful', and how is it expressed in nft?
items.back: It tracks connection state via conntrack. `ct state established,related accept` admits return traffic for connections you initiated, so you only need to explicitly allow new inbound services.

items.front: What single sysctl turns a Linux box into a router?
items.back: `net.ipv4.ip_forward=1` (and `net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding=1` for IPv6). Set with `sysctl -w` at runtime or in `/etc/sysctl.d/` to persist. It lets the kernel forward packets between interfaces.

items.front: How do nftables NAT and masquerade fit in?
items.back: NAT is a chain attached to the `nat` hook. `masquerade` rewrites outbound source addresses to the egress interface's IP — the standard way a Linux router/container host shares one public IP.
items.hint: A nat-hook chain; masquerade on egress.

items.front: What do `ufw` and `firewalld` actually do?
items.back: They are higher-level front ends that generate underlying nftables/iptables rules from simpler commands or zones. Convenient, but the rules they emit are still netfilter — know `nft` underneath.
items.hint: Wrappers that emit the real rules.

items.prompt: You want a firewall that allows SSH and drops all other new inbound traffic. Which chain setup is correct?
choices: An input chain with policy accept and a single drop rule for port 22
An input chain with policy drop, plus accept rules for established/related, loopback, and tcp dport 22
A forward chain with masquerade on port 22
A nat chain that redirects all ports to 22
items.explanation: Default-deny is the safe posture: set the input chain policy to drop, then explicitly accept established/related (return traffic), loopback, and new SSH (tcp dport 22). Everything else falls through to the drop policy.

items.prompt: A host has two NICs on different subnets but won't pass traffic between them even with correct routes. What is missing?
choices: A VLAN tag on each interface
A bonding interface joining the NICs
items.explanation: By default the kernel does not forward packets between interfaces (it acts as a host, not a router). Setting net.ipv4.ip_forward=1 enables routing; correct routes and firewall forward rules must also be present.

items.prompt: You suspect intermittent packet loss to a remote host across several hops. Which tool best shows per-hop loss over time?
items.explanation: `mtr` combines traceroute and ping, continuously probing each hop and reporting loss and latency per hop — ideal for localizing intermittent loss. `ping` tests only the endpoint; `ethtool` reports local link state; `ss` shows sockets.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the hand-off from this Linux topic to topic 06 (networking protocols)?
choices: Topic 06 replaces iproute2 with protocol-specific tools
Linux provides the interfaces, sockets, routing table, and firewall; topic 06 explains TCP/IP, routing protocols, and what's on the wire
Topic 06 covers only wireless, so there is no overlap
The two topics are independent and share no concepts
items.explanation: This module covers the Linux mechanisms — the interfaces, sockets, routing table, and firewall you manipulate with ip/ss/nft. Topic 06 covers the protocols themselves (TCP/IP, routing protocols, packet formats) that those mechanisms carry.

lessons.title: What eBPF Is
lessons.content:
The one-line definition to memorize: **eBPF is a safe, verified, JIT-compiled virtual machine inside the Linux kernel that runs small sandboxed programs attached to kernel hooks — without writing a kernel module or rebooting.**

Before eBPF, changing kernel behavior or instrumenting it deeply meant patching the kernel, loading a `.ko` module (Module 9), or rebooting — all risky on a production box. eBPF flips that: you write a tiny program, the kernel **verifies** it is safe, JIT-compiles it to native instructions, and attaches it to a **hook**. When that hook fires (a syscall entry, a function call, a packet arriving), your program runs in the kernel at near-native speed, then is removed cleanly when you detach it.

The hooks are the key idea. Your program can attach to:

- **kprobes / kretprobes** — entry/return of (almost) any kernel function
- **uprobes** — functions inside a userspace binary
- **tracepoints** — stable, named instrumentation points the kernel exposes
- **XDP / tc** — the network packet path, before or in the stack
- **LSM** — Linux Security Module hooks for policy decisions

Results flow back to userspace through **maps** (shared key/value tables) or ring buffers, so a userspace tool can read counts, histograms, or events live.

```bash
# Is bpftrace present and which probe types exist?
bpftrace --version
bpftrace -l 'tracepoint:syscalls:*' | head
```

Think of it as a controlled, sandboxed plugin system for the running kernel: dynamic, removable, and safe enough to run on production.

items.front: Give the one-line definition of eBPF.
items.back: A safe, verified, JIT-compiled virtual machine inside the Linux kernel that runs small sandboxed programs attached to kernel hooks — without writing a kernel module or rebooting.
items.hint: Safe + verified + JIT VM + in the kernel + no module/reboot.

items.front: Name five kinds of hooks an eBPF program can attach to.
items.back: kprobes/kretprobes (kernel function entry/return), uprobes (userspace function entry/return), tracepoints (stable named kernel instrumentation points), XDP/tc (network packet path), and LSM (security policy hooks).
items.hint: Two are kernel functions, one is userspace functions, two are networking, one is security.

items.front: How does data get from an eBPF program in the kernel back to a userspace tool?
items.back: Through eBPF maps (shared key/value data structures, e.g. hashes and histograms) and ring/perf buffers for streaming events. Userspace reads them to display counts, histograms, or per-event data.
items.hint: Shared tables and a streaming buffer.

items.front: What did instrumenting the kernel deeply require before eBPF, and what does eBPF replace it with?
items.back: Previously: patching the kernel source, loading a custom .ko module, or rebooting — all risky in production. eBPF replaces this with a small program the kernel verifies and JIT-compiles, attaches to a hook, and removes cleanly on detach.
items.hint: Module 9's .ko is the thing eBPF lets you avoid.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures what eBPF is?
choices: A userspace daemon that polls /proc and aggregates statistics
A safe, verified VM in the kernel running sandboxed programs on hooks, with no module or reboot
A patched kernel build with extra tracing compiled in
A replacement C library that wraps every syscall
items.explanation: eBPF runs verified, sandboxed programs inside the kernel attached to hooks, JIT-compiled to native code — no kernel module and no reboot. It is not a userspace poller, a custom kernel build, or a libc wrapper.

items.prompt: You want to fire an eBPF program whenever any process enters a syscall. Which hook type is most appropriate?
items.explanation: Syscall entry/exit is exposed by stable tracepoints (e.g. raw_syscalls:sys_enter). uprobes trace userspace functions, XDP is the packet path, and LSM is for security policy decisions.

items.prompt: Which hook attaches an eBPF program to a function inside a userspace binary?
items.explanation: uprobes (and uretprobes) attach to userspace functions. kprobes target kernel functions, tracepoints are kernel-defined points, and tc is on the network traffic-control path.

items.prompt: Why is eBPF considered production-safe in a way that loading a custom kernel module is not?
choices: eBPF programs run in userspace, so a crash cannot affect the kernel
The kernel verifier statically checks the program is bounded and safe before it runs, and it can be detached cleanly without a reboot
eBPF programs are written in Python, which cannot crash
eBPF disables the program if the CPU exceeds 50% usage
items.explanation: The verifier proves safety properties (bounded loops, valid memory access, termination) before the program loads, and programs detach cleanly. A buggy .ko module runs with full kernel privilege and can panic the box. eBPF programs do run in the kernel, just sandboxed.

lessons.title: The Verifier, the Ecosystem, and bpftrace
lessons.content:
What makes "run arbitrary code in the kernel" actually safe is the **verifier**. When you load an eBPF program, the verifier statically analyzes every possible execution path and rejects the program unless it can prove it is bounded and safe: no unbounded loops (it must provably terminate), no out-of-bounds or arbitrary memory access, no reading uninitialized data, no dereferencing untrusted pointers. Only programs that pass are JIT-compiled and attached. This is why a buggy eBPF program gets *rejected at load time* instead of panicking your kernel — the trade-off for that safety is that you cannot do everything you could in a real module.

You rarely write raw eBPF bytecode. The **ecosystem** sits on top:

- **bpftrace** — a high-level, awk-like language for one-liners and short scripts. The most accessible entry point; think *the awk of the kernel*.
- **bcc** (BPF Compiler Collection) — a Python/C++ framework and a large set of ready-made tools (`execsnoop`, `opensnoop`, `biolatency`, ...).
- **libbpf + CO-RE** — *Compile Once, Run Everywhere*: compile a program against BTF type info once and run the same binary across kernel versions without recompiling per host.

bpftrace's model mirrors awk: `probe { action }`, with maps written as `@name`.

```bash
# Count syscalls per process name
bpftrace -e 'tracepoint:raw_syscalls:sys_enter { @[comm] = count(); }'

# Every file opened, system-wide
bpftrace -e 'tracepoint:syscalls:sys_enter_openat { printf("%s %s\n", comm, str(args->filename)); }'
```

bpftrace answers ad-hoc questions interactively; bcc ships polished, reusable tools.

items.front: What does the eBPF verifier do, and what happens to a program that fails it?
items.back: It statically analyzes all execution paths to prove the program is safe and bounded — provable termination (no unbounded loops), only valid/in-bounds memory access, no uninitialized reads. A program that fails is rejected at load time and never runs, rather than risking a kernel panic.
items.hint: Static proof of safety; failure means rejection, not a crash.

items.front: Why is bpftrace called "the awk of the kernel"?
items.back: It uses an awk-like 'probe { action }' model with associative arrays written as @maps, letting you write powerful one-liners and short scripts to instrument the kernel. It is the most accessible entry point into eBPF.

items.front: What does CO-RE (Compile Once, Run Everywhere) solve?
items.back: It lets a single compiled eBPF program (built with libbpf against BTF type information) run across different kernel versions without recompiling on each host, by relocating struct field offsets at load time. It removes the old need to ship a compiler and kernel headers everywhere.
items.hint: One compiled binary, many kernels, thanks to BTF.

items.front: Contrast bpftrace and bcc in one sentence each.
items.back: bpftrace is a high-level awk-like language for ad-hoc one-liners and short scripts you write interactively. bcc is a Python/C++ framework plus a large collection of polished, ready-made tools (execsnoop, opensnoop, biolatency, etc.).
items.hint: One is a language for quick questions; the other is a toolbox/framework.

items.prompt: An eBPF program contains a loop whose bound the verifier cannot prove. What happens?
choices: It loads but is throttled to one iteration per second
It is rejected at load time and never attaches
It loads and the kernel kills it if it runs too long
It silently runs in userspace instead
