items.explanation: The verifier requires provable termination. If it cannot bound the loop, it rejects the program at load time — it never attaches or runs. This rejection is exactly what keeps eBPF production-safe.

items.prompt: Which bpftrace one-liner counts syscalls grouped by process name?
items.explanation: Attaching to the raw_syscalls:sys_enter tracepoint and keying the map by comm (the process name) gives per-process syscall counts. The others count a single function, trace ls's main, or build a histogram of openat return values.

items.prompt: You need a polished, ready-to-run tool to watch every process exec across the system. Best first reach?
choices: Write raw eBPF bytecode by hand
Recompile the kernel with audit enabled
items.explanation: bcc ships execsnoop, a maintained tool that prints every new exec() system-wide. Hand-writing bytecode is unnecessary, recompiling the kernel is overkill, and strace on PID 1 will not see arbitrary new processes.

items.prompt: In bpftrace, what does the @ prefix (e.g. @[comm]) denote?
choices: A map (associative array) that aggregates data, like an awk array
items.explanation: @name denotes a bpftrace map — an associative array used to aggregate values (counts, histograms) keyed by something like comm. It is the direct analog of an awk associative array.

lessons.title: Why It Matters: Tracing, Networking, Security, and Scope
lessons.content:
eBPF reshaped three domains, all building on earlier modules.

**Performance tracing** improves on the `/proc`-based tools (Module 8: `vmstat`, `iostat`, `free`) and on `strace` (Module 7). Those tools either sample coarse counters or, like `strace`, intercept every syscall via ptrace — which can slow a target by an order of magnitude, making it unsafe under production load. eBPF instead aggregates *in the kernel* and ships only summaries up, so you get a per-syscall latency histogram or per-process counts with negligible overhead.

```bash
# In-kernel latency histogram of read() (no per-event userspace cost)
bpftrace -e 'tracepoint:syscalls:sys_enter_read { @t[tid] = nsecs; }
  tracepoint:syscalls:sys_exit_read /@t[tid]/ { @us = hist((nsecs - @t[tid]) / 1000); delete(@t[tid]); }'
```

**Networking**: at the **XDP** hook a program runs the instant a packet hits the driver — before the kernel allocates an sk_buff — enabling line-rate filtering, load-balancing, and DDoS drop. **Cilium** uses this for Kubernetes networking/policy; it is the container-networking story (Modules 14/16) done in eBPF instead of iptables.

**Security observability**: **Falco** and **Tetragon** watch syscalls and kernel events via eBPF to flag or block suspicious behavior (a shell spawned in a container, an unexpected file open) with full context and low overhead.

**Honest scope**: this is an *intro*. The goal is to explain eBPF as "safe sandboxed programs in the kernel without a module or reboot," know the ecosystem, and run a few canned bpftrace/bcc tools to answer a real question. Writing production eBPF programs end-to-end is a genuine specialty — a next step, not this lesson.

items.front: Why can eBPF tracing run safely under production load when strace often cannot?
items.back: strace uses ptrace to intercept every syscall, context-switching to userspace per event and slowing the target dramatically. eBPF aggregates in the kernel (e.g. building a histogram in a map) and ships only summaries to userspace, so overhead is negligible.
items.hint: Per-event ptrace cost vs in-kernel aggregation.

items.front: What makes the XDP hook special for networking, and what real project uses it?
items.back: XDP runs an eBPF program the moment a packet arrives at the driver — before an sk_buff is allocated and before the normal network stack — enabling line-rate filtering, load balancing, and DDoS drop. Cilium uses eBPF/XDP for Kubernetes networking and policy in place of iptables.
items.hint: Earliest possible point in the packet path; Cilium.

items.front: Name two eBPF-based security observability tools and what they do.
items.back: Falco and Tetragon watch syscalls and kernel events via eBPF to detect (and, for Tetragon, enforce against) suspicious runtime behavior — e.g. a shell spawned inside a container or an unexpected file access — with rich context and low overhead.
items.hint: Runtime security monitoring on kernel events.

items.front: What is the honest scope of an eBPF intro — what should you be able to do?
items.back: Explain eBPF as 'safe sandboxed programs in the kernel without a module or reboot,' know the ecosystem (bpftrace, bcc, libbpf/CO-RE) and its three big domains (tracing, networking, security), and run a few canned bpftrace/bcc one-liners to answer a real question. Writing production eBPF end-to-end is a specialty beyond the intro.
items.hint: Understand + run canned tools; deep authoring is a later specialty.

items.prompt: Why does an eBPF latency histogram of read() impose far less overhead than computing the same with strace?
choices: eBPF skips most read() calls to save time
eBPF aggregates the timing into an in-kernel map and only the summary crosses to userspace, avoiding a per-event userspace round trip
strace runs in the kernel and eBPF runs in userspace
eBPF disables timestamps, so it does less work
items.explanation: eBPF builds the histogram inside the kernel and exports only the aggregate. strace (ptrace) stops the target and switches to userspace on every syscall, which is the expensive part. eBPF does not skip events or drop timing accuracy.

items.prompt: Cilium implements Kubernetes networking and policy primarily using which mechanism?
choices: eBPF programs on hooks like XDP/tc
A userspace proxy intercepting all traffic
items.explanation: Cilium uses eBPF on the packet path (XDP/tc) for routing, load balancing, and policy, replacing the older per-pod iptables approach. It is not a userspace proxy or a loadable .ko module.

items.prompt: Which describes the appropriate takeaway from this INTRO module?
choices: You should be able to author and ship a production CO-RE networking program
You should be able to explain eBPF in one line, know its ecosystem and uses, and run canned bpftrace/bcc tools
You should replace all /proc tools with hand-written eBPF
You should compile a custom kernel to enable eBPF
items.explanation: The honest intro scope is conceptual fluency plus running ready-made tools to answer a real question. Authoring production programs is a specialty; you do not replace all of /proc, and modern kernels already ship eBPF support.

items.prompt: How does eBPF relate to the kernel module from Module 9?
choices: eBPF is just a kind of kernel module with a different file extension
eBPF is the thing that lets you avoid writing a kernel module for observability/networking/security tasks
eBPF requires a kernel module to be loaded first
eBPF and kernel modules are unrelated
items.explanation: A key framing: eBPF lets you instrument and extend the kernel for tracing, networking, and security WITHOUT writing or loading a .ko module and without rebooting. It is the safe, dynamic alternative to the module approach for these tasks.

subtopics.title: Embedded Linux: Yocto/Buildroot, Device Tree & SBCs

lessons.title: What Makes Embedded Linux Different
lessons.content:
Desktop and server Linux assume abundant RAM/flash, a self-describing bus (PCIe/USB enumeration, ACPI tables on x86), and a package manager you run *on the box*. Embedded Linux throws most of that away. You are running on an ARM (or RISC-V) **SoC** with maybe 128 MB-1 GB of RAM and a few GB of flash, bringing up **custom hardware** whose peripherals cannot announce themselves to the kernel.

Four things define the embedded workflow:

- **Constrained resources.** You strip the kernel and userspace down to what the product needs. BusyBox replaces dozens of GNU tools with one multicall binary; you may have no shell at all in production.
- **Cross-compilation (Module 13).** You build on a fast x86 host *for* the target ARM. The target never compiles its own code, so there is usually **no package manager on the device** — the whole image is built and flashed as a unit.
- **Custom hardware.** A vendor or you designed the board. Non-discoverable peripherals (I2C sensors, SPI flash, GPIO LEDs) must be described to the kernel via the **device tree**, not probed.
- **Robust, often read-only rootfs.** A device in the field cannot run `apt upgrade` after a power cut corrupts the filesystem. Embedded images favor a **read-only root** (squashfs) with a small writable overlay, atomic A/B update schemes, and long support lifecycles measured in years.

For you this is familiar ground inverted: instead of soldering and probing radar boards, you describe that hardware to software and bring it up. Your EE instincts about clocks, buses, and signal integrity transfer directly.

items.front: Name the four properties that distinguish embedded Linux from desktop/server Linux.
items.back: Constrained resources (small RAM/flash), cross-compilation on a host for the target, custom non-discoverable hardware, and a robust/read-only rootfs with long support lifecycles. Usually no package manager runs on the device.
items.hint: Think small, built elsewhere, custom board, can't apt-upgrade in the field.

items.front: Why is there usually no package manager on an embedded target?
items.back: The entire image is cross-compiled on a host and flashed as a unit. The device has no toolchain, limited storage, and needs reproducible/atomic images, so software is added at build time (via Buildroot/Yocto), not installed on-device.
items.hint: It is built elsewhere and flashed whole.

items.front: What is BusyBox and why is it ubiquitous in embedded Linux?
items.back: BusyBox is a single multicall binary that implements stripped-down versions of many common Unix utilities (sh, ls, cp, init, etc.) selected by the name it is invoked as. It saves enormous space versus shipping full GNU coreutils on a constrained device.
items.hint: One binary, many symlinked tool names.

items.front: Why do embedded products favor a read-only root filesystem?
items.back: A read-only rootfs (e.g., squashfs) survives sudden power loss without corruption, can be integrity-checked, and prevents field drift. Writable state is confined to a small overlay or separate data partition, often with atomic A/B image updates.
items.hint: Power can be yanked at any instant in the field.

items.prompt: On an x86 server, the kernel learns about onboard devices largely from ACPI tables provided by firmware. What plays the analogous role on a typical ARM embedded board?
choices: The device tree, a separate data structure describing non-discoverable hardware
The PCIe configuration space, scanned at boot
USB enumeration performed by the boot ROM
items.explanation: Most ARM SoC peripherals are not self-describing and there is no ACPI. The device tree is a separate binary blob (DTB) passed to the kernel that enumerates the hardware so drivers can bind.

items.prompt: Which statement about cross-compilation in embedded Linux is correct?
choices: You compile on the ARM target because only it has the right headers
You compile on a fast x86 host using a toolchain that emits ARM binaries
Cross-compilation is only needed for the kernel, not userspace
It is identical to native compilation but with a -static flag
items.explanation: Embedded development cross-compiles on a powerful host (x86) using a toolchain (e.g., aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc) that targets the device architecture. The target is too slow/small to build its own image.

items.prompt: A field device loses power mid-write and afterward boots fine with no filesystem corruption. Which design choice most directly enabled this?
choices: A read-only squashfs root with a separate writable overlay/data area
Using systemd instead of BusyBox init
items.explanation: A read-only root cannot be corrupted by an interrupted write. Mutable state is isolated to an overlay or data partition, often combined with journaling or atomic updates, so the core system is always consistent.

items.front: How does the 'long support lifecycle' of embedded products affect kernel/toolchain choices?
items.back: Products must be maintained for years (sometimes a decade), so teams pin a long-term-support (LTS) kernel, a fixed toolchain, and reproducible build configs, rather than tracking the latest rolling release the way a desktop might.
items.hint: Defense and industrial gear can't chase the newest release every six months.

lessons.title: The ARM Boot Chain & U-Boot
lessons.content:
On x86 you learned: UEFI firmware -> GRUB (from the ESP) -> kernel + initramfs -> systemd. The ARM embedded chain looks different at every stage, and there is **no BIOS/UEFI**.

1. **SoC boot ROM.** Masked silicon inside the chip runs first. It reads a fixed boot-source order (eMMC/SD, SPI-NOR, USB, UART) and loads a tiny first-stage loader into on-chip SRAM, because DRAM is not configured yet.
2. **SPL** (Secondary Program Loader, sometimes `MLO`). A size-limited first stage whose job is to **initialize DRAM and clocks**, then load the full bootloader into RAM.
3. **U-Boot.** The workhorse. It has an interactive prompt, a persistent **environment** (key=value pairs in flash), and a script variable **`bootcmd`** that runs automatically after a countdown. U-Boot loads the **kernel image and the DTB** (and optionally an initramfs) from MMC, flash, or the network, then jumps into the kernel passing it the DTB pointer.
4. **Kernel** parses the DTB, brings up drivers, mounts the root filesystem (from the `bootargs`/kernel cmdline), and execs **init** (PID 1) — BusyBox init or systemd — which ends at a serial-console login.

Interrupting U-Boot (hit a key during the countdown) is a core skill:

```bash
# at the U-Boot prompt
printenv bootcmd            # see the auto-boot script
setenv bootargs 'console=ttyS0,115200 root=/dev/mmcblk0p2 rw'
fatload mmc 0:1 0x80008000 Image          # load kernel into RAM
fatload mmc 0:1 0x84000000 board.dtb      # load device tree
booti 0x80008000 - 0x84000000             # boot kernel, no initrd, with DTB
saveenv                                   # persist changes to flash
```

For development, U-Boot can pull the kernel over **TFTP** and mount root over **NFS**, so you reflash nothing while iterating.

items.front: List the full ARM embedded boot chain in order.
items.back: SoC boot ROM -> SPL (MLO) -> U-Boot -> kernel + DTB -> init (PID 1). The boot ROM is on-chip silicon; SPL sets up DRAM/clocks; U-Boot loads kernel+DTB; the kernel mounts root and execs init.
items.hint: Five stages, starting inside the chip and ending at a login prompt.

items.front: What is the SPL's primary job, and why can't U-Boot do it directly?
items.back: SPL initializes DRAM controllers and clocks and then loads the full U-Boot into RAM. The boot ROM can only load a tiny image into on-chip SRAM before DRAM exists, so a small first stage must configure memory before the larger U-Boot can run.
items.hint: DRAM isn't usable yet when the boot ROM runs.

items.front: What are U-Boot's environment and bootcmd?
items.back: The environment is a persistent set of key=value variables stored in flash (edited with setenv/saveenv). bootcmd is the variable holding the script U-Boot runs automatically after the boot-delay countdown, typically loading and booting the kernel + DTB.

items.front: In development, how can U-Boot avoid reflashing the device on every kernel change?
items.back: U-Boot can fetch the kernel and DTB over TFTP into RAM, and the kernel can mount its root filesystem over NFS (root=/dev/nfs with nfsroot= in bootargs). You rebuild on the host and reboot; nothing is written to the device's flash.
items.hint: Network boot: TFTP for the kernel, NFS for root.

items.prompt: Which is the single biggest structural difference between the ARM embedded boot chain and the x86/UEFI chain?
choices: ARM uses GRUB while x86 uses U-Boot
There is no BIOS/UEFI; an on-chip boot ROM plus SPL bootstraps U-Boot, and a device tree replaces ACPI hardware self-description
x86 has no kernel command line
items.explanation: ARM SoCs boot from masked on-chip ROM, not UEFI firmware, and chain through SPL to U-Boot. They lack ACPI, so the device tree describes the hardware. The first three options are factually wrong.

items.prompt: At the U-Boot prompt you run printenv and want the kernel to use a serial console and an SD root partition. Which variable do you set?
choices: bootcmd, because it holds the kernel command line
bootargs, which becomes the kernel command line passed at boot
loadaddr, which sets the console device
fdtaddr, which selects the root filesystem
items.explanation: bootargs is passed to the kernel as its command line (console=, root=, etc.). bootcmd is the U-Boot script that boots; loadaddr/fdtaddr are RAM load addresses for the kernel and DTB.

items.prompt: After U-Boot loads the kernel and DTB and jumps into the kernel, what is the last handoff before userspace begins?
choices: The kernel calls saveenv to persist the environment
The kernel parses the DTB, mounts the root filesystem, and execs init as PID 1
U-Boot mounts the root filesystem and starts systemd
The boot ROM re-runs to verify the image
items.explanation: The kernel uses the DTB to bring up drivers, mounts root per the cmdline, then execs /sbin/init (or init= override) as PID 1 — the kernel-to-userspace handoff. U-Boot is long gone by then.

items.front: The ARM embedded boot chain stage by stage: on-chip boot ROM -> SPL (DRAM/clock init) -> U-Boot (env, bootcmd, load kernel+DTB) -> kernel + DTB -> init, contrasted with the x86/UEFI path.
items.back: Review the diagram above.

lessons.title: The Device Tree: Describing Hardware to the Kernel

items.front: Why does the Linux device tree exist?
items.back: To describe non-discoverable hardware (I2C/SPI/GPIO peripherals, memory map, clocks) to the kernel without hardcoding board details into kernel C code. It replaced the unmaintainable per-board 'board file' approach on ARM.
items.hint: Some buses can't enumerate their devices.

items.front: What is the role of the compatible property in a device-tree node?
items.back: compatible is a string (or list) the kernel matches against each driver's of_device_id table. On a match, the driver's probe() is called and it binds to that node. It is the link between hardware description and driver code.
items.hint: It's how a DT node finds its driver.

items.front: What is the difference between .dts, .dtsi, and .dtb?
items.back: .dts is device-tree source for a specific board; .dtsi is a shared include (e.g., the SoC definition) pulled into board files; .dtb is the compiled binary blob handed to the kernel. dtc compiles .dts (+ .dtsi includes) into .dtb.

items.front: What is a device-tree overlay and when is it useful?
items.back: An overlay is a fragment that patches the base device tree (adding/enabling nodes) without recompiling the full DTB. Raspberry Pi uses them to enable I2C/SPI/GPIO peripherals at boot. Great for experimentation and optional hardware (HATs/capes).
items.hint: A patch on top of the base tree, often loaded by the bootloader.

items.front: What are the three cardinal mistakes when working with device trees?
items.back: 1) A wrong/typo compatible string so the driver never binds. 2) Editing the binary .dtb instead of the .dts source. 3) Forgetting to recompile (dtc) and redeploy the DTB to the device after changing the source.
items.hint: Wrong string, edit the blob, forget to redeploy.

items.prompt: You added an I2C sensor node to the device tree, but after boot dmesg shows the driver never ran and /sys has no entry. The driver is built in. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The kernel was compiled with the wrong optimization level
The compatible string in the DT node does not match the driver's of_device_id table
items.explanation: Driver binding hinges on the compatible string matching the driver's of_device_id entry. A mismatch (typo, wrong vendor prefix) means no probe() call and a silently absent device — the classic DT bug.

items.prompt: Which command compiles device-tree source into the binary blob the kernel consumes?
items.explanation: dtc (device-tree compiler) converts between .dts and .dtb. Here -I dts -O dtb specifies input/output formats. gcc, menuconfig, and objcopy do not compile device trees.

items.prompt: What is the cleanest way to enable an optional SPI peripheral on a Raspberry Pi without rebuilding the entire base device tree?
choices: Hand-edit the binary .dtb with a hex editor
Load a device-tree overlay that patches the base tree at boot
Recompile the kernel with the device baked in
items.explanation: Overlays are designed exactly for this: a small fragment enabling/adding nodes is applied over the base DTB at boot, so optional hardware can be toggled without recompiling the full tree.

items.front: Device-tree binding: a node's compatible string ('bosch,bmp280') is matched against a driver's of_device_id table; on a match the driver's probe() runs and binds. A typo on either side means the driver never probes.
items.back: Review the diagram above.

lessons.title: Buildroot vs Yocto, BSPs, Filesystems & Booting an SBC
lessons.content:
A **Board Support Package (BSP)** is the vendor-supplied bundle that makes a specific board boot: the bootloader (SPL+U-Boot), a kernel with the right drivers, and the board's device tree. You build a root filesystem on top of the BSP using one of two dominant systems.

**Buildroot** is the simpler one: a big **Kconfig**/Makefile system (`make menuconfig`) that cross-compiles a toolchain, kernel, and a minimal rootfs into a single flashable image. It is fast to learn, fast to build, and ideal for small, fixed images — the recommended starting point.

```bash
make raspberrypi3_defconfig   # start from a board defconfig
make menuconfig               # tweak packages
make -j$(nproc)               # produces output/images/sdcard.img
```

**Yocto / OpenEmbedded** is the industry standard for products. It is heavier and built around **layers** (`bblayers.conf`), **recipes** (`.bb` files describing how to fetch/build each component), the **BitBake** task engine, machine configs, and `local.conf`. It generates SDKs, handles licensing/SBOM, and scales to large product lines — at the cost of a steep learning curve and long first builds.

**Rule of thumb:** Buildroot for simplicity and small/fixed images; Yocto when you need recipes, layering, package feeds, SBOM, and production-grade maintainability across many boards.

Embedded **filesystems** match the storage: **squashfs** (compressed, read-only root), **UBIFS/JFFS2** (for raw NAND/NOR flash with wear-leveling), and an **overlay** (overlayfs/tmpfs) for a writable layer over read-only base. The kernel itself is configured via `make menuconfig` from a **defconfig**, then you build the kernel image, **modules**, and **DTB**.

Finally, you boot a real **SBC**. Flash the image to an SD card and **boot it**:

```bash
lsblk                                   # CONFIRM the device first!
sudo dd if=output/images/sdcard.img of=/dev/sdX bs=4M conv=fsync status=progress
```

A wrong `of=` here can wipe your laptop disk — always check `lsblk` first. The **Raspberry Pi** is the most accessible board; the **BeagleBone Black** exposes **PRUs** (programmable real-time units), which are compelling for deterministic, radar-like real-time I/O alongside Linux.

items.front: What is a Board Support Package (BSP)?
items.back: The vendor-supplied bundle that boots a specific board: the bootloader (SPL + U-Boot), a kernel with the board's drivers, and its device tree. Build systems layer a root filesystem on top of the BSP.
items.hint: Bootloader + kernel + drivers + device tree for one board.

items.front: Contrast Buildroot and Yocto/OpenEmbedded.
items.back: Buildroot: Kconfig/Makefile based, simple, fast, great for small/fixed images and learning. Yocto/OE: layers + recipes (.bb) + BitBake, machine configs, SDK and SBOM generation; heavier and slower but the production standard scaling across many boards. Pick Buildroot for simplicity, Yocto for maintainable products.
items.hint: Simple menuconfig image vs layered recipe-based product system.

items.front: Name Yocto's core building blocks.
items.back: Layers (bblayers.conf) group recipes; recipes (.bb files) describe how to fetch and build each component; BitBake is the task scheduler/build engine; machine configs and local.conf select the target and settings. It can also generate an SDK and license/SBOM data.

items.front: Match the embedded filesystem to its use: squashfs, UBIFS/JFFS2, overlayfs.
items.back: squashfs: compressed read-only root. UBIFS/JFFS2: filesystems for raw NAND/NOR flash with wear-leveling. overlayfs (over tmpfs or a data partition): a writable layer stacked on a read-only base so the system can be modified at runtime without making root writable.
items.hint: Read-only root, raw-flash FS, writable layer.

items.front: What three kernel build outputs do you typically produce for an SBC, and how do you start the config?
items.back: The kernel image (zImage/Image), the loadable modules, and the DTB(s). You start from a board defconfig (make <board>_defconfig) and adjust with make menuconfig, then build all three for the target architecture.
items.hint: Image, modules, DTB — start from a defconfig.

items.prompt: A small team needs a minimal, fixed Linux image for one Raspberry Pi product and wants to be productive quickly. Which build system fits best, and why?
choices: Yocto, because BitBake recipes are required for any SD image
Buildroot, because its Kconfig/Makefile flow is simpler and fast for small fixed images
Yocto, because Buildroot cannot cross-compile a kernel
Neither; you must hand-assemble the rootfs with debootstrap
items.explanation: Buildroot's menuconfig-driven flow produces a flashable image quickly and is ideal for small, fixed images. Yocto's power (layers, recipes, SBOM) is overkill here and slower to learn. Buildroot fully cross-compiles the kernel.

items.prompt: Why is squashfs combined with an overlay a popular rootfs scheme for shipping devices?
choices: squashfs is faster to write than ext4
The compressed read-only base resists corruption and saves flash, while the overlay supplies needed writability
It removes the need for a device tree
It lets the device run a package manager safely
items.explanation: squashfs is compressed and read-only (corruption-resistant, space-saving); an overlay (over tmpfs or a data partition) provides the small amount of runtime writability needed without exposing the base to corruption.

items.prompt: Which command is correct and safe for writing a built SD-card image to a card, and what is the critical precaution?
choices: sudo dd if=sdcard.img of=/dev/sdX bs=4M conv=fsync — first confirm /dev/sdX with lsblk
sudo dd if=/dev/sdX of=sdcard.img bs=4M — the direction does not matter
cp sdcard.img /dev/sdX — dd is never needed
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdX then copy files — dd corrupts images
items.explanation: dd writes the raw image to the block device; if/of direction matters and a wrong of= can destroy your host disk, so you must verify the target with lsblk first. cp to a raw device won't lay down the partition table/bootloader correctly.

items.prompt: Why might an engineer with a radar/real-time background find the BeagleBone Black especially interesting versus a Raspberry Pi?
choices: It exposes PRUs (programmable real-time units) for deterministic real-time I/O alongside Linux
It has no device tree, simplifying bring-up
It cannot run Buildroot or Yocto
items.explanation: The BeagleBone's PRUs are independent real-time cores for deterministic, low-jitter I/O — attractive for signal/real-time work that Linux's scheduler can't guarantee, which resonates with radar/real-time hardware experience.

lessons.title: Infrastructure as Code: The Declarative Mindset & Ansible
lessons.content:
Configuring one box by hand is fine. Configuring fifty the same way, repeatably, after someone reboots or rebuilds them, is the modern job. The shift is from *remembered commands* to a **committed artifact** that describes the desired end state. That artifact is **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)**.

The key word is **declarative**: you say *what* the machine should look like ("package nginx installed, this config present, this service running"), not *how* to get there step by step. The tool figures out the diff and only changes what's wrong. That makes runs **idempotent** — run it once or ten times, the end state is identical and re-runs after the first are mostly no-ops.

**Ansible** is the most accessible and widely demanded tool here, and the one to learn first. It is **agentless**: nothing is installed on the targets — it connects over plain **SSH** and runs Python. You describe state in **playbooks** (YAML), targeting hosts listed in an **inventory**.

```yaml
# site.yml — run with: ansible-playbook -i inventory site.yml
- hosts: webservers
  become: true            # use sudo
  tasks:
    - name: Install nginx
      ansible.builtin.package:
        name: nginx
        state: present
    - name: Ensure nginx is enabled and running
      ansible.builtin.service:
        name: nginx
        state: started
        enabled: true
```

Think of each task as an assertion of state, not a script line. **Terraform** is its sibling, but for a different layer: it *provisions* cloud infrastructure (VMs, networks, DNS) rather than configuring the inside of an OS. Ansible configures; Terraform provisions.

items.front: What does "declarative" mean in the context of configuration management?
items.back: You describe the desired end state (what the machine should look like) rather than the step-by-step procedure to get there. The tool computes the difference and changes only what's out of state.

items.front: What does it mean for a playbook to be idempotent, and why does it matter?
items.back: Running it once or many times converges the host to the same end state; after the first run, subsequent runs are mostly no-ops. It means the artifact — not your memory of commands — is the reliable source of truth and is safe to re-run anytime.

items.front: Why is Ansible described as "agentless," and what does it use to reach targets?
items.back: Nothing needs to be installed on the managed nodes. Ansible connects over standard SSH from a control node and runs Python on the target, then collects results. This makes adoption easy — if you can SSH in, you can manage it.

items.front: In Ansible, what is a playbook vs an inventory?
items.back: A playbook is a YAML file describing the desired state as a list of tasks/plays. An inventory is the list of target hosts (and groups like 'webservers'), with optional per-host/per-group variables, that the playbook runs against.
items.hint: What to do vs where to do it.

items.front: Ansible vs Terraform: what layer does each operate at?
items.back: Terraform provisions infrastructure — it creates cloud resources (VMs, networks, DNS, load balancers). Ansible configures the inside of those machines (packages, files, services). Provision with Terraform, then configure with Ansible.
items.hint: Create the box vs set up the box.

items.prompt: You run an Ansible playbook that asserts nginx is installed and running. The package is already installed and the service is already up. What happens on this run?
choices: It errors because the package is already present
It reinstalls nginx and restarts the service to be safe
It reports the tasks as 'ok' (no change), because the desired state already holds
It uninstalls and reinstalls to guarantee a clean state
items.explanation: Idempotent, declarative tasks check current state against desired state and only act on a difference. Since the state already matches, the tasks report 'ok'/changed=0 — a no-op.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the core value of Infrastructure as Code over manual setup?
choices: It runs commands faster than typing them by hand
It expresses a host's setup as a committed, re-runnable artifact that can be reviewed, versioned, and reproduced
It eliminates the need for SSH access to machines
It removes the need to understand the underlying Linux tools
items.explanation: IaC turns 'set this machine up like this' into a versioned artifact you can review and re-run, rather than a list of remembered commands. Speed is incidental; you still need SSH and Linux knowledge underneath.

items.prompt: In the Ansible task `state: present` on a package, what is `present` describing?
choices: The order in which tasks execute
A shell command to run on the target
The desired end state of the resource (installed), which Ansible enforces
items.explanation: `state: present` declares the desired condition of the resource — the package should be installed. Ansible compares against current state and installs only if it isn't already there. It is a state assertion, not a command.

items.prompt: A teammate says "just SSH in and rerun the setup script we wrote." Why is a declarative Ansible playbook preferable for fleet management?
choices: An imperative script may not be safely re-runnable (it can double-apply or fail on partial state), while a declarative playbook converges to the same state every run
Ansible runs without any network access
Playbooks execute instantly regardless of fleet size
items.explanation: A hand-written imperative script often isn't idempotent — re-running can duplicate edits or fail when the box is partially configured. A declarative playbook checks and converges to the declared state safely on every run.

lessons.title: Remote Access at Scale, Secrets, and Scheduling
lessons.content:
Managing a fleet means reaching machines that aren't directly on your network and doing it without typing passwords or leaking credentials.

**SSH config & jump hosts.** Internal hosts usually sit behind a **bastion** (jump host) — the one box exposed to the internet. Rather than chaining `ssh` commands, encode it once in `~/.ssh/config` so `ssh web-01` transparently hops through the bastion:

```
Host bastion
    HostName bastion.example.com
    User deploy
Host web-* db-*
    User deploy
    ProxyJump bastion       # tunnel through the bastion automatically
```

**Key management.** Use SSH **keys**, not passwords — generate `ssh-ed25519`, push the public key to targets (`ssh-copy-id`), and protect the private key with a passphrase loaded into `ssh-agent`. Harden `sshd` to disable password auth entirely. Keys are also how Ansible authenticates non-interactively.

**Secrets: don't commit them.** Your playbooks live in git — so API tokens, DB passwords, and private keys must **never** be checked in as plaintext. Use a **vault**: Ansible Vault encrypts variable files at rest; teams often centralize on **HashiCorp Vault** or a cloud secrets manager that issues short-lived credentials. The rule: code in git, secrets in a vault, injected at run time.

**Scheduling: cron vs systemd timers.** Classic **cron** (`crontab -e`) still works for simple recurring jobs. Modern systems increasingly prefer **systemd timers** (Module 6): a `.timer` triggers a `.service`. Timers give you journald logging, dependency ordering, `Persistent=true` to catch missed runs after downtime, randomized delays, and resource control — things bare cron lacks.

items.front: What is a bastion / jump host, and what does ProxyJump do?
items.back: A bastion is the single hardened host exposed to the outside that you hop through to reach internal machines. `ProxyJump bastion` (in ~/.ssh/config or `ssh -J`) tells SSH to transparently tunnel the connection to the target through the bastion in one command.
items.hint: One door into the internal network.

items.front: Why use SSH keys over passwords for fleet access, and what protects the private key?
items.back: Keys enable non-interactive, scriptable auth (needed by tools like Ansible) and are far stronger than passwords. The private key stays on your machine, protected by a passphrase that ssh-agent caches in memory so you type it once per session.

items.front: What is the cardinal rule about secrets and version control?
items.back: Never commit plaintext secrets (passwords, tokens, private keys) to git. Code lives in the repo; secrets live in a vault (Ansible Vault, HashiCorp Vault, or a cloud secrets manager) and are decrypted/injected only at run time.
items.hint: Code in git, secrets in a vault.

items.front: Name two capabilities systemd timers offer that classic cron does not.
items.back: Among others: integrated journald logging of the job, dependency ordering against other units, `Persistent=true` to run missed jobs after downtime, randomized delays (RandomizedDelaySec) to avoid thundering herds, and cgroup resource limits.

items.front: What does a vault that issues short-lived (dynamic) credentials buy you over a static password?
items.back: Credentials are generated on demand and expire quickly, so a leaked secret has a small blast radius and limited lifetime. There's no long-lived password sitting in config to be stolen, rotated by hand, or accidentally committed.

items.prompt: You need to ssh to web-01, which is only reachable from inside the network behind bastion.example.com. What's the cleanest standing solution?
choices: Give web-01 a public IP and open SSH to the world
Add a `ProxyJump bastion` entry for web-* in ~/.ssh/config so `ssh web-01` tunnels through automatically
Copy your private key onto the bastion and ssh from there
Disable the firewall on web-01 during your session
items.explanation: ProxyJump in ssh_config makes `ssh web-01` hop through the bastion transparently with one command, keeping web-01 off the public internet and your private key on your own machine (never copied to the bastion).

items.prompt: A backup job must still run after a server was powered off during its scheduled time. Which scheduling choice handles this most cleanly?
choices: A cron entry, which automatically replays missed jobs
A systemd timer with `Persistent=true`, which runs the job at next boot if the trigger was missed
Neither can recover a missed run
An @reboot cron line, which runs the backup on every boot regardless
items.explanation: Classic cron simply skips runs that occur while the machine is off. A systemd timer with `Persistent=true` records the last run and fires the missed job once at next boot — exactly the recovery behavior wanted.

items.prompt: Your Ansible repo needs a database password to configure an app. What is the correct way to handle it?
choices: Hard-code it in the playbook so the run is self-contained
Store it in a vars file committed to git but in a private repo
Keep it in an encrypted vault (e.g. Ansible Vault) and inject it at run time, never committing plaintext
Pass it on the command line in shell history every time
items.explanation: Even a private repo can leak; plaintext secrets in git are the classic mistake. Encrypt with a vault and inject at run time so the repo holds only ciphertext, and access is controlled separately from the code.

items.prompt: Why does Ansible's agentless model depend heavily on good SSH key management?
choices: Because Ansible encrypts playbooks with SSH keys
Because Ansible authenticates to every target over SSH and runs non-interactively, so it needs key-based (passwordless) auth that ssh-agent or a service key can provide
Because SSH keys replace the need for an inventory file
items.explanation: Ansible logs into each node over SSH and must do so without a human typing a password. Key-based auth (with an agent or a dedicated automation key) provides the non-interactive, auditable access this requires.

lessons.title: Observability, Immutable Infrastructure & the Production Horizon
lessons.content:
Provisioning a fleet is only half the job — in production you must *see* it and *ship to* it safely.

**The observability stack.** Logs start local in **journald** (Module 6), then get shipped off-box to an **aggregator** (Loki, Elasticsearch, or a hosted service) so you can query across all hosts at once — you don't SSH to ten boxes to grep. Metrics follow a different path: **Prometheus** *scrapes* numeric time-series (CPU, request rate, queue depth) from exporters running on each host, stores them, and **Grafana** dashboards and alerts on them. For the deepest layer — "what syscalls/files is this process actually touching?" — **eBPF** tooling (bpftrace/bcc, Module 17) instruments the live kernel with near-zero overhead. Roughly: journald/Loki = logs, Prometheus/Grafana = metrics, eBPF = deep kernel-level tracing.

**Immutable infrastructure.** Instead of patching long-lived "pet" servers in place (which drift into unique, un-reproducible states), you bake a known-good image and **replace** the whole instance to change it — servers become disposable "cattle." Containers (Module 14) are the natural unit; a registry holds versioned images.

**Kubernetes** is the scale-out horizon: a declarative orchestrator that schedules containers across many hosts, restarts failures, and rolls out new versions — IaC's philosophy applied to a whole cluster.

**CI/CD touches Linux** at every step: a pipeline checks out code, **builds and tests inside containers** for reproducibility, produces an image, and deploys it (often via the same Ansible/Terraform/k8s tooling).

```bash
# Each pipeline stage is just Linux in a container
docker build -t app:$GIT_SHA .
docker run --rm app:$GIT_SHA pytest   # test in a clean, reproducible env
```

**Honest scope:** this module is orientation. The goal is that the *vocabulary and landscape* are familiar in interviews and on the job — not mastery of each tool.

items.front: Trace the path of a log line in a fleet observability stack.
items.back: It's written locally to journald on the host, then shipped/forwarded to a central aggregator (Loki, Elasticsearch, or a hosted log service) where you can search across all hosts at once instead of SSHing into each box to grep.
items.hint: journald, then ship to a central store.

items.front: How do Prometheus and Grafana divide responsibility for metrics?
items.back: Prometheus scrapes and stores numeric time-series metrics from exporters on each host and evaluates alerting rules. Grafana queries Prometheus to render dashboards and visualize/alert on those metrics. Prometheus = collect/store, Grafana = visualize.

items.front: Where does eBPF fit relative to logs and metrics in observability?
items.back: It's the deepest layer: eBPF (via bpftrace/bcc) instruments the running kernel to answer 'what syscalls, files, or network events is this process actually doing right now?' with near-zero overhead — detail that pre-emitted logs and metrics can't provide.

items.front: What is immutable infrastructure (and the pets-vs-cattle idea)?
items.back: Rather than patching long-lived 'pet' servers in place (which drift into unique, un-reproducible states), you bake a versioned known-good image and replace the whole instance to change it. Servers become disposable, interchangeable 'cattle.'

items.front: What role does Kubernetes play in the infrastructure landscape?
items.back: It's a declarative container orchestrator: you declare desired state (N replicas of this image) and it schedules containers across a cluster of hosts, restarts failures, and rolls out updates. It's the scale-out endpoint of the same declarative/IaC philosophy.

items.front: Why do CI/CD pipelines build and test inside containers?
items.back: Containers give a clean, pinned, reproducible environment so a build/test behaves identically on every run and on every machine (your laptop, the CI runner, production). It eliminates 'works on my machine' drift and produces a versioned artifact to deploy.

items.prompt: You need to know exactly which files and syscalls a misbehaving production process is touching, live, without restarting it. Which tool layer fits?
choices: eBPF tracing (bpftrace/bcc) attached to the running kernel
Re-reading the Ansible playbook that configured it
items.explanation: Metrics and logs only show what was pre-instrumented or emitted. eBPF attaches to the live kernel to observe actual syscalls, file access, and network events with low overhead — exactly this 'what is it really doing right now' question.

items.prompt: A server has been hand-patched for two years and no one can reproduce its exact state. Which philosophy directly prevents this problem?
choices: Immutable infrastructure — bake a versioned image and replace instances instead of patching in place
Adding more Prometheus exporters to the host
Switching from cron to systemd timers
Giving the server a public IP for easier access
items.explanation: The drift comes from mutating a long-lived 'pet' in place. Immutable infrastructure builds a known-good image and replaces the whole instance on change, so the running state always matches a versioned, reproducible artifact.

items.prompt: Why is querying a central log aggregator preferable to SSHing into each host to grep journald?
choices: journald cannot be read with journalctl
It lets you search and correlate logs across the whole fleet at once, which doesn't scale by hand per-host
Aggregators replace the need for metrics entirely
It makes the logs immutable on each host
items.explanation: Per-host grepping doesn't scale and can't correlate events across machines. Shipping journald logs to a central aggregator lets you query and correlate the entire fleet from one place — the point of log aggregation.

items.prompt: How does Kubernetes extend the declarative/idempotent idea you saw with Ansible?
choices: It replaces SSH with a faster custom protocol
You declare desired cluster state (e.g. 3 replicas of an image) and it continuously reconciles reality to match — restarting and rescheduling as needed
It is an imperative tool where you run each container by hand
It only manages a single host, like a local container runtime
items.explanation: Kubernetes is declarative at cluster scale: you state the desired end state and a control loop continuously reconciles actual state to match (restarting failures, rescheduling, rolling out updates) — the same 'declare and converge' philosophy as a playbook, applied across many hosts.
